steps.helpDetail: Naloxone reverses opioid effects within 2-3 minutes. If no response after 3 minutes, give second dose in other nostril. Effects last 30-90 minutes — overdose may return.

techniques.name: Asthma Attack Response
techniques.subtitle: Help someone through an acute asthma episode

steps.instruction: Help the person sit upright — leaning slightly forward with hands on knees.
steps.helpDetail: This 'tripod position' opens the airways maximally. Never lay an asthma patient flat — it worsens breathing.

steps.instruction: Assist with their rescue inhaler if available: shake, exhale fully, inhale medicine, hold 10 seconds.
steps.helpDetail: 2 puffs with 1-minute gap between. Use a spacer if available. Without a spacer, hold inhaler 2 finger-widths from open mouth.

steps.instruction: Encourage slow, pursed-lip breathing: inhale through nose, exhale through pursed lips (like blowing out a candle).
steps.helpDetail: Pursed-lip breathing creates back-pressure that keeps airways open longer. Exhale should be twice as long as inhale.

steps.instruction: Remove triggers: move away from smoke, dust, cold air, animals, or strong odors.
steps.helpDetail: Continued exposure worsens the attack. Move indoors if triggered by cold air or pollen. Cover mouth with scarf if moving through cold.

steps.instruction: If no improvement after 2 inhaler doses, or lips turn blue, or cannot speak in full sentences — this is a medical emergency.
steps.helpDetail: Severe asthma attacks can be fatal. Keep the person calm and still. Do NOT give them water to drink while wheezing heavily.

techniques.name: Flail Chest Management
techniques.subtitle: Stabilize a segment of broken ribs

steps.instruction: Recognize flail chest: a section of ribs moves OPPOSITE to breathing (inward on inhale, outward on exhale).
steps.helpDetail: Flail chest occurs when 3+ adjacent ribs break in 2+ places. The 'paradoxical movement' compromises breathing. This is life-threatening.

steps.instruction: Have the patient lie on the injured side, or stabilize the flail segment with your hand.
steps.helpDetail: Lying on the injured side splints the flail segment against the ground. If they must sit up, tape a bulky pad firmly over the segment.
steps.illustrationDescription: Have the patient lie on the in

steps.instruction: Do NOT wrap the entire chest — this restricts breathing of the good lung.
steps.helpDetail: Only stabilize the flail segment. Circumferential wrapping causes respiratory failure. The uninjured side must expand freely.
steps.illustrationDescription: Do NOT wrap the entire chest —

steps.instruction: Monitor oxygen level: watch for blue lips, confusion, rapid heart rate, worsening distress.
steps.helpDetail: Flail chest often has underlying lung bruise (pulmonary contusion). Breathing will worsen over 24-48 hours even with stabilization.

steps.instruction: Evacuate urgently. Keep patient semi-upright (45°) for easiest breathing.
steps.helpDetail: This injury requires hospital care — pain management and possible ventilator support. Position for comfort and airway.

techniques.name: Pelvic Fracture Management
techniques.subtitle: Stabilize a suspected broken pelvis

steps.instruction: Suspect pelvic fracture if: pain in groin/hip area, inability to bear weight, leg appears shortened or rotated.
steps.helpDetail: Do NOT rock or squeeze the pelvis to test — this can worsen bleeding. Pelvic fractures can cause massive internal hemorrhage.

steps.instruction: Do NOT move the patient unless in immediate danger. Stabilize where they lie.
steps.helpDetail: Any movement can displace fracture fragments and increase bleeding. The pelvis is surrounded by major blood vessels.
steps.illustrationDescription: Do NOT move the patient unless

steps.instruction: Wrap a sheet, belt, or SAM splint tightly around the hips at the level of the greater trochanters.
steps.helpDetail: This is a pelvic binder — it compresses the pelvis to reduce bleeding space. Pull snug but not so tight breathing is affected.
steps.illustrationDescription: Wrap a sheet, belt, or SAM spl

steps.instruction: Place a rolled blanket or pack between the knees and tie the legs together.
steps.helpDetail: This prevents rotation and further displacement. Tie at knees and ankles. The bound legs act as an additional splint.
steps.illustrationDescription: Place a rolled blanket or pack

steps.instruction: Treat for shock: keep warm, elevate legs only if no spinal injury suspected, monitor consciousness.
steps.helpDetail: Pelvic fractures can bleed 3+ liters internally. Aggressive shock management is critical. Evacuate by stretcher — do not let patient walk.
steps.illustrationDescription: Treat for shock: keep warm, el

techniques.name: Wound Irrigation
techniques.subtitle: Pressure-flush debris from wounds

steps.instruction: Gather clean water (boiled and cooled, or purified). You need at least 500mL.
steps.helpDetail: Tap water is acceptable if clean. Saline is ideal: 1 teaspoon salt per liter of boiled water. Avoid hydrogen peroxide — it damages tissue.

steps.instruction: Create pressure: poke a small hole in a plastic bag or use a syringe (if available).
steps.helpDetail: 8-12 PSI is ideal — roughly equivalent to a syringe with an 18-gauge needle. A squeeze bottle with a small hole works in the field.

steps.instruction: Irrigate from the cleanest end of the wound toward the dirtiest. Flush thoroughly.
steps.helpDetail: Aim the stream into the wound, not across it. Flush for 30-60 seconds minimum. Use ALL your water — more irrigation = less infection.

steps.instruction: Remove visible debris with clean tweezers. Pick out gravel, splinters, plant matter.
steps.helpDetail: Only remove debris you can see and reach easily. Do NOT probe deep wounds — you risk pushing contamination deeper.

steps.instruction: Pat dry around the wound (not in it) and apply appropriate dressing.
steps.helpDetail: Leave the wound bed slightly moist — this promotes healing. Cover with non-stick gauze. Change dressing every 12-24 hours.

techniques.name: Wound Infection Recognition
techniques.subtitle: Identify and manage wound infections early

steps.instruction: Check wound daily. Normal: decreased redness, less pain, clear/straw-colored drainage.
steps.helpDetail: Some redness and warmth around a wound is normal inflammation. It should decrease over 3-5 days, not increase.

steps.instruction: Warning signs: INCREASING redness spreading outward, warmth, swelling, and pain after day 2-3.
steps.helpDetail: Infection causes expanding redness (cellulitis). Mark the border of redness with a pen — if it spreads past the mark, infection is progressing.

steps.instruction: Red streaks extending from the wound toward the heart = urgent. This is lymphangitis.
steps.helpDetail: Red streaks indicate the infection is spreading through the lymphatic system toward the bloodstream. This becomes sepsis without treatment.

steps.instruction: Thick, foul-smelling pus (yellow/green) indicates bacterial infection. Fever above 100.4°F confirms systemic infection.
steps.helpDetail: Clear or straw-colored fluid is normal wound drainage. Cloudy, colored, smelly drainage = infection.

steps.instruction: Treatment: clean wound aggressively, apply antiseptic, change dressings frequently. Antibiotics if available.
steps.helpDetail: In the field, honey is an effective topical antibiotic. Apply raw honey directly into the wound — it is antimicrobial and promotes healing.

techniques.name: Basic Splinting Principles
techniques.subtitle: Immobilize any fracture with available materials

steps.instruction: Splint in the position found. Do NOT try to straighten a deformed limb.
steps.helpDetail: Straightening risks cutting blood vessels or nerves with sharp bone ends. Exception: if there's no pulse below the fracture.

steps.instruction: Splint must immobilize the joint ABOVE and BELOW the fracture.
steps.helpDetail: A forearm fracture needs wrist AND elbow immobilized. A shin fracture needs ankle AND knee. This prevents movement at the break site.

steps.instruction: Pad the splint generously — especially at bony prominences.
steps.helpDetail: Unpadded splints cause pressure sores within hours. Use clothing, moss, leaves, rolled socks. Pad between fingers/toes if splinted together.

steps.instruction: Tie splint firmly but not so tight that circulation is cut off. Check pulse, sensation, and movement below.
steps.helpDetail: Test the 'CSM' — Circulation (pulse/color), Sensation (can they feel?), Movement (can they wiggle fingers/toes?). Recheck every 30 minutes.
steps.illustrationDescription: Tie splint firmly but not so t

steps.instruction: Materials: sticks, trekking poles, rolled magazines, SAM splints, cardboard, bark slabs, foam pads.
steps.helpDetail: A good splint is rigid enough to prevent movement, long enough to span joints, and padded enough for comfort. Two sticks + cloth = functional splint.

techniques.name: Heat Illness Spectrum
techniques.subtitle: Recognize and treat heat cramps → exhaustion → stroke

steps.instruction: Heat Cramps (mild): muscle spasms during exertion. Stop activity, rest in shade, drink electrolyte water.
steps.helpDetail: Cramps are caused by salt and water depletion. Stretch affected muscles gently. Add 1/4 teaspoon salt per liter of water.

steps.instruction: Heat Exhaustion (moderate): heavy sweating, weakness, nausea, headache, cool clammy skin, core temp under 104°F.
steps.helpDetail: Remove excess clothing, apply cool water to skin, fan vigorously. Sip cool water if not vomiting. Rest in shade. This is reversible.

steps.instruction: Heat Stroke (CRITICAL): core temp above 104°F, confusion, hot DRY skin, seizures, unconsciousness.
steps.helpDetail: Heat stroke is life-threatening. The body's cooling system has failed. Brain damage begins rapidly. This is a true emergency.

steps.instruction: For heat stroke: cool AGGRESSIVELY. Immerse in cold water, pack ice around neck, armpits, groin.
steps.helpDetail: Every minute of cooling matters. If no ice bath, soak clothing in cold water and fan. Evaporative cooling is the fastest field method.

steps.instruction: Do NOT give fluids to a heat stroke patient who is confused or unconscious. Continue cooling until temp drops to 101°F.
steps.helpDetail: Confused patients cannot protect their airway and may aspirate fluids. Focus entirely on external cooling. Stop cooling at 101°F to prevent overcorrection.
steps.illustrationDescription: Do NOT give fluids to a heat s

techniques.name: Traumatic Amputation Response
techniques.subtitle: Manage a severed body part in the field

steps.instruction: Control bleeding on the stump FIRST. Apply direct pressure, then tourniquet if bleeding is severe.
steps.helpDetail: The patient's life takes priority over the severed part. Apply tourniquet 2-3 inches above the amputation. Note the time applied.

steps.instruction: Wrap the stump with sterile gauze or clean cloth. Apply firm pressure bandage.
steps.helpDetail: Do NOT apply dirt, leaves, or spider webs (folk remedies). Clean, firm pressure is the standard. Elevate if possible.
steps.illustrationDescription: Wrap the stump with sterile ga

steps.instruction: Treat for shock: lay flat, elevate legs, keep warm, reassure.
steps.helpDetail: Amputation causes both hemorrhagic shock (blood loss) and psychological shock. Talk to the patient. Monitor breathing and consciousness.
steps.illustrationDescription: Treat for shock: lay flat, ele

steps.instruction: Recover the severed part if safe to do so. Rinse gently with clean water.
steps.helpDetail: Do NOT scrub the part. Remove gross contamination only. Every piece matters — surgeons can sometimes reattach partial tissue.
steps.illustrationDescription: Recover the severed part if sa

steps.instruction: Wrap the part in moist gauze, place in a sealed bag, then place bag on ice. Do NOT freeze or submerge in water.
steps.helpDetail: Cool, not frozen. Direct ice contact damages tissue. Moist gauze → sealed bag → bag on ice. Reimplantation is possible up to 6-12 hours for fingers.
steps.illustrationDescription: Wrap the part in moist gauze,

techniques.name: Tension Pneumothorax Recognition
techniques.subtitle: Identify life-threatening air pressure in the chest

steps.instruction: Recognize: worsening difficulty breathing after chest injury, even with a chest seal applied.
steps.helpDetail: Air is leaking into the chest cavity with each breath but cannot escape. Pressure builds, compressing the heart and good lung.

steps.instruction: Look for: distended (bulging) neck veins, trachea shifting to one side, absent breath sounds on injured side.
steps.helpDetail: These are classic signs. Distended veins occur because blood cannot return to the compressed heart.

steps.instruction: Patient will be anxious, tachycardic (fast heart rate), hypotensive (low blood pressure), and cyanotic (blue).
steps.helpDetail: This is rapidly fatal if untreated. Death occurs from cardiac arrest as the heart is compressed and cannot fill with blood.

steps.instruction: Emergency treatment: if chest seal is applied, briefly LIFT the seal to allow trapped air to escape.
steps.helpDetail: Burp the seal — a rush of air should escape, providing immediate relief. If symptoms recur, burp again. This buys time.

steps.instruction: Needle decompression (if trained): 14-gauge needle in 2nd intercostal space, midclavicular line, injured side.
steps.helpDetail: Insert needle over the top of the 3rd rib (avoids the nerve/artery below each rib). A rush of air confirms tension pneumothorax. This is an advanced procedure.

techniques.name: Emergency Eye Irrigation
techniques.subtitle: Flush chemicals or debris from the eye

steps.instruction: Chemical exposure: begin flushing IMMEDIATELY. Do not wait to identify the chemical.
steps.helpDetail: Time is tissue. Every second of delayed flushing increases permanent damage. Tilt head so contaminated eye is lower (prevents chemical running into good eye).

steps.instruction: Use clean water — pour steadily from a bottle or cup. Hold eye open with fingers.
steps.helpDetail: The victim will reflexively close the eye. You may need to hold it open. Flush from inner corner (near nose) outward.

steps.instruction: Flush continuously for minimum 15-20 minutes for chemicals. 5 minutes for debris.
steps.helpDetail: Set a timer. 15 minutes feels like forever but is necessary for chemical burns. Use 1-2 liters of water total.

steps.instruction: For embedded debris (metal, glass): do NOT rub. Cover with a cup or cone taped over the eye.
steps.helpDetail: Rubbing drives debris deeper and risks corneal laceration. A paper cup taped over the eye socket protects from pressure.

steps.instruction: After flushing: cover loosely with clean cloth. Do not patch tightly. Seek medical care.
steps.helpDetail: Patching tightly traps bacteria and can worsen infection. A loose cover prevents light sensitivity. Do not apply ointments without medical guidance.

techniques.name: Seizure Response
techniques.subtitle: Protect a seizing person from injury

steps.instruction: Do NOT restrain the person. Do NOT put anything in their mouth.
steps.helpDetail: You cannot stop a seizure. Restraining can cause fractures. Bite guards/spoons cause broken teeth and airway obstruction. People do not swallow their tongues.
steps.illustrationDescription: Do NOT restrain the person. Do

steps.instruction: Clear the area of hazards: move furniture, sharp objects, rocks away from the person.
steps.helpDetail: Prevent injury from thrashing. If on a hard surface, place something soft under their head — jacket, backpack, your hands.
steps.illustrationDescription: Clear the area of hazards: mov

steps.instruction: Time the seizure. If it exceeds 5 minutes, this is a medical emergency.
steps.helpDetail: Status epilepticus (seizure >5 min) can cause brain damage. Call for help if available. Note the time seizure started.
steps.illustrationDescription: Time the seizure. If it exceed

steps.instruction: Once convulsions stop, roll the person onto their side (recovery position).
steps.helpDetail: Post-seizure, the person may vomit or have increased secretions. Side position keeps the airway clear. They will be confused and drowsy.

steps.instruction: Stay with them until fully alert. Speak calmly. Orient them: 'You had a seizure. You are safe. It's [time].'
steps.helpDetail: Post-ictal confusion can last 5-30 minutes. They may be combative if startled. Do not crowd them. Let them recover at their pace.
steps.illustrationDescription: Stay with them until fully ale

techniques.name: Hypothermia Wrap
techniques.subtitle: Insulate a hypothermia patient for rewarming

steps.instruction: Remove all wet clothing. Cut off if patient is shivering too hard to undress.
steps.helpDetail: Wet clothing conducts heat away 25x faster than dry clothing. Every second in wet clothes worsens hypothermia.
steps.illustrationDescription: Remove all wet clothing. Cut o

steps.instruction: Lay a vapor barrier (tarp, emergency blanket, trash bags) on the ground first.
steps.helpDetail: Ground conduction is a major heat drain. The vapor barrier prevents body heat from being absorbed by cold ground.

steps.instruction: Wrap patient in dry insulation layers: sleeping bag, blankets, dry clothing. Cover head.
steps.helpDetail: Head accounts for 10% of heat loss. Cover everything. Multiple thin layers trap more air and insulate better than one thick layer.

steps.instruction: Add heat sources to the core: warm water bottles at neck, armpits, and groin.
steps.helpDetail: Do NOT rewarm extremities first — this causes cold blood to rush to the heart, potentially triggering cardiac arrest (afterdrop).
steps.illustrationDescription: Add heat sources to the core:

steps.instruction: Wrap the outer layer with another vapor barrier (tarp/emergency blanket). Create a 'burrito.'
steps.helpDetail: The outer vapor barrier reflects radiated heat back and blocks wind. Patient's own body heat, trapped in the insulation, gradually rewarms them.
steps.illustrationDescription: Wrap the outer layer with anot

techniques.name: Foreign Body in Ear
techniques.subtitle: Remove objects or insects from the ear canal

steps.instruction: If a live insect is buzzing in the ear: tilt head so affected ear faces up, pour in warm oil (olive, vegetable, or mineral).
steps.helpDetail: Oil suffocates the insect and stops the extremely distressing buzzing. Wait 1-2 minutes for insect to die, then tilt ear down to drain.
steps.illustrationDescription: If a live insect is buzzing in

steps.instruction: For visible objects near the canal opening: use tweezers ONLY if you can clearly see and grasp the object.
steps.helpDetail: Never probe blindly into the ear canal. You risk pushing the object deeper or rupturing the eardrum. If you can't see it, don't fish.
steps.illustrationDescription: For visible objects near the c

steps.instruction: Gravity method: tilt head with affected ear down and gently pull earlobe backward and downward.
steps.helpDetail: This straightens the ear canal and may allow the object to fall out with gravity. Gently jiggle the head.

steps.instruction: Irrigation: use warm (body temperature) water with a bulb syringe. Squirt gently along the canal wall, NOT directly at the object.
steps.helpDetail: Do NOT irrigate if a button battery is suspected — water accelerates chemical burns. Do not irrigate if eardrum may be perforated.

steps.instruction: If unsuccessful, leave the object alone and seek medical help. Do NOT try repeatedly.
steps.helpDetail: Repeated attempts cause canal swelling, making professional removal harder. Ears are delicate. An intact eardrum is more important than immediate removal.

techniques.name: Field Blood Pressure Assessment
techniques.subtitle: Estimate blood pressure without equipment

steps.instruction: Check radial pulse (wrist): if present, systolic blood pressure is at least 80 mmHg.
steps.helpDetail: The radial artery requires ~80 mmHg to produce a palpable pulse. Absence indicates severe hypotension or shock.

steps.instruction: Check femoral pulse (groin): if present, systolic BP is at least 70 mmHg.
steps.helpDetail: If radial pulse is absent but femoral is present, patient is in compensated shock. Act urgently.

steps.instruction: Check carotid pulse (neck): if present, systolic BP is at least 60 mmHg.
steps.helpDetail: If only the carotid is palpable, the patient is in severe shock. Death is imminent without intervention. Hemorrhage control + fluid resuscitation.

steps.instruction: Assess capillary refill: press fingernail bed white, release, count seconds to return pink.
steps.helpDetail: Normal: under 2 seconds. 2-4 seconds: reduced perfusion. Over 4 seconds: shock. Cold environments falsely prolong refill time.

steps.instruction: Mental status is the most sensitive indicator: alert → confused → verbal response only → unresponsive.
steps.helpDetail: The brain is the first organ to suffer from reduced blood flow. Confusion is often the earliest sign of shock before pulse changes.
steps.illustrationDescription: Mental status is the most sens

techniques.name: Knee Injury Stabilization
techniques.subtitle: Immobilize a knee injury in the field

steps.instruction: Immobilize the knee in the position found. If bent, splint bent. If straight, splint straight.
steps.helpDetail: Forcing a knee straight or bent can worsen ligament tears, dislocations, or fractures. Splint in the position of comfort.
steps.illustrationDescription: Immobilize the knee in the pos

steps.instruction: For a straight leg: place padded splints on both sides from mid-thigh to below the ankle.
steps.helpDetail: Sticks, trekking poles, tent poles work well. Padding is essential — especially behind the knee and at the ankle bones.
steps.illustrationDescription: For a straight leg: place padd

steps.instruction: For a bent leg: support with a figure-8 bandage and leave the knee flexed at a comfortable angle.
steps.helpDetail: A blanket rolled under the calf provides support. Do not attempt to straighten. Secure with triangular bandage or cravat wraps.
steps.illustrationDescription: For a bent leg: support with a

steps.instruction: Apply RICE: Rest, Ice (wrapped — never directly on skin), Compression, Elevation.
steps.helpDetail: Ice reduces swelling and pain. 20 minutes on, 20 off. In the field, cold water-soaked cloth or snow in a bag works. Elevate leg above heart.

steps.instruction: Check circulation below the splint: feel for foot pulse, check skin color and temperature, wiggle toes.
steps.helpDetail: A tight splint can cut off circulation. Recheck every 30 minutes. If foot turns blue, cold, or numb, loosen immediately.

techniques.name: Near-Drowning Aftercare
techniques.subtitle: Post-rescue management of drowning victims

steps.instruction: Even if the person seems fine after rescue, ALL near-drowning victims need monitoring for 24+ hours.
steps.helpDetail: Secondary drowning (pulmonary edema) can develop 1-24 hours after the event. Water in the lungs causes delayed inflammation and fluid buildup.
steps.illustrationDescription: Even if the person seems fine

steps.instruction: Remove wet clothing, dry thoroughly, rewarm. Treat for hypothermia if cold water.
steps.helpDetail: Cold water accelerates hypothermia. Rewarm the core first (blankets, warm drinks if alert). Avoid hot water or heating pads directly on skin.

steps.instruction: Position: if conscious, sit upright or in recovery position. If unconscious, recovery position.
steps.helpDetail: Upright position helps drain water from airways. Be prepared for repeated vomiting — position for airway protection.

steps.instruction: Watch for delayed symptoms: coughing, chest pain, difficulty breathing, foamy sputum, lethargy.
steps.helpDetail: These signs can appear hours later and indicate water in the lungs. Any coughing or chest discomfort after near-drowning demands medical evaluation.

steps.instruction: If breathing deteriorates at any point, begin rescue breathing. Have a plan for evacuation.
steps.helpDetail: Near-drowning can progress rapidly. Keep the victim awake and talking. Monitor breathing rate — increasing rate indicates pulmonary edema developing.

techniques.name: Chilblains Treatment
techniques.subtitle: Manage cold-induced skin inflammation

steps.instruction: Recognize: red or purple swollen patches on fingers, toes, ears, or nose that itch and burn.
steps.helpDetail: Chilblains result from repeated cold exposure without freezing. They are NOT frostbite — skin is damaged but not frozen.

steps.instruction: Rewarm GRADUALLY. Move to a warm area. Do NOT apply direct heat (no fire, hot water, or heating pads).
steps.helpDetail: Rapid rewarming causes painful vasodilation and worsens inflammation. Let the area warm naturally at room temperature.
steps.illustrationDescription: Rewarm GRADUALLY. Move to a wa

steps.instruction: Keep the area dry and covered. Apply gentle moisturizer to prevent cracking.
steps.helpDetail: Cracked chilblains are infection-prone. Lanolin or petroleum jelly protects fragile skin. Do not scratch despite intense itching.
steps.illustrationDescription: Keep the area dry and covered.

steps.instruction: Elevate affected hands or feet to reduce swelling.
steps.helpDetail: Elevation reduces edema and pain. Wiggle fingers/toes gently to promote circulation without causing friction on damaged skin.

steps.instruction: Prevention: keep extremities dry, change wet socks frequently, wear vapor barrier liners in boots.
steps.helpDetail: Chilblains are preventable. Moisture is the enemy — wet cold causes more chilblains than dry cold. Carry spare socks.

techniques.name: Fishhook Removal
techniques.subtitle: Remove an embedded fishhook in the field

steps.instruction: Assess the hook: is the barb visible through the skin (push-through possible) or deeply embedded?
steps.helpDetail: Three removal techniques exist depending on depth. If near the eye, in a joint, or deeply embedded in face/neck — do NOT attempt removal.
steps.illustrationDescription: Assess the hook: is the barb v

steps.instruction: String-yank method (most common): loop strong line around the hook bend, press the shank down, yank the line parallel to the skin.
steps.helpDetail: The downward pressure on the shank disengages the barb. The quick yank pulls the hook out along the entry path. One firm, fast pull.

steps.instruction: Push-through method (barbless or shallow): advance the hook through the skin until the barb exits, cut off the barb, back the hook out.
steps.helpDetail: Only use if the hook is close to exiting. Clean the exit point. Cut the barb with wire cutters, pliers, or a multi-tool.

steps.instruction: Clean the wound thoroughly with clean water. The wound will look small but is a puncture — infection risk is high.
steps.helpDetail: Irrigate with pressure. Puncture wounds are particularly prone to tetanus. Monitor for redness, swelling, and pus over the next 3-5 days.

steps.instruction: Apply antibiotic ointment if available and cover with a bandage. Watch for signs of infection.
steps.helpDetail: Change the dressing daily. If redness spreads or fever develops, the wound is infected and needs medical attention.

techniques.name: Lightning Strike First Aid
techniques.subtitle: Respond to a lightning strike victim

steps.instruction: Lightning victims do NOT carry an electrical charge — it is SAFE to touch them immediately.
steps.helpDetail: This is the most common myth. Do not hesitate to provide first aid. Move to a safer location if lightning continues.

steps.instruction: Check responsiveness and breathing. Begin CPR immediately if no pulse or breathing.
steps.helpDetail: Lightning's most common cause of death is cardiac arrest. Immediate CPR has a high success rate — lightning victims have excellent survival if CPR begins quickly.

steps.instruction: Reverse triage: in a mass casualty situation, treat the 'dead-looking' patients FIRST.
steps.helpDetail: This is opposite to normal triage. Those who appear dead from lightning often respond to CPR. Those who are conscious will likely survive without immediate intervention.
steps.illustrationDescription: Reverse triage: in a mass casu

steps.instruction: Check for burns at entry and exit points — common locations: head, shoulders, feet.
steps.helpDetail: Burns are usually superficial. The lightning 'Lichtenberg figure' (fern pattern) on skin is not a burn — it fades in 24 hours.
steps.illustrationDescription: Check for burns at entry and e

steps.instruction: Monitor for delayed complications: confusion, memory gaps, hearing loss, vision changes, muscle pain.
steps.helpDetail: Even 'mild' lightning exposure can cause cardiac arrhythmia, ruptured eardrums, and neurological damage. All lightning victims need medical evaluation.

techniques.name: Abdominal Assessment
techniques.subtitle: Evaluate abdominal injury severity in the field

steps.instruction: Have the patient lie flat with knees slightly bent to relax abdominal muscles.
steps.helpDetail: Bent knees reduce tension on the abdominal wall, making palpation easier and less painful. Remove belt and loosen clothing.
steps.illustrationDescription: Have the patient lie flat with

steps.instruction: Visually inspect: look for bruising, distension (swelling), asymmetry, or open wounds.
steps.helpDetail: Bruising patterns indicate internal injury: left upper = spleen, right upper = liver. Distension indicates internal bleeding or organ swelling.

steps.instruction: Gently palpate all four quadrants, starting AWAY from the pain. Note rigid or guarded areas.
steps.helpDetail: Start at the quadrant farthest from reported pain. Rigidity (board-like hardness) indicates peritonitis — a surgical emergency.

steps.instruction: Monitor for signs of shock: rapid pulse, pale/cool skin, confusion, falling blood pressure.
steps.helpDetail: Internal abdominal bleeding can be massive yet invisible externally. Shock signs = internal hemorrhage until proven otherwise.
steps.illustrationDescription: Monitor for signs of shock: ra

steps.instruction: Position of comfort: left lateral (left side down) if vomiting. Keep warm. Nothing by mouth.
steps.helpDetail: NPO (nothing by mouth) is critical — abdominal injuries may require surgery. Evacuate immediately if shock signs appear.

techniques.name: Femoral Artery Pressure Point
techniques.subtitle: Control severe leg bleeding using pressure points

steps.instruction: Locate the femoral artery: in the crease where the leg meets the groin, midway between the hip bone and pubic bone.
steps.helpDetail: Feel for the pulse — the femoral artery is large and the pulse is strong. This is the main blood supply to the entire leg.

steps.instruction: Press firmly with the heel of your hand directly over the pulse point.
steps.helpDetail: Use body weight. The artery must be compressed against the pelvic bone underneath. This requires significant force.
steps.illustrationDescription: Press firmly with the heel of

steps.instruction: Maintain constant pressure while another person applies direct pressure or a tourniquet to the wound.
steps.helpDetail: Pressure point control is temporary — it buys time to prepare a tourniquet or proper bandage. Do not release until the wound is controlled.

steps.instruction: If alone: apply a tourniquet high and tight on the upper thigh, then release pressure point.
steps.helpDetail: Tourniquet goes as close to the groin as possible. Tighten until bleeding stops. Note the time. Do NOT remove once applied.
steps.illustrationDescription: If alone: apply a tourniquet h

steps.instruction: Monitor for shock: keep the patient warm, elevate uninjured legs, and reassure them.
steps.helpDetail: Femoral bleeds can be fatal in minutes. Even after control, the patient needs medical evacuation. Monitor consciousness and breathing.
steps.illustrationDescription: Monitor for shock: keep the pa

techniques.name: Emergency Dental Pain Relief
techniques.subtitle: Manage dental emergencies with field materials

steps.instruction: For a lost filling: pack the cavity with softened candle wax, chewing gum, or pine resin.
steps.helpDetail: Temporary filling material protects the exposed nerve from air, food, and temperature. Any pliable, clean material works.
steps.illustrationDescription: For a lost filling: pack the c

steps.instruction: For toothache: apply crushed clove (eugenol) or a piece of fresh garlic directly to the painful tooth.
steps.helpDetail: Clove oil contains eugenol, a natural anesthetic. Garlic has antibacterial properties. Both reduce pain significantly.

steps.instruction: For a knocked-out tooth: keep it moist in saliva (between cheek and gum) or milk. Reimplant within 30 minutes if possible.
steps.helpDetail: Handle the tooth by the crown ONLY — never touch the root. Gently rinse if dirty. Push into the socket and hold with gauze.

steps.instruction: For a broken tooth: cover sharp edges with wax to prevent tongue/cheek cuts. Take pain relief if available.
steps.helpDetail: A broken tooth exposes nerves. Covering the sharp edge reduces pain from air exposure and prevents soft tissue injury.
steps.illustrationDescription: For a broken tooth: cover shar

steps.instruction: For an abscess (swelling with pus): rinse with warm salt water every 2 hours. Do NOT pop. Seek evacuation.
steps.helpDetail: Dental abscesses can spread to the bloodstream (sepsis). Salt water reduces bacteria. Antibiotic treatment is essential — evacuate.

techniques.name: Core Temperature Assessment
techniques.subtitle: Assess hypothermia or hyperthermia severity without a thermometer

steps.instruction: Touch the skin at the abdomen or armpit — these reflect core temperature better than extremities.
steps.helpDetail: Hands and feet cool first and are unreliable. Trunk skin temperature correlates better with core temp.
steps.illustrationDescription: Touch the skin at the abdomen

steps.instruction: Shivering assessment: mild hypothermia = vigorous shivering. Severe = shivering STOPS (very dangerous sign).
steps.helpDetail: Shivering is the body's heat generation mechanism. When it stops, the body has exhausted its ability to warm itself.

steps.instruction: Mental status: mild cold = clear thinking. Moderate = confusion, slurred speech. Severe = unconscious.
steps.helpDetail: Mental decline tracks closely with core temperature drop. Confusion at ~90°F. Unconsciousness at ~86°F.

steps.instruction: For heat: hot, DRY skin = heat stroke (emergency). Hot, WET skin = heat exhaustion (serious but less critical).
steps.helpDetail: The absence of sweating in extreme heat means the body's cooling system has failed. Heat stroke is immediately life-threatening.
steps.illustrationDescription: For heat: hot, DRY skin = heat

steps.instruction: Pulse assessment: cold = slow, weak pulse. Heat stroke = rapid, bounding pulse. Both indicate core temp emergency.
steps.helpDetail: Combine pulse with skin feel and mental status for a reliable field assessment without instruments.

techniques.name: Figure-8 Ankle Bandage
techniques.subtitle: Properly wrap a sprained ankle for support

steps.instruction: Start with two anchor wraps around the ball of the foot.
steps.helpDetail: Begin just behind the toes. Wrap twice to secure the starting point. Maintain moderate tension — not too tight.
steps.illustrationDescription: Start with two anchor wraps ar

steps.instruction: Bring the bandage diagonally across the top of the foot to the outside of the ankle.
steps.helpDetail: The diagonal across the top of the foot creates the first half of the figure-8 pattern.

steps.instruction: Wrap behind the ankle, then cross diagonally back across the front of the ankle to the inside bottom of the foot.
steps.helpDetail: This completes the figure-8 loop. The crossover point on the front of the ankle provides lateral stability.
steps.illustrationDescription: Wrap behind the ankle, then cr

steps.instruction: Repeat the figure-8 pattern 3-4 times, overlapping each layer by half.
steps.helpDetail: Overlapping layers distribute pressure evenly. Each loop should be the same tension as the previous.

steps.instruction: Secure the end with tape, a clip, or tuck under the wraps. Check circulation: toes should stay pink and warm.
steps.helpDetail: If toes become pale, blue, numb, or cold — the wrap is too tight. Unwrap and redo with less tension.
steps.illustrationDescription: Secure the end with tape, a cl

techniques.name: Sucking Chest Wound Dressing
techniques.subtitle: Seal an open chest wound to restore breathing

steps.instruction: Identify: bubbling or sucking sound from a chest wound on breathing. Air entering the chest cavity collapses the lung.
steps.helpDetail: Any penetrating wound to the chest between the collarbones and the bottom of the ribcage can cause this. The sound is distinctive.

steps.instruction: Cover the wound IMMEDIATELY with any airtight material: plastic wrap, credit card, duct tape, foil, or a gloved hand.
steps.helpDetail: Time is critical. Every breath draws air into the chest cavity. Use whatever is fastest — even your palm.

steps.instruction: Tape the covering on THREE sides only, leaving the bottom edge open.
steps.helpDetail: The three-sided seal acts as a flutter valve: on inhale, the seal closes (preventing air entry). On exhale, the open bottom lets trapped air escape.
steps.illustrationDescription: Tape the covering on THREE sid

steps.instruction: Check for an EXIT wound on the back — there may be two holes. Both need sealing.
steps.helpDetail: Roll the patient to check. If an exit wound exists, seal it the same way. Mark the time of injury.
steps.illustrationDescription: Check for an EXIT wound on the

steps.instruction: Monitor breathing closely. If breathing worsens despite the seal, briefly lift one edge to release trapped air.
steps.helpDetail: Tension pneumothorax (too much trapped air) is as dangerous as the original wound. If the patient deteriorates, burp the seal.

techniques.name: Abdominal Evisceration Management
techniques.subtitle: Handle exposed abdominal organs in the field

steps.instruction: DO NOT push organs back in. DO NOT touch them with bare hands.
steps.helpDetail: Reinserting organs risks further contamination and damage. Bare hands introduce bacteria. Use the cleanest materials available.
steps.illustrationDescription: DO NOT push organs back in. DO

steps.instruction: Cover exposed organs with a moist, clean dressing — soaked in clean water or saline if available.
steps.helpDetail: Organs must stay MOIST. Drying causes tissue death. Use a clean cloth soaked in the cleanest water you have.
steps.illustrationDescription: Cover exposed organs with a mo

steps.instruction: Cover the moist dressing with plastic wrap or a waterproof layer to retain moisture.
steps.helpDetail: This creates a sealed, moist environment. The plastic prevents evaporation. Secure loosely — do not compress.

steps.instruction: Position patient on their back with knees bent and supported (pillow, rolled clothing under knees).
steps.helpDetail: Bent knees reduce tension on the abdominal wall. This reduces pain and prevents organs from protruding further.

steps.instruction: Keep warm, monitor for shock, give nothing by mouth. Evacuate IMMEDIATELY — this is a surgical emergency.
steps.helpDetail: This injury is unsurvivable without surgical intervention. Every minute matters. Keep patient calm and warm.

techniques.name: Emergency Eye Patching
techniques.subtitle: Protect an injured eye in the field

steps.instruction: For objects embedded in the eye: DO NOT remove. Stabilize with a cup, cone, or ring of cloth around the object.
steps.helpDetail: The cup prevents accidental contact with the embedded object. A paper cup with holes cut in the bottom works. Tape securely.
steps.illustrationDescription: For objects embedded in the ey

steps.instruction: Patch BOTH eyes — not just the injured one. Eyes move together (consensual movement).
steps.helpDetail: When one eye moves, the other moves identically. Patching both eyes prevents the injured eye from tracking.
steps.illustrationDescription: Patch BOTH eyes — not just the

steps.instruction: Use a soft pad over the closed eye, secured with tape or a bandage wrap around the head.
steps.helpDetail: The pad should apply zero pressure on the eyeball. Just cover it. For chemical burns, flush FIRST for 20 minutes, then patch.
steps.illustrationDescription: Use a soft pad over the closed

steps.instruction: For chemical exposure: flush the eye with clean water for at least 20 minutes BEFORE patching.
steps.helpDetail: Tilt head so water flows from the nose side outward — preventing contamination of the other eye. Use a steady, gentle stream.

steps.instruction: Guide the patient — they are now effectively blind. Keep them calm and describe the environment.
steps.helpDetail: A blind patient is disoriented and anxious. Verbal guidance and reassurance are as important as the physical treatment.
steps.illustrationDescription: Guide the patient — they are n

techniques.name: Cravat Bandage Sling
techniques.subtitle: Use a triangular bandage for arm support

steps.instruction: Fold a square cloth (shirt, bandana, etc.) diagonally to create a triangle.
steps.helpDetail: Any fabric approximately 36 inches square works. A t-shirt cut corner-to-corner creates two triangular bandages.

steps.instruction: Place the forearm across the triangle with the elbow at the point and the hand toward the longest edge.
steps.helpDetail: The elbow sits in the triangle's point. The wrist sits near the long edge. The forearm should be slightly elevated.
steps.illustrationDescription: Place the forearm across the t

steps.instruction: Bring both ends up and tie behind the neck. Adjust so the forearm is supported across the chest.
steps.helpDetail: The knot should sit to one side of the spine, not directly on it. Pad the knot with soft material to prevent neck pressure.
steps.illustrationDescription: Bring both ends up and tie beh

steps.instruction: Pin or tie the elbow point to prevent the arm from swinging.
steps.helpDetail: Securing the elbow creates a stable, immobile sling. Use a safety pin, tape, or twist and tuck the fabric.
steps.illustrationDescription: Pin or tie the elbow point to

steps.instruction: Check circulation: fingers should stay pink, warm, and mobile. Adjust tightness if circulation is compromised.
steps.helpDetail: The sling should support weight but not compress vessels. Test by pressing a fingernail — it should blanch white then return pink in 2 seconds.

techniques.name: Rapid Trauma Assessment (MARCH)
techniques.subtitle: Systematic trauma assessment in the field

steps.instruction: M — Massive hemorrhage: scan the entire body for life-threatening bleeding. Apply tourniquet or pressure immediately.
steps.helpDetail: This is the #1 killer in trauma. Check the obvious areas: limbs, neck, groin. Bright red spurting = arterial. Dark oozing = venous.

steps.instruction: A — Airway: is the airway open? Tilt head, lift chin. Clear blood, vomit, or debris from the mouth.
steps.helpDetail: Unconscious patients' tongues can block the airway. The head-tilt chin-lift is the fastest airway intervention.
steps.illustrationDescription: A — Airway: is the airway open

steps.instruction: R — Respiration: is the patient breathing? Check for adequate rise and fall of the chest. Seal chest wounds.
steps.helpDetail: Look, listen, and feel for 10 seconds. Unequal chest rise = possible pneumothorax. Absent breathing = start rescue breathing.

steps.instruction: C — Circulation: check pulse (wrist or neck). Assess skin color, temperature, and moisture for shock.
steps.helpDetail: Rapid, weak pulse + pale, cool, clammy skin = shock. Slow, bounding pulse in heat = heat stroke. No pulse = CPR.

steps.instruction: H — Hypothermia/Head: protect from heat loss (insulate from ground, cover). Assess consciousness (AVPU scale).
steps.helpDetail: AVPU: Alert, responds to Voice, responds to Pain, Unresponsive. All trauma patients trend toward hypothermia — insulate proactively.

techniques.name: Nail Bed Injury Treatment
techniques.subtitle: Handle torn or lost fingernails and toenails

steps.instruction: If the nail is partially torn: do NOT pull it off. Trim the loose section and leave the attached portion.
steps.helpDetail: Pulling a partially attached nail causes extreme pain and tears the nail bed. Cut the loose part with scissors or a knife.
steps.illustrationDescription: If the nail is partially torn:

steps.instruction: If the nail is completely lost: clean the exposed nail bed gently with clean water. Do not scrub.
steps.helpDetail: The nail bed is extremely sensitive. Gentle irrigation removes debris without damaging the regenerating tissue.
steps.illustrationDescription: If the nail is completely lost

steps.instruction: Apply antibiotic ointment if available. Cover with a non-stick pad secured with tape.
steps.helpDetail: The nail bed needs protection from friction, debris, and bacteria. A non-stick pad prevents the healing tissue from bonding to the bandage.

steps.instruction: For a subungual hematoma (blood under the nail causing throbbing pain): heat a paperclip and melt through the nail.
steps.helpDetail: This is called trephination. The hole releases trapped blood and instantly relieves the pressure pain. Do NOT press — let the hot metal melt through.

steps.instruction: Change dressings daily. A new nail will grow back in 3-6 months. Keep the area clean and protected.
steps.helpDetail: Nail regrowth is slow but reliable. The main risk is infection. Monitor for redness, swelling, warmth, or pus — signs of infection.

techniques.name: Field Wound Closure
techniques.subtitle: Close wounds using butterfly strips or improvised sutures

steps.instruction: ONLY close wounds that are clean, less than 6 hours old, and have smooth edges.
steps.helpDetail: Never close: animal bites, crush injuries, dirty/embedded-debris wounds, or punctures. These MUST heal from inside out (secondary intention).
steps.illustrationDescription: ONLY close wounds that are cle

steps.instruction: Butterfly strips: cut tape into narrow strips with wider ends. Apply perpendicular to the wound, pulling edges together.
steps.helpDetail: Space strips 1/4 inch apart. Apply one side, pull the wound edges together, then press the other side. Don't overlap the wound itself.

steps.instruction: Improvised sutures: use a sterilized fishhook or needle and clean thread (dental floss, inner paracord strand).
steps.helpDetail: Sterilize by boiling for 10 minutes or heating in flame until glowing. Floss is stronger than thread. Boil 10 minutes.

steps.instruction: Simple interrupted suture: enter 1/4 inch from wound edge, pass through both sides, tie with square knot.
steps.helpDetail: Space sutures 1/4 inch apart. Tie just tight enough to bring edges together — not so tight that skin bunches. Cut excess thread.

steps.instruction: Monitor for infection daily: redness spreading from wound, increased swelling, warmth, pus, or red streaks = remove sutures immediately.
steps.helpDetail: An infected closed wound is far more dangerous than an open one. If ANY infection signs appear, remove closures and irrigate aggressively.

techniques.name: Hands-Only CPR
techniques.subtitle: Simplified CPR for untrained bystanders

steps.instruction: Confirm the person is unresponsive: tap shoulders firmly and shout 'Are you okay?'
steps.helpDetail: If no response and no normal breathing (gasping does NOT count as breathing), begin CPR immediately. Every minute without CPR reduces survival by 10%.

steps.instruction: Call for help. If alone, call emergency services first if you have a phone. Speaker mode.
steps.helpDetail: In wilderness: designate someone to go for help while you do CPR. Don't stop CPR to make the call if someone else can go.
steps.illustrationDescription: Call for help. If alone, call

steps.instruction: Place the heel of one hand on the center of the chest (between the nipples). Stack the other hand on top.
steps.helpDetail: Interlace fingers. Lock elbows straight. Position yourself directly above the patient so your arms are vertical.
steps.illustrationDescription: Place the heel of one hand on

steps.instruction: Push HARD and FAST: compress at least 2 inches deep, at 100-120 beats per minute.
steps.helpDetail: Think of the beat of 'Stayin' Alive' by the Bee Gees — that's 104 BPM. Let the chest fully recoil between compressions.
steps.illustrationDescription: Push HARD and FAST: compress a

steps.instruction: DO NOT STOP until: the person starts breathing, professional help arrives, or you are physically unable to continue.
steps.helpDetail: Compression-only CPR doubles survival compared to doing nothing. Quality matters: push hard, push fast, don't stop.
steps.illustrationDescription: DO NOT STOP until: the person

techniques.name: Chemical Water Purification
techniques.subtitle: Use iodine or bleach to purify drinking water

steps.instruction: Iodine: add 5-10 drops of 2% tincture of iodine per liter of clear water. Double for cloudy water.
steps.helpDetail: Iodine kills bacteria, viruses, and most protozoa. Giardia requires longer contact time. Available at pharmacies and outdoor stores.

steps.instruction: Bleach: add 2 drops of unscented household bleach (5-8% sodium hypochlorite) per liter of clear water.
steps.helpDetail: Use ONLY plain, unscented bleach. Scented or splashless formulations contain additives unsafe for consumption.

steps.instruction: Wait 30 minutes before drinking. If water is cold or cloudy, wait 60 minutes.
steps.helpDetail: Chemical disinfection requires contact time. Cold water slows the chemical reaction. Cloudy water has more organic matter to neutralize.

steps.instruction: After treatment, water should have a slight chemical taste/smell. If not, add another dose and wait 15 more minutes.
steps.helpDetail: The slight taste confirms sufficient chemical concentration remained after neutralizing pathogens. No taste = all chemical was consumed by organic matter.

steps.instruction: Limitations: chemical treatment does NOT remove chemical contaminants, and may not kill Cryptosporidium.
steps.helpDetail: Crypto is resistant to both iodine and chlorine at normal doses. For Crypto-prone water sources, BOILING is the only reliable field method.

techniques.name: Snake Bite Pressure Immobilization
techniques.subtitle: Apply pressure immobilization for neurotoxic snake bites

steps.instruction: DO NOT: cut the wound, suck venom, apply ice, use a tourniquet, or apply electric shock.
steps.helpDetail: All of these 'treatments' are myths that cause additional harm. None removes venom. Some cause tissue death.
steps.illustrationDescription: DO NOT: cut the wound, suck ve

steps.instruction: Keep the victim CALM and STILL. Movement accelerates venom spread through the lymphatic system.
steps.helpDetail: Carried, not walking, is ideal. If alone, move slowly and deliberately. Panic increases heart rate and venom circulation.
steps.illustrationDescription: Keep the victim CALM and STILL

steps.instruction: For neurotoxic snakes (cobras, mambas, sea snakes): apply a compression bandage starting at the bite, wrapping toward the heart.
steps.helpDetail: The bandage should be firm but not tight — you should be able to slide a finger under it. This slows lymphatic drainage, not blood flow.

steps.instruction: Immobilize the bitten limb with a splint. Movement of muscles pumps lymph fluid, spreading venom.
steps.helpDetail: Splint the entire limb. The patient should move as little as possible. Carry them if possible.

steps.instruction: For hemotoxic snakes (vipers, rattlesnakes): do NOT apply compression bandage. Keep limb below heart level and evacuate.
steps.helpDetail: Hemotoxic venom causes local tissue destruction. Compression traps the venom in one area, increasing local damage. Different snakes = different treatment.

techniques.name: Field Infection Prevention
techniques.subtitle: Prevent wound infections without medical supplies

steps.instruction: Irrigate every wound aggressively with the cleanest water available. Pressure is key — use a squeeze bottle or syringe.
steps.helpDetail: High-pressure irrigation physically removes bacteria, dirt, and debris. A punctured water bottle cap creates adequate pressure. Use at least 500mL per wound.

steps.instruction: Natural antiseptics: diluted raw honey, crushed garlic juice, or sphagnum moss applied to wounds.
steps.helpDetail: Honey has scientifically proven antibacterial properties (hydrogen peroxide production). Sphagnum moss was used in WWI as surgical dressing.

steps.instruction: Keep wounds OPEN in the field — do not close dirty or animal-bite wounds. Pack loosely with clean material.
steps.helpDetail: Open wounds drain bacteria and fluid. Closed infected wounds trap bacteria, creating abscesses. Pack with clean cloth changed twice daily.
steps.illustrationDescription: Keep wounds OPEN in the field

steps.instruction: Change dressings at least twice daily. Inspect for: increasing redness, warmth, swelling, pus, or red streaks.
steps.helpDetail: Red streaks moving away from the wound toward the heart = lymphangitis = blood infection developing. This is a critical emergency.

steps.instruction: Elevate infected limbs above heart level. Gravity reduces swelling and slows pathogen spread.
steps.helpDetail: Elevation combined with immobilization and clean dressings is the triad of field infection management.

techniques.name: Dislocated Shoulder Reduction
techniques.subtitle: Relocate a dislocated shoulder in the field

steps.instruction: Confirm dislocation: the shoulder looks squared off (not rounded), arm is held away from body, intense pain with movement.
steps.helpDetail: Compare to the uninjured shoulder. Dislocations create visible asymmetry. Do NOT confuse with a fracture — if crepitus (grinding), splint instead.

steps.instruction: Cunningham technique (safest): have the patient sit upright. Gently massage the muscles around the shoulder.
steps.helpDetail: Start by massaging the trapezius (top of shoulder), then the deltoid (cap), then the bicep. Use firm, steady pressure for 5-10 minutes.

steps.instruction: While massaging, instruct the patient to slowly shrug their shoulders upward and squeeze their shoulder blades together.
steps.helpDetail: This combination of muscle relaxation (massage) and patient-initiated movement often allows the shoulder to slide back in on its own.

steps.instruction: If the Cunningham technique fails: lay patient face-down with the injured arm hanging over a ledge. Hang a weight (8-10 lbs) from the wrist.
steps.helpDetail: Gravity traction: the weight provides steady, gentle pull while muscles gradually relax. This may take 20-30 minutes. Do not force.

steps.instruction: After reduction: immobilize in a sling. Ice if available. Do NOT use the arm for overhead activities.
steps.helpDetail: Successful reduction produces immediate pain relief and restored shoulder contour. If you can't reduce it, splint in the position found and evacuate.

techniques.name: Active Rewarming Techniques
techniques.subtitle: Warm a hypothermia patient using available heat sources

steps.instruction: Insulate from the ground FIRST — ground conducts heat 25x faster than air.
steps.helpDetail: Multiple layers under the patient are more important than layers on top. Use pads, branches, leaves, clothing — anything between the body and cold ground.

steps.instruction: Warm the core, NOT the extremities. Apply heat to the neck, armpits, and groin.
steps.helpDetail: These are areas where major blood vessels are close to the surface. Warming extremities first drives cold blood to the core, causing dangerous 'afterdrop'.
steps.illustrationDescription: Warm the core, NOT the extremi

steps.instruction: Heat sources: hot water bottles (plastic bottles filled with warm water), heated rocks wrapped in cloth, body-to-body contact.
steps.helpDetail: Warm — not hot. Test on your own skin first. Burns are common on numb hypothermic skin because the patient can't feel the heat.

steps.instruction: Warm, sweet drinks if the patient is conscious and can swallow. NO alcohol.
steps.helpDetail: Warm liquids add heat from inside. Sugar provides energy for the body's own heat production. Alcohol dilates blood vessels and increases heat loss.
steps.illustrationDescription: Warm, sweet drinks if the pati

steps.instruction: Rewarming takes HOURS, not minutes. Monitor continuously. Severe hypothermia patients may appear dead but still be viable.
steps.helpDetail: The saying: 'They're not dead until they're warm and dead.' Cold patients can survive prolonged cardiac arrest. Continue efforts for hours.

techniques.name: Field Spinal Assessment
techniques.subtitle: Determine if spinal immobilization is needed

steps.instruction: Mechanism of injury: fall from height, vehicle accident, diving, or force to head/neck = suspect spine injury.
steps.helpDetail: If the mechanism COULD have caused a spine injury, treat as if it did until you can clear it. Err on the side of caution.

steps.instruction: Ask: 'Do you have any neck or back pain?' If yes — immobilize. Do not move the patient.
steps.helpDetail: Pain along the spine is the most reliable field indicator. Even mild pain warrants immobilization. Log-roll only if necessary.
steps.illustrationDescription: Ask: 'Do you have any neck or

steps.instruction: Test sensation: can they feel you touch their fingers and toes? Compare both sides.
steps.helpDetail: Numbness, tingling, or absent sensation = possible spinal cord involvement. Unequal sensation = possible cord compression.

steps.instruction: Test motor: can they wiggle fingers and toes on command? Can they squeeze your hands equally?
steps.helpDetail: Weakness or inability to move = possible spinal cord injury. Unequal strength = possible cord compression.
steps.illustrationDescription: Test motor: can they wiggle fi

steps.instruction: If alert, no pain, full sensation, full motor, and no intoxication — spine can be cautiously cleared for movement.
steps.helpDetail: All five criteria must be met. ANY failure = maintain immobilization. Package the patient and evacuate.
steps.illustrationDescription: If alert, no pain, full sensat

techniques.name: improvised Hemostatic Agents
techniques.subtitle: Stop bleeding with natural clotting materials

steps.instruction: Yarrow (Achillea millefolium): crush fresh leaves and pack directly into the wound.
steps.helpDetail: Yarrow has been used as a wound herb for thousands of years (named after Achilles). It promotes clotting and has mild antiseptic properties.

steps.instruction: Spider web: collect and pack clean spider webs into minor wounds.
steps.helpDetail: Spider silk has natural clotting properties and is antimicrobial. Historically used by soldiers. Effective for small cuts and abrasions.
steps.illustrationDescription: Spider web: collect and pack c

steps.instruction: Wood ash (clean, white ash from hardwood): pack into wounds as a last resort.
steps.helpDetail: Ash is alkaline and promotes clotting. It also has mild antiseptic properties. Use clean ash from a well-established fire.

steps.instruction: Coffee grounds: unused, dry grounds packed into wounds promote clotting.
steps.helpDetail: Caffeine causes vasoconstriction (blood vessel narrowing), reducing blood flow. The granular texture also mechanically promotes clot formation.

steps.instruction: These are supplements to direct pressure — they do NOT replace it. Maintain firm pressure over any natural agent applied.
steps.helpDetail: No hemostatic agent replaces direct manual pressure as the primary bleeding control method. Apply agents, THEN press, THEN bandage.

techniques.name: Leukotape Blister Moleskin
techniques.subtitle: Advanced blister prevention and treatment

steps.instruction: Clean and dry the affected area completely. Tape will not stick to sweaty or dirty skin.
steps.helpDetail: If a blister has already formed, do not pop it unless it is too painful to walk. If intact, leave it alone.
steps.illustrationDescription: Clean and dry the affected are

steps.instruction: Cut a piece of Leukotape or moleskin larger than the hot spot.
steps.helpDetail: Round the corners of the tape with scissors or your knife to prevent the edges from catching and peeling off.
steps.illustrationDescription: Cut a piece of Leukotape or mo

steps.instruction: For an intact blister: Cut a hole in the center of the moleskin (a 'donut' shape) slightly larger than the blister.
steps.helpDetail: This relieves direct pressure from the blister roof. Place the donut over the blister.
steps.illustrationDescription: For an intact blister: Cut a h

steps.instruction: Cover the entire donut pad with a solid, larger piece of Leukotape.
steps.helpDetail: This secures the protective layer. Smooth it down firmly so it adheres tight to your skin.
steps.illustrationDescription: Cover the entire donut pad wit

steps.instruction: Change your socks.
steps.helpDetail: A wet sock causes extreme friction. Put on a dry pair of wool or synthetic socks immediately.

techniques.name: Improvised Rigid Foam Splint
techniques.subtitle: Mold a sleeping pad into a custom fracture splint

steps.instruction: Cut a section of a closed-cell foam sleeping pad long enough to extend past the joints above and below the fracture.
steps.helpDetail: The pad's rigidity increases when folded or layered.
steps.illustrationDescription: Cut a section of a closed-cell

steps.instruction: Fold the pad lengthways into a U-shape or 'gutter'.
steps.helpDetail: The U-shape provides structural rigidity, preventing the limb from bending. A flat piece of foam is useless.
steps.illustrationDescription: Fold the pad lengthways into a

steps.instruction: Pad the injured limb with spare clothing before applying the foam gutter.
steps.helpDetail: Direct contact with foam can cause sweat buildup and skin breakdown over days. Padding distributes pressure.
steps.illustrationDescription: Pad the injured limb with spar

steps.instruction: Place the limb in the gutter and secure it tightly with cravats, ace bandages, or duct tape.
steps.helpDetail: Tie the bandages snugly but not tight enough to cut off circulation. Check the pulse at the wrist or foot after tying.
steps.illustrationDescription: Place the limb in the gutter a

steps.instruction: Elevate the splinted limb.
steps.helpDetail: Keep it above the heart to reduce agonizing throbbing and dangerous swelling inside the rigid construct.

techniques.name: Stingray Barb Treatment
techniques.subtitle: Neutralize marine venom using extreme heat

steps.instruction: Stingray venom is a heat-labile protein, meaning it breaks down and denatures at high temperatures.
steps.helpDetail: Cold water or ice will increase the pain. You must use heat.

steps.instruction: Immerse the wound in water as hot as you can tolerate (110-115°F / 43-46°C).
steps.helpDetail: Test the water on yourself or a non-injured limb first to ensure you do not cause scald burns. The injured limb may be numb to heat.
steps.illustrationDescription: Immerse the wound in water as

steps.instruction: Soak for 30 to 90 minutes.
steps.helpDetail: The pain should subside significantly within the first 15 minutes as the venom proteins unravel. Keep adding hot water to maintain the temperature.

steps.instruction: Carefully attempt to remove the barb using tweezers or pliers.
steps.helpDetail: Stingray barbs are serrated and break off easily. Pull it straight out in the direction it entered. If it resists, leave it for a surgeon.

steps.instruction: Scrub the wound vigorously with soap and water afterward.
steps.helpDetail: Marine wounds are notorious for causing rapid, aggressive necrotic infections (like Vibrio). Wash it out completely.

techniques.name: Jellyfish Sting Neutralization
techniques.subtitle: Deactivate nematocysts with acid

steps.instruction: Rinse the sting area gently with SEAWATER, not freshwater.
steps.helpDetail: Freshwater changes the osmotic pressure and causes un-fired stingers (nematocysts) to explode, injecting more venom.
steps.illustrationDescription: Rinse the sting area gently wi

steps.instruction: Do NOT rub the area or apply ice.
steps.helpDetail: Friction and cold both trigger remaining stingers.
steps.illustrationDescription: Do NOT rub the area or apply i

steps.instruction: Douse the area heavily with household vinegar (5% acetic acid).
steps.helpDetail: Vinegar chemically deactivates the stingers. Urine is a myth and can actually trigger them due to varying pH levels.
steps.illustrationDescription: Douse the area heavily with ho

steps.instruction: Pluck any visible tentacles off the skin using tweezers or the edge of a credit card.
steps.helpDetail: Never use your bare hands or you will be stung.

steps.instruction: If the patient experiences severe shortness of breath or facial swelling, treat for anaphylaxis immediately.
steps.helpDetail: Some species (like the Box Jellyfish or Irukandji) require immediate emergency evacuation and antivenom.

techniques.name: String-Yank Hook Removal
techniques.subtitle: Remove an embedded barbed fish hook

steps.instruction: Tie a strong piece of fishing line or braided cord in a loop.
steps.helpDetail: The line should be thick enough not to snap, but thin enough to loop over the hook bend.
steps.illustrationDescription: Tie a strong piece of fishing

steps.instruction: Loop the string around the bend of the embedded hook.
steps.helpDetail: Wrap the other end of the string securely around your dominant hand's index finger.
steps.illustrationDescription: Loop the string around the ben

steps.instruction: With your non-dominant hand, press firmly down on the eye (shank) of the hook.
steps.helpDetail: This downward pressure disengages the barb from the tissue inside the wound.

steps.instruction: Align the string parallel to the shank of the hook, directly opposite the direction it went in.
steps.helpDetail: The angle of the pull is absolutely critical. It must back out exactly how it went in.
steps.illustrationDescription: Align the string parallel to t

steps.instruction: On a count of three, give the string a single, fast, violent yank.
steps.helpDetail: Do not hesitate. A slow pull will tear flesh on the barb and hurt immensely. A fast pop removes it cleanly.
steps.illustrationDescription: On a count of three, give the

techniques.name: Acute Mountain Sickness
techniques.subtitle: Identify and stop the progression of AMS

steps.instruction: Recognize AMS: Headache, nausea, extreme fatigue, dizziness, and loss of appetite occurring above 8,000 feet.
steps.helpDetail: A headache combined with any one of the other symptoms is AMS until proven otherwise.

steps.instruction: STOP ASCENDING IMMEDIATELY.
steps.helpDetail: The cardinal rule of altitude illness: Do not go any higher if you have symptoms of AMS.

steps.instruction: Hydrate aggressively and rest.
steps.helpDetail: Altitude causes rapid fluid loss. Drink 1 liter of water and take Ibuprofen for the headache.

steps.instruction: If symptoms do not improve within 24 hours (or if they worsen), you MUST descend.
steps.helpDetail: Going down is the only permanent cure. Descend 1,000 to 3,000 feet.
steps.illustrationDescription: If symptoms do not improve wit

steps.instruction: Never leave someone with AMS alone.
steps.helpDetail: They can rapidly deteriorate into HACE (High Altitude Cerebral Edema), becoming confused and lethargic.
steps.illustrationDescription: Never leave someone with AMS a

techniques.name: HACE Assessment (Ataxia)
techniques.subtitle: Test for lethal brain swelling at altitude

steps.instruction: High Altitude Cerebral Edema (HACE) is lethal swelling of the brain. It kills quickly.
steps.helpDetail: It usually follows untreated AMS. The primary sign is a loss of coordination (ataxia) and severe confusion.

steps.instruction: Perform the Heel-to-Toe walk test (Tandem Gait).
steps.helpDetail: Draw a straight line in the dirt. Ask the patient to walk 10 steps, placing the heel of one foot directly against the toe of the other.

steps.instruction: If they stumble, fall over, or cannot touch heel-to-toe, they are failing the test.
steps.helpDetail: A healthy but tired person can do this. Someone with HACE cannot.
steps.illustrationDescription: If they stumble, fall over, or

steps.instruction: Ask them simple orientation questions.
steps.helpDetail: What is your name? Where are we? What day is it? Confusion is a glaring red flag.

steps.instruction: If ataxia or confusion is present, DESCEND IMMEDIATELY.
steps.helpDetail: This is a true emergency. Do not wait until morning. Drag them down 3,000 feet immediately even if it is night. Treat with Dexamethasone if available.
steps.illustrationDescription: If ataxia or confusion is pres

techniques.name: HAPE Assessment
techniques.subtitle: Test for lethal fluid in the lungs at altitude

steps.instruction: High Altitude Pulmonary Edema (HAPE) is the lungs filling with fluid due to pressure changes.
steps.helpDetail: It can happen with or without AMS. It is the leading cause of death related to altitude.

steps.instruction: Listen for a wet, rattling, or gurgling cough.
steps.helpDetail: This is fluid. They may also cough up frothy, pink-tinged sputum.
steps.illustrationDescription: Listen for a wet, rattling, or

steps.instruction: Check their breathing rate while resting.
steps.helpDetail: Severe shortness of breath even when sitting completely still is a primary indicator. Extreme fatigue with zero exertion.

steps.instruction: Check their lips and nail beds for cyanosis.
steps.helpDetail: A blue or gray tint indicates profound oxygen starvation.
steps.illustrationDescription: Check their lips and nail beds

steps.instruction: DESCEND IMMEDIATELY.
steps.helpDetail: You must descend at least 3,000 feet (1,000 meters) immediately. Exertion worsens HAPE, so carry them if possible. Keep them warm. Treat with Nifedipine if available.

techniques.name: Black Widow Bite Treatment
techniques.subtitle: Neurotoxic venom management

steps.instruction: Identify the bite: Slight pinprick followed by intense, radiating pain.
steps.helpDetail: The widow's venom is neurotoxic. The bite itself may not look bad (two tiny red marks), but the systemic pain is severe.

steps.instruction: Prepare for severe muscle cramps and rigidity.
steps.helpDetail: Within 30-60 minutes, the patient will develop severe abdominal and back spasms (often mistaken for appendicitis).

steps.instruction: Clean the bite site with soap and water, and apply a cool compress.
steps.helpDetail: Do NOT apply a tourniquet or attempt to suction the venom. Death is extremely rare, but the pain is excruciating.
steps.illustrationDescription: Clean the bite site with soap

steps.instruction: Administer pain relief if available, and keep the patient calm.
steps.helpDetail: Elevate the bitten area if possible, and reassure the patient that the spasms, while terrifying, are rarely fatal to healthy adults.

steps.instruction: Evacuate for medical care.
steps.helpDetail: They need antivenom, muscle relaxants, and potent pain medications at a hospital.

techniques.name: Brown Recluse Bite Treatment
techniques.subtitle: Cytotoxic venom management

steps.instruction: Identify the bite: Usually painless initially, developing into a red, swollen target lesion over hours.
steps.helpDetail: Recluse venom is cytotoxic (flesh-destroying). The center of the bite often turns a dark blue or purple, eventually ulcerating.

steps.instruction: Elevate the limb above the heart.
steps.helpDetail: This helps limit swelling and the spread of the venom through the local tissue.
steps.illustrationDescription: Elevate the limb above the hea

steps.instruction: Apply a cool compress or ice pack (10 minutes on, 10 minutes off).
steps.helpDetail: Cold slows the enzymatic destruction of the tissue. Do NOT apply heat; heat accelerates the venom's necrotic action.
steps.illustrationDescription: Apply a cool compress or ice p

steps.instruction: Do not attempt to cut, lance, or suck out the venom.
steps.helpDetail: Cutting the wound will only introduce worse bacterial infections into tissue that is already dying.
steps.illustrationDescription: Do not attempt to cut, lance,

steps.instruction: Monitor for systemic signs and evacuate.
steps.helpDetail: Fever, chills, and rash require immediate evacuation. The ulcer may require surgical debridement later.

techniques.name: Airway Assessment (Chin Lift)
techniques.subtitle: Open an obstructed airway manually

steps.instruction: If a person is unresponsive and not breathing, assume their tongue has fallen back and blocked their airway.
steps.helpDetail: The most common airway obstruction in an unconscious person is their own tongue.
steps.illustrationDescription: If a person is unresponsive an

steps.instruction: Kneel beside their head. Place one hand flat on their forehead and push backward.
steps.helpDetail: This tilts the head back.

steps.instruction: Place the fingers of your other hand under the bony part of their lower jaw near the chin.
steps.helpDetail: Do NOT press deeply into the soft tissue under the chin, which can block the airway further.
steps.illustrationDescription: Place the fingers of your othe

steps.instruction: Lift the jaw upward to bring the chin forward.
steps.helpDetail: This pulls the tongue away from the back of the throat. Look, listen, and feel for breath for 10 seconds.
steps.illustrationDescription: Lift the jaw upward to bring t

steps.instruction: If you suspect a spinal injury, use the Jaw Thrust maneuver instead.
steps.helpDetail: Place your fingers behind the angles of the lower jaw and push it forward without tilting the head back.
steps.illustrationDescription: If you suspect a spinal injury

techniques.name: Improvised Tourniquet
techniques.subtitle: Stopping massive arterial hemorrhage with field materials

steps.instruction: A tourniquet is ONLY for life-threatening arterial bleeding that cannot be controlled by direct pressure.
steps.helpDetail: Bright red, pulsing, spurting blood = arterial. Dark, oozing blood = venous (use pressure, not a tourniquet).
steps.illustrationDescription: A tourniquet is ONLY for life-

steps.instruction: Use a 2-inch-wide strip of material (belt, shirt strip, bandana). Never use narrow cords or wire.
steps.helpDetail: Narrow material concentrates force and will slice through skin and damage nerves.

steps.instruction: Place the tourniquet 2 to 3 inches above the wound (between the wound and the heart).
steps.helpDetail: Never place it directly on a joint (elbow or knee).

steps.instruction: Tie a half-knot, place a sturdy stick on top, tie a full knot over the stick.
steps.helpDetail: Twist the stick (windlass) until the bleeding STOPS COMPLETELY. Not slows. Stops.
steps.illustrationDescription: Tie a half-knot, place a sturd

steps.instruction: Secure the windlass and write the time of application on the patient's forehead.
steps.helpDetail: Do NOT loosen the tourniquet. Once applied, only a surgeon removes it. Tissue death occurs after 6 hours.

techniques.name: Sucking Chest Wound Seal
techniques.subtitle: Sealing a penetrating chest injury to prevent lung collapse

steps.instruction: Identify a sucking chest wound: a hole in the chest wall that makes a wet, sucking sound when the patient breathes.
steps.helpDetail: Air is entering the chest cavity through the hole instead of the trachea, collapsing the lung.

steps.instruction: Have the patient exhale forcefully.
steps.helpDetail: This pushes air OUT of the chest cavity. Seal the wound at the moment of maximum exhalation.

steps.instruction: Cover the wound with any airtight material: plastic wrap, a credit card, a Ziploc bag, duct tape, or a gloved hand.
steps.helpDetail: The seal must be completely airtight on 3 sides.
steps.illustrationDescription: Cover the wound with any airti

steps.instruction: Tape the seal on only THREE sides, leaving the bottom edge open.
steps.helpDetail: This creates a flutter valve: air can escape out the bottom when the patient exhales, but the seal prevents air from being sucked in during inhalation.
steps.illustrationDescription: Tape the seal on only THREE si

steps.instruction: Monitor for tension pneumothorax: increasing difficulty breathing, neck veins bulging, trachea shifting to one side.
steps.helpDetail: If tension pneumothorax develops, briefly lift the seal to release trapped air, then reseal.

techniques.name: Shoulder Reduction (Field)
techniques.subtitle: Relocating a dislocated shoulder using the Cunningham technique

steps.instruction: Confirm anterior shoulder dislocation: the arm is held away from the body, the shoulder looks squared off, and the patient cannot rotate the arm inward.
steps.helpDetail: Do not attempt this if you suspect a fracture.

steps.instruction: Have the patient sit upright in a chair, leaning slightly forward.
steps.helpDetail: Time and muscle relaxation are your primary tools. Do NOT yank the arm.
steps.illustrationDescription: Have the patient sit upright i

steps.instruction: Have the patient shrug their shoulders upward and backward, pinching the shoulder blades together.
steps.helpDetail: Coach them through deep breathing to relax the spasming muscles.
steps.illustrationDescription: Have the patient shrug their s

steps.instruction: Gently massage the bicep, deltoid, and trapezius muscles to release the spasm.
steps.helpDetail: As the muscles relax, the humeral head will often slide back into the socket on its own with an audible clunk.

steps.instruction: Once reduced, immobilize the arm in a sling against the body.
steps.helpDetail: The patient must not use the arm for navigation or climbing. Evacuate for X-ray to rule out an associated fracture.

techniques.name: Field Dental Emergency
techniques.subtitle: Managing knocked-out teeth, broken fillings, and tooth abscesses

steps.instruction: Knocked-out tooth: Pick it up by the CROWN (the white part), never the root.
steps.helpDetail: Rinse it gently with clean water. Do not scrub it.

steps.instruction: Attempt to reimplant the tooth into the socket immediately.
steps.helpDetail: Push it firmly back in and bite down on a cloth to hold it in place. Speed is critical: after 30 minutes, reimplantation success drops dramatically.

steps.instruction: If you cannot reimplant it, store the tooth in milk, saliva, or held inside the cheek.
steps.helpDetail: The root cells must stay alive. Do NOT let the tooth dry out or store it in water.
steps.illustrationDescription: If you cannot reimplant it, st

steps.instruction: Lost filling or cracked tooth: Pack the cavity with sugar-free gum, dental wax, or candle wax.
steps.helpDetail: This prevents air and food from contacting the exposed nerve, which causes excruciating pain.

steps.instruction: Tooth abscess: Rinse with warm salt water. Take anti-inflammatory medication.
steps.helpDetail: An abscess is a serious infection that can spread to the jaw bone and bloodstream (sepsis). Evacuate for antibiotics.

techniques.name: Severe Nosebleed Control
techniques.subtitle: Stopping epistaxis in the field

steps.instruction: Sit upright and lean FORWARD.
steps.helpDetail: Leaning backward causes blood to drain down the throat, triggering nausea and vomiting, and obscures how much blood you are actually losing.

steps.instruction: Pinch the soft part of the nose firmly closed using your thumb and index finger.
steps.helpDetail: Pinch below the bony bridge. Hold constant pressure for a full 15 minutes WITHOUT checking.
steps.illustrationDescription: Pinch the soft part of the nos

steps.instruction: Breathe through your mouth.
