steps.helpDetail: Spit out any blood that drains into the mouth. Do not swallow it.

steps.instruction: After 15 minutes, gently release.
steps.helpDetail: If the bleeding has stopped, do not blow your nose, pick at it, or sniff forcefully for 24 hours.

steps.instruction: If bleeding does not stop after 20 minutes of firm pressure, pack the nostril with a rolled gauze strip.
steps.helpDetail: Leave 1 inch of gauze protruding for removal. Evacuate if packing fails.
steps.illustrationDescription: If bleeding does not stop afte

techniques.name: Field Hygiene Protocol
techniques.subtitle: Preventing disease through basic sanitation in the wild

steps.instruction: Wash your hands before eating and after defecating.
steps.helpDetail: Use soap and water, wood ash lye, or sand scrubbing. The #1 killer in long-term survival is not starvation — it is diarrheal infection from fecal contamination.

steps.instruction: Designate a latrine area 200+ feet downhill and downstream from camp and water sources.
steps.helpDetail: Dig a cat hole 6–8 inches deep. Cover completely after each use.

steps.instruction: Change socks daily or air-dry them.
steps.helpDetail: Trench foot begins within 48 hours of constant moisture and can lead to gangrene and amputation.

steps.instruction: Inspect your body daily for ticks, leeches, and developing skin infections.
steps.helpDetail: Small infections in the field become life-threatening rapidly without antibiotics.
steps.illustrationDescription: Inspect your body daily for ti

steps.instruction: Boil eating utensils and cooking surfaces.
steps.helpDetail: Even a quick dip in boiling water kills bacteria on shared cups and spoons.

techniques.name: Deep Trench Latrine
techniques.subtitle: Building a proper long-term latrine for multi-day camps

steps.instruction: Dig a trench 4 feet long, 1 foot wide, and 2 feet deep.
steps.helpDetail: Place it at least 200 feet from camp and water.
steps.illustrationDescription: Dig a trench 4 feet long, 1 fo

steps.instruction: Pile the excavated dirt beside the trench with a scoop tool.
steps.helpDetail: After each use, throw a layer of dirt over the waste to contain odor and flies.

steps.instruction: Lay two log rails across the trench for footing.
steps.helpDetail: Squat on the rails. This prevents the trench walls from collapsing under your weight.
steps.illustrationDescription: Lay two log rails across the t

steps.instruction: Sprinkle wood ash into the trench periodically.
steps.helpDetail: Ash raises the alkalinity, killing bacteria and reducing the stench.
steps.illustrationDescription: Sprinkle wood ash into the tre

steps.instruction: When the trench is within 1 foot of full, bury it completely and dig a new one.
steps.helpDetail: Mark old latrines clearly so they are not accidentally reopened.
steps.illustrationDescription: When the trench is within 1 fo

techniques.name: Natural Insect Repellent
techniques.subtitle: Plants and techniques that deter mosquitoes and ticks

steps.instruction: Crush wild basil, citronella grass, or lemon balm leaves and rub them directly on exposed skin.
steps.helpDetail: These plants contain natural volatile oils (citronellal, geraniol) that repel mosquitoes.

steps.instruction: Burn green leaves of these plants on your campfire.
steps.helpDetail: The thick smoke drives insects away from the immediate campsite area.
steps.illustrationDescription: Burn green leaves of these pla

steps.instruction: Apply mud thickly to exposed skin.
steps.helpDetail: A thick mud layer creates a physical barrier that biting insects cannot penetrate. It also provides sun protection.
steps.illustrationDescription: Apply mud thickly to exposed s

steps.instruction: Tuck pants into socks and sleeves into gloves.
steps.helpDetail: Physical barriers are the most reliable insect defense. Tape gaps if available.
steps.illustrationDescription: Tuck pants into socks and slee

steps.instruction: Set up camp on high, breezy ground away from standing water.
steps.helpDetail: Mosquitoes breed in stagnant water. Wind above 5 mph prevents them from flying effectively.
steps.illustrationDescription: Set up camp on high, breezy gr

techniques.name: Hands-Only CPR
techniques.subtitle: Compression-only resuscitation for cardiac arrest

steps.instruction: Confirm the patient is unresponsive and not breathing normally.
steps.helpDetail: Shake their shoulders firmly and shout. Check for breathing for no more than 10 seconds.

steps.instruction: Place the heel of one hand on the center of the chest, between the nipples.
steps.helpDetail: Interlock your other hand on top.
steps.illustrationDescription: Place the heel of one hand on

steps.instruction: Compress hard and fast, at least 2 inches deep, at 100–120 compressions per minute.
steps.helpDetail: Think of the beat of 'Stayin' Alive' by the Bee Gees.
steps.illustrationDescription: Compress hard and fast, at lea

steps.instruction: Do NOT stop for any reason.
steps.helpDetail: Brain death begins at 4 minutes without blood flow. Continuous compressions buys time.
steps.illustrationDescription: Do NOT stop for any reason.

steps.instruction: Continue until emergency services arrive or the patient shows signs of life.
steps.helpDetail: Fatigue degrades your compressions. Switch with another rescuer every 2 minutes if possible.

techniques.name: Choking (Heimlich Maneuver)
techniques.subtitle: Abdominal thrusts for a conscious choking victim

steps.instruction: Ask 'Are you choking?' If they cannot speak, cough, or breathe, they have a complete obstruction.
steps.helpDetail: If they can cough forcefully, encourage coughing. Do not intervene on a partial obstruction.

steps.instruction: Stand behind the victim. Wrap your arms around their waist.
steps.helpDetail: Make a fist with one hand and place the thumb side against the abdomen, above the navel and below the breastbone.

steps.instruction: Grasp your fist with the other hand. Perform firm, quick, inward-and-upward thrusts.
steps.helpDetail: Each thrust should be a distinct, forceful J-shaped motion, not a squeeze.
steps.illustrationDescription: Grasp your fist with the other

steps.instruction: Repeat until the object is expelled or the victim becomes unconscious.
steps.helpDetail: If they go unconscious, lower them to the ground and begin CPR.
steps.illustrationDescription: Repeat until the object is exp

steps.instruction: For infants: Use back blows and chest thrusts, NOT abdominal thrusts.
steps.helpDetail: Abdominal thrusts can rupture an infant's liver.
steps.illustrationDescription: For infants: Use back blows an

techniques.name: Mild Allergic Reaction Management
techniques.subtitle: Treating itching, hives, and swelling from stings or plants

steps.instruction: Administer an oral antihistamine (Benadryl/diphenhydramine) if available.
steps.helpDetail: Standard adult dose is 25–50mg.

steps.instruction: Apply a cold compress to swollen or itchy areas.
steps.helpDetail: Cold constricts blood vessels, reducing swelling and histamine release.
steps.illustrationDescription: Apply a cold compress to swoll

steps.instruction: Remove the stinger if visible.
steps.helpDetail: Scrape it sideways with a credit card edge. Do NOT squeeze with tweezers, as this injects more venom.

steps.instruction: Apply hydrocortisone cream or a paste of baking soda and water.
steps.helpDetail: This reduces localized inflammation.

steps.instruction: Monitor for 30 minutes for signs of anaphylaxis.
steps.helpDetail: If breathing difficulty, facial swelling, or dizziness develop, this is escalating to anaphylaxis.

techniques.name: Butterfly Closure Strips
techniques.subtitle: Closing a laceration without sutures

steps.instruction: Clean the wound thoroughly with clean water under pressure.
steps.helpDetail: Irrigate aggressively. The most important factor in wound healing is cleanliness, not closure.

steps.instruction: Dry the skin on BOTH sides of the wound completely.
steps.helpDetail: Adhesive strips will not stick to wet or bloody skin.
steps.illustrationDescription: Dry the skin on BOTH sides of

steps.instruction: Apply tincture of benzoin to the skin on both sides of the wound.
steps.helpDetail: This dramatically increases the stickiness of the adhesive strips. It stings intensely.
steps.illustrationDescription: Apply tincture of benzoin to t

steps.instruction: Apply butterfly strips perpendicular to the wound, pulling the edges together firmly.
steps.helpDetail: Space them 1/4 inch apart. The wound edges should touch but not overlap.

steps.instruction: Cover with a sterile gauze pad and tape.
steps.helpDetail: Check wound daily for infection signs: redness, warmth, swelling, pus, red streaks radiating outward.
steps.illustrationDescription: Cover with a sterile gauze pad

techniques.name: Leech Removal Protocol
techniques.subtitle: Safe removal of leeches without leaving mouthparts embedded

steps.instruction: Do NOT yank, burn, or salt the leech while it is attached.
steps.helpDetail: These cause the leech to regurgitate stomach contents into the wound, introducing bacteria.
steps.illustrationDescription: Do NOT yank, burn, or salt the

steps.instruction: Slide a flat edge (fingernail, credit card) under the oral sucker (thin end).
steps.helpDetail: Break the seal gently, then flick the leech away.

steps.instruction: The wound will bleed freely for some time because leech saliva contains an anticoagulant.
steps.helpDetail: This is normal. Let it bleed for 1-2 minutes to flush the wound.
steps.illustrationDescription: The wound will bleed freely fo

steps.instruction: Clean the wound with antiseptic or clean water.
steps.helpDetail: Apply pressure if bleeding is excessive.

steps.instruction: Prevent leeches by tucking pants into socks and applying insect repellent to boot tops.
steps.helpDetail: Check your entire body twice daily in leech country, especially groin, armpits, and behind ears.

techniques.name: Snow Blindness Treatment
techniques.subtitle: Managing ultraviolet keratitis from sun glare on snow

steps.instruction: Snow blindness is a sunburn of the cornea caused by UV reflection off snow, water, or sand.
steps.helpDetail: Symptoms: burning, gritty feeling, tearing, swelling, temporary vision loss. Onset 6-12 hours after exposure.
steps.illustrationDescription: Snow blindness is a sunburn of

steps.instruction: Move the patient into shade or a dark shelter.
steps.helpDetail: Further UV exposure worsens the damage.
steps.illustrationDescription: Move the patient into shade or

steps.instruction: Apply cold compresses over closed eyes.
steps.helpDetail: Do NOT rub the eyes. The corneal surface is raw and rubbing tears it further.

steps.instruction: Administer pain medication if available.
steps.helpDetail: This condition is extremely painful. Ibuprofen reduces both pain and inflammation.

steps.instruction: Prevention: Wear sunglasses or improvised slit-goggles made from bark, cardboard, or duct tape.
steps.helpDetail: Cut narrow horizontal slits to allow just enough vision. This blocks 90%+ of UV.

techniques.name: Frostbite Rewarming Protocol
techniques.subtitle: Field rewarming of frozen tissue

steps.instruction: Do NOT rewarm frostbitten tissue if there is ANY chance it will refreeze.
steps.helpDetail: Thaw-refreeze cycles destroy tissue far more severely than leaving it frozen.

steps.instruction: Immerse the affected area in water heated to 98-102°F (37-39°C).
steps.helpDetail: Test the water with YOUR elbow, not the patient's numb hand. Too hot causes burns on insensate tissue.
steps.illustrationDescription: Immerse the affected area in w

steps.instruction: Keep the water temperature constant for 30-60 minutes.
steps.helpDetail: Constantly add warm water as it cools. The rewarming is complete when the tissue turns red/purple and becomes soft.

steps.instruction: EXTREME PAIN accompanies successful rewarming.
steps.helpDetail: Administer the strongest pain medication available. This is one of the most painful medical procedures.

steps.instruction: After rewarming, place sterile gauze between fingers/toes, wrap loosely, and elevate.
steps.helpDetail: Do NOT pop blisters. Do NOT allow the patient to walk on rewarmed feet. Evacuate immediately.

techniques.name: ABCDE Primary Survey
techniques.subtitle: Rapid systematic assessment of a critically injured patient

steps.instruction: A — Airway: Is the airway open? Can the patient speak?
steps.helpDetail: If obstructed: jaw thrust, head tilt chin lift, clear visible obstructions.
steps.illustrationDescription: A — Airway: Is the airway open

steps.instruction: B — Breathing: Is the chest rising? Can you hear breath sounds on both sides?
steps.helpDetail: If not breathing: begin rescue breathing. If unequal: suspect pneumothorax.
steps.illustrationDescription: B — Breathing: Is the chest ri

steps.instruction: C — Circulation: Is there a pulse? Is there major bleeding?
steps.helpDetail: Control hemorrhage with direct pressure or tourniquet. Check capillary refill (should be <2 seconds).
steps.illustrationDescription: C — Circulation: Is there a pu

steps.instruction: D — Disability: What is the level of consciousness? AVPU: Alert, Voice, Pain, Unresponsive.
steps.helpDetail: Check pupil size and reactivity. Unequal pupils = brain injury.
steps.illustrationDescription: D — Disability: What is the le

steps.instruction: E — Exposure: Expose the patient and check for hidden injuries. Then protect from hypothermia.
steps.helpDetail: Cut away clothing to find wounds you can't see. Then immediately cover with blankets.

techniques.name: Secondary Survey (Head-to-Toe)
techniques.subtitle: Systematic physical examination after primary survey is clear

steps.instruction: Run your hands along the scalp feeling for depressions, blood, or swelling.
steps.helpDetail: Check behind the ears for Battle's sign (bruising = basilar skull fracture).
steps.illustrationDescription: Run your hands along the scalp

steps.instruction: Palpate the cervical spine, collarbones, shoulders, and each arm.
steps.helpDetail: Ask 'Does this hurt?' at each point. Check grip strength in both hands.

steps.instruction: Palpate the chest wall, pressing each rib.
steps.helpDetail: Pain on compression suggests rib fracture. Check for paradoxical movement (flail chest).

steps.instruction: Press on all 4 quadrants of the abdomen.
steps.helpDetail: Rigid, board-like abdomen = internal bleeding. Guarding (tensing when you press) = peritonitis.
steps.illustrationDescription: Press on all 4 quadrants of th

steps.instruction: Check pelvis stability by pressing inward on the hip bones ONCE.
steps.helpDetail: If unstable, do NOT repeat. Pelvic fractures cause massive internal hemorrhage. Bind with a belt or shirt.

techniques.name: Shock Treatment Protocol
techniques.subtitle: Managing circulatory shock in the field

steps.instruction: Recognize shock: pale/ashen skin, rapid weak pulse, rapid shallow breathing, confusion, anxiety.
steps.helpDetail: Shock = circulatory failure. The vital organs are not getting enough blood.

steps.instruction: Control the cause: stop bleeding, splint fractures, treat allergic reactions.
steps.helpDetail: Shock has a cause. Treat the cause or shock will progress to death.

steps.instruction: Lay the patient flat and elevate legs 12 inches.
steps.helpDetail: This moves blood from the legs back to the vital organs. Exception: do NOT elevate legs with head/chest/spinal injuries.
steps.illustrationDescription: Lay the patient flat and eleva

steps.instruction: Prevent heat loss aggressively.
steps.helpDetail: Shock patients lose body heat rapidly. Insulate from the ground and cover with blankets.

steps.instruction: Give small sips of water if the patient is conscious and not vomiting.
steps.helpDetail: Do NOT give food. Maintain body temperature and monitor vital signs. Evacuate immediately.
steps.illustrationDescription: Give small sips of water if th

techniques.name: Cold Water Immersion Response
techniques.subtitle: Surviving sudden immersion in cold water

steps.instruction: Cold shock response: The first 1-3 minutes are the most dangerous.
steps.helpDetail: The gasp reflex and hyperventilation can cause you to inhale water. Fight the urge. Control your breathing.

steps.instruction: Do NOT try to swim immediately.
steps.helpDetail: Spend the first minute floating and controlling your breathing. Cold water causes uncontrollable hyperventilation.
steps.illustrationDescription: Do NOT try to swim immediately

steps.instruction: Swim failure occurs after 3-30 minutes.
steps.helpDetail: Cold muscles lose the ability to coordinate swimming. Get to something floatable or climbable FAST.

steps.instruction: Hypothermia sets in after 30+ minutes.
steps.helpDetail: Adopt the HELP position (Heat Escape Lessening Posture): knees to chest, arms crossed over chest, remain still.

steps.instruction: If in a group, huddle together.
steps.helpDetail: Bodies in contact share heat. Children go in the center.
steps.illustrationDescription: If in a group, huddle together

techniques.name: Scorpion Sting Treatment
techniques.subtitle: Field management of scorpion envenomation

steps.instruction: Most scorpion stings cause only localized pain similar to a bee sting.
steps.helpDetail: The exception: the Arizona Bark Scorpion (Centruroides sculpturatus) can cause systemic symptoms.

steps.instruction: Clean the sting site with soap and water.
steps.helpDetail: Apply a cold compress to reduce swelling and slow venom absorption.
steps.illustrationDescription: Clean the sting site with soap

steps.instruction: Immobilize the affected limb.
steps.helpDetail: Movement accelerates venom distribution.

steps.instruction: Monitor for severe symptoms: muscle twitching, difficulty breathing, excessive salivation, blurred vision.
steps.helpDetail: These indicate dangerous envenomation requiring immediate evacuation.

steps.instruction: Administer pain medication and antihistamines if available.
steps.helpDetail: Most adults recover from scorpion stings within 24-48 hours without treatment. Children are at much higher risk.

techniques.name: Centipede Bite Treatment
techniques.subtitle: Managing the painful envenomation from large centipedes

steps.instruction: Centipede bites cause intense, localized pain, swelling, and redness.
steps.helpDetail: The venom is rarely dangerous to adults but the pain can be excruciating.

steps.instruction: Clean the wound thoroughly.
steps.helpDetail: Centipede fangs can introduce bacteria.

steps.instruction: Apply hot water (not scalding) to the bite area.
steps.helpDetail: Heat denatures the venom proteins and provides significant pain relief. Test temperature on unaffected skin first.

steps.instruction: Take anti-inflammatory medication.
steps.helpDetail: Ibuprofen reduces both pain and swelling effectively.

steps.instruction: Watch for secondary infection over the next 48 hours.
steps.helpDetail: Redness spreading beyond the initial bite area, red streaks, or increasing warmth indicate infection requiring antibiotics.

techniques.name: Cone Snail Envenomation
techniques.subtitle: Emergency treatment for cone snail stings

steps.instruction: Cone snails fire a hollow, harpoon-like tooth that injects conotoxin.
steps.helpDetail: There is NO antivenom. Some species can kill a human in minutes.
steps.illustrationDescription: Cone snails fire a hollow, har

steps.instruction: Apply a pressure immobilization bandage immediately.
steps.helpDetail: Wrap firmly from the sting site toward the heart, similar to a snakebite bandage.

steps.instruction: Immobilize the affected limb completely.
steps.helpDetail: Any movement accelerates venom distribution.

steps.instruction: Monitor breathing constantly.
steps.helpDetail: Conotoxin causes progressive paralysis including respiratory muscles. Be prepared to begin rescue breathing.

steps.instruction: Evacuate IMMEDIATELY.
steps.helpDetail: If breathing stops, continuous rescue breathing can keep the victim alive until venom wears off (12-24 hours). Do not give up.

techniques.name: Blue-Ring Octopus Bite
techniques.subtitle: Emergency response to tetrodotoxin envenomation

steps.instruction: The blue-ringed octopus injects tetrodotoxin (TTX) — the same poison as pufferfish.
steps.helpDetail: TTX causes progressive, total paralysis including respiratory muscles. The victim remains conscious.

steps.instruction: There is NO antivenom.
steps.helpDetail: Survival depends entirely on maintaining artificial ventilation until the toxin wears off (12-24 hours).

steps.instruction: Apply pressure immobilization bandage to the bite site.
steps.helpDetail: Wrap firmly. Immobilize the limb.

steps.instruction: Begin rescue breathing as soon as the patient shows any difficulty breathing.
steps.helpDetail: The patient may be fully conscious but unable to move or breathe. Talk to them constantly.

steps.instruction: Continuous rescue breathing for 12-24 hours can result in full recovery.
steps.helpDetail: Do NOT assume the patient is dead if they stop breathing. Maintain ventilation and evacuate to a hospital with a mechanical ventilator.

techniques.name: Sea Urchin Spine Removal
techniques.subtitle: Extracting embedded spines from hands and feet

steps.instruction: Soak the affected area in the hottest water tolerable (110-115°F) for 30-60 minutes.
steps.helpDetail: Heat denatures venom proteins and significantly reduces pain.
steps.illustrationDescription: Soak the affected area in the

steps.instruction: Remove visible spines with tweezers.
steps.helpDetail: Pull slowly and steadily in the direction of entry. Spines are brittle and snap easily.

steps.instruction: Deeply embedded spines that do not protrude may need to be left in place.
steps.helpDetail: The body will calcify around small spine fragments and eventually absorb or expel them over weeks.

steps.instruction: Apply vinegar to dissolve remaining tiny spine tips.
steps.helpDetail: Soak or apply vinegar-soaked gauze for 30 minutes.

steps.instruction: Monitor for infection.
steps.helpDetail: Redness, swelling, warmth, and pus indicate infection. Clean daily and apply antibiotic ointment if available.

techniques.name: Baseline Vital Signs
techniques.subtitle: Taking and recording vital signs in the field

steps.instruction: Heart rate: Count the pulse at the wrist (radial artery) for 15 seconds, multiply by 4.
steps.helpDetail: Normal adult range: 60-100 bpm. Above 120 at rest = abnormal.
steps.illustrationDescription: Heart rate: Count the pulse at

steps.instruction: Respiratory rate: Count breaths for 30 seconds (without telling the patient).
steps.helpDetail: Normal: 12-20 breaths/min. People breathe differently when they know you're counting.

steps.instruction: Skin signs: Check Color, Temperature, and Moisture (CTM).
steps.helpDetail: Pink/warm/dry = normal. Pale/cool/clammy = shock. Hot/red/dry = heat stroke.

steps.instruction: Level of consciousness: AVPU — Alert, responds to Voice, responds to Pain, Unresponsive.
steps.helpDetail: Record the HIGHEST level of response.

steps.instruction: Record vitals every 15 minutes and note the TREND.
steps.helpDetail: A single reading means nothing. The trend (improving or deteriorating) drives all treatment decisions.

techniques.name: Spinal Log Roll
techniques.subtitle: Safely rolling a supine patient for examination or transfer

steps.instruction: The log roll requires 3 people minimum: head holder + 2 body controllers.
steps.helpDetail: One additional person to slide a backboard or pad underneath is ideal.

steps.instruction: The head holder is in charge.
steps.helpDetail: They control the entire movement with verbal commands. Hands cradle the head with fingers along the jawline.
steps.illustrationDescription: The head holder is in charge.

steps.instruction: Body controllers kneel beside the patient: one at shoulders/hips, one at hips/legs.
steps.helpDetail: Reach across the patient and grip at the shoulder, hip, and knee.

steps.instruction: On command, roll the patient toward the controllers in ONE smooth motion.
steps.helpDetail: The spine must remain perfectly aligned. The body rotates as a single unit — no twisting.
steps.illustrationDescription: On command, roll the patient t

steps.instruction: Slide the backboard or pad under the patient, then roll them back.
steps.helpDetail: The head holder maintains cervical alignment throughout and gives ALL commands.
steps.illustrationDescription: Slide the backboard or pad und

techniques.name: Improvised Cervical Collar
techniques.subtitle: Building a neck stabilizer from field materials

steps.instruction: A cervical collar prevents flexion, extension, and rotation of the neck.
steps.helpDetail: It is NOT a guarantee of spinal protection; it is an aid.

steps.instruction: Use an SAM splint, rolled newspaper, or a folded towel wrapped inside a triangular bandage.
steps.helpDetail: The splint should be shaped into a curve that cups under the chin and rests on the collarbones.
steps.illustrationDescription: Use an SAM splint, rolled news

steps.instruction: The collar must be tall enough to prevent the patient from looking down.
steps.helpDetail: If too short, it provides no stabilization.
steps.illustrationDescription: The collar must be tall enough

steps.instruction: It must NOT compress the trachea or jugular veins.
steps.helpDetail: If the patient's breathing worsens after collar application, loosen or remove immediately.
steps.illustrationDescription: It must NOT compress the trach

steps.instruction: A collar alone does NOT clear the spine.
steps.helpDetail: Manual in-line stabilization (a person holding the head) must continue until definitive medical clearance.
steps.illustrationDescription: A collar alone does NOT clear

techniques.name: Willow Bark Tea (Natural Aspirin)
techniques.subtitle: Extracting salicin from willow bark for pain and fever

steps.instruction: Willow bark contains salicin, which the body converts to salicylic acid (aspirin).
steps.helpDetail: White willow, weeping willow, and crack willow all work. Also: aspen and poplar bark.

steps.instruction: Peel the inner bark (cambium) from young branches.
steps.helpDetail: The bark should be moist, pliable, and slightly sweet-smelling.

steps.instruction: Simmer 1-2 teaspoons of shredded inner bark in a cup of water for 10-15 minutes.
steps.helpDetail: Do not boil vigorously — high heat degrades the salicin.

steps.instruction: Strain and drink.
steps.helpDetail: Taste is bitter and astringent. Add honey or pine needle tea to improve flavor.

steps.instruction: Effective for headache, muscle pain, fever, and inflammation.
steps.helpDetail: Do NOT give to children (Reye's syndrome risk), people with aspirin allergies, or anyone with bleeding disorders.

techniques.name: Plantain Leaf Poultice
techniques.subtitle: Using broadleaf plantain for wound care and insect stings

steps.instruction: Broadleaf plantain (Plantago major) grows in lawns, paths, and disturbed soil worldwide.
steps.helpDetail: It has wide, oval leaves with prominent parallel veins running lengthwise.

steps.instruction: Chew or crush several fresh leaves into a green paste.
steps.helpDetail: Chewing releases the active compounds (aucubin, allantoin) most effectively.
steps.illustrationDescription: Chew or crush several fresh le

steps.instruction: Apply the paste directly to insect stings, minor cuts, or rashes.
steps.helpDetail: Cover with a clean leaf and secure with a bandage or cord.
steps.illustrationDescription: Apply the paste directly to in

steps.instruction: The plantain draws out toxins, reduces inflammation, and promotes wound healing.
steps.helpDetail: Replace the poultice every 2-4 hours with fresh leaves.

steps.instruction: Plantain also soothes poison ivy rash when applied immediately after contact.
steps.helpDetail: It is one of the most versatile and widely available medicinal plants on earth.

techniques.name: Activated Charcoal for Poisoning
techniques.subtitle: Using charcoal to absorb ingested toxins

steps.instruction: Activated charcoal adsorbs many poisons and prevents them from entering the bloodstream.
steps.helpDetail: It is NOT the same as BBQ charcoal. Field-made charcoal works but is less effective.

steps.instruction: Grind hardwood charcoal into the finest possible powder.
steps.helpDetail: The finer the powder, the greater the surface area for adsorption.

steps.instruction: Mix 2-3 tablespoons of charcoal powder into a cup of water.
steps.helpDetail: It will be gritty and black. Drink it all.

steps.instruction: Most effective within 1 hour of ingestion.
steps.helpDetail: After 2 hours, most poison has already been absorbed from the stomach.

steps.instruction: Charcoal does NOT work against: alcohols, cyanide, heavy metals, or caustic acids/bases.
steps.helpDetail: It is most effective against plant alkaloids, medication overdoses, and organic poisons. Always evacuate poisoning patients.

techniques.name: Honey Wound Dressing
techniques.subtitle: Using raw honey as an antimicrobial wound dressing

steps.instruction: Raw, unprocessed honey has potent antibacterial properties.
steps.helpDetail: Its high sugar concentration draws moisture from bacteria via osmosis, killing them.

steps.instruction: Clean the wound thoroughly before application.
steps.helpDetail: Remove all debris and irrigate with clean water.

steps.instruction: Apply a thick layer of honey directly to the wound surface.
steps.helpDetail: Fill the entire wound bed. The honey should be in contact with all wound surfaces.
steps.illustrationDescription: Apply a thick layer of honey d

steps.instruction: Cover with a clean bandage.
steps.helpDetail: The honey will liquefy from body heat and may seep through thin bandages. Use thick gauze.

steps.instruction: Change the dressing every 24 hours.
steps.helpDetail: Honey dressings are clinically proven to promote faster healing, reduce infection rates, and reduce scarring. Manuka honey is the gold standard, but any raw honey works in the field.

techniques.name: Garlic as Field Antibiotic
techniques.subtitle: Using raw garlic for antimicrobial treatment

steps.instruction: Raw garlic contains allicin, a potent antimicrobial compound.
steps.helpDetail: Allicin is activated when garlic cells are crushed or chopped.

steps.instruction: Crush a fresh garlic clove and let it sit for 10 minutes.
steps.helpDetail: This waiting period allows the enzyme alliinase to convert alliin into allicin.
steps.illustrationDescription: Crush a fresh garlic clove and

steps.instruction: For wound treatment: Apply crushed garlic to the wound periphery, NOT directly into the wound.
steps.helpDetail: Direct application to open wounds causes tissue burn. Apply around the edges.

steps.instruction: For internal infection: Eat 2-3 raw, crushed cloves per day.
steps.helpDetail: Swallow small pieces with water like pills if the taste is unbearable.

steps.instruction: Wild garlic grows across North America and Eurasia.
steps.helpDetail: Verify by smell — if it smells strongly of garlic, it IS garlic. If it doesn't smell, do NOT eat it (possible Death Camas).

techniques.name: Advanced Airway: Jaw Thrust
techniques.subtitle: Open airway without spinal movement

steps.instruction: Kneel at the victim's head.
steps.helpDetail: Ensure you are on a stable surface above the victim's head.

steps.instruction: Place palms on cheekbones.
steps.helpDetail: Rest your elbows on the ground or your knees for stability.

steps.instruction: Hook fingers under the jaw angle.
steps.helpDetail: Find the bony angle of the lower jaw just below the ears.

steps.instruction: Lift the jaw upward.
steps.helpDetail: Push the jaw up and forward to pull the tongue away from the back of the throat without tilting the neck.

techniques.name: Manage Tension Pneumothorax
techniques.subtitle: Burping a vented chest seal

steps.instruction: Monitor for worsening breathing.
steps.helpDetail: If breathing worsens significantly after a chest seal is applied, air is trapped.

steps.instruction: Locate the chest seal edge.
steps.helpDetail: Find the edge of the dressing covering the penetrating wound.

steps.instruction: Lift one edge upon exhalation.
steps.helpDetail: Peel back one corner temporarily to allow trapped pressurized air or blood to escape ('burping').

steps.instruction: Reseal the dressing.
steps.helpDetail: Wipe away excess blood and press the seal back down firmly to prevent new air from entering.

techniques.name: Improvised Pelvic Binder
techniques.subtitle: Stabilize a shattered pelvis

steps.instruction: Identify pelvic fracture.
steps.helpDetail: Look for trauma mechanism (fall, crush), asymmetry, or pain upon gentle pressure.

steps.instruction: Prepare a broad sheet.
steps.helpDetail: Use a bedsheet, jacket, or commercial binder. Do not use thin ropes or belts.

steps.instruction: Slide under the greater trochanters.
steps.helpDetail: The binder must be placed low, over the bony bumps of the upper femur, NOT the soft stomach.

steps.instruction: Pull tight and cross over.
steps.helpDetail: Cross the ends tight to internally rotate the toes and compress the pelvic ring.

steps.instruction: Tie off or secure.
steps.helpDetail: Tie a firm knot or use clips. Do not release tension until definitive surgical care.

techniques.name: Hemostatic Wound Packing
techniques.subtitle: Stop massive junctional hemorrhage

steps.instruction: Identify source of massive bleed.
steps.helpDetail: Expose the wound. Wipe away pooled blood to see the exact arterial bleeder.

steps.instruction: Push gauze deep into the cavity.
steps.helpDetail: Feed standard or hemostatic gauze directly onto the bleeding artery inside the wound. Do not just cover the top.

steps.instruction: Pack tightly until full.
steps.helpDetail: Keep feeding gauze under continuous pressure until the wound cavity is completely packed tight.

steps.instruction: Apply direct downward pressure.
steps.helpDetail: Push down on the packed wound with maximum body weight for 3-5 minutes (if hemostatic) or 10 minutes (if plain gauze).

techniques.name: Severe Hypothermia Wrap
techniques.subtitle: The Hypothermia Burrito Protocol

steps.instruction: Remove wet clothing.
steps.helpDetail: Cut away wet clothes if necessary to minimize movement. Rough movement can cause cardiac arrest in severe hypothermia.

steps.instruction: Layer a tarp and sleeping pad.
steps.helpDetail: Place a waterproof layer on the ground, followed by insulating foam pads to block conductive heat loss.

steps.instruction: Add sleeping bags and patient.
steps.helpDetail: Place the patient inside a dry sleeping bag. Add active heat sources (hot water bottles) to the neck, armpits, and groin if available.

steps.instruction: Wrap the outer tarp.
steps.helpDetail: Fold the outer waterproof tarp over the sleeping bag like a burrito to block all convective wind loss and trap heat.

techniques.name: Frostbite Rapid Rewarming
techniques.subtitle: Thawing deep frostbite safely

steps.instruction: Ensure no risk of refreezing.
steps.helpDetail: NEVER thaw frostbite if there is a chance it could freeze again before reaching a hospital. Thawing and refreezing is catastrophic.

steps.instruction: Prepare a warm water bath.
steps.helpDetail: Heat water to 104-108°F (40-42°C). Test it with a thermometer or a healthy elbow. It should feel warm, not burning.

steps.instruction: Immerse the frozen tissue.
steps.helpDetail: Suspend the affected limb in the water. Do not let it touch the sides of the container.

steps.instruction: Circulate and refill.
steps.helpDetail: The frozen tissue will rapidly cool the water. Add warm water as needed to maintain the temperature for 30 minutes until tissue is soft and pliable.

steps.instruction: Air dry and pad.
steps.helpDetail: Do not rub. Air dry, and place sterile cotton between toes/fingers. Elevate and protect.

techniques.name: Heat Stroke Rapid Cooling
techniques.subtitle: Tarp-Assisted Cold Water Immersion

steps.instruction: Recognize Heat Stroke.
steps.helpDetail: Altered mental status (confusion, combativeness) with high body temperature. A medical emergency.

steps.instruction: Create a tarp basin.
steps.helpDetail: Place a tarp on the ground. Have 4-6 people hold the edges up, or wrap it around the patient like a taco.

steps.instruction: Add ice and water.
steps.helpDetail: Pour as much ice and cold water as possible into the tarp to submerge the patient's torso and limbs.

steps.instruction: Agitate the water.
steps.helpDetail: Continuously stir the ice water vigorously to break the thermal boundary layer and maximize heat transfer.

steps.instruction: Cool then transport.
steps.helpDetail: Cool for 15-20 minutes or until shivering begins, then immediately transport to definitive care.

techniques.name: Dry Chemical Decontamination
techniques.subtitle: For unknown or reactive powders

steps.instruction: Do NOT use water immediately.
steps.helpDetail: Many dry chemicals (like pure sodium or lime) react violently with water, causing severe burns.

steps.instruction: Brush off the powder.
steps.helpDetail: Use a dry cloth, a credit card edge, or a soft brush to physically sweep the powder off the skin and clothing.

steps.instruction: Remove contaminated clothing.
steps.helpDetail: Carefully remove clothes. Cut them off instead of pulling them over the head to avoid exposing the eyes and airway.

steps.instruction: Flush ONLY after brushing.
steps.helpDetail: Once all visible powder is physically removed, flush the area with massive amounts of low-pressure water for 20 minutes.

techniques.name: Wet Chemical Decontamination
techniques.subtitle: For corrosive liquids and gases

steps.instruction: Protect yourself first.
steps.helpDetail: Do not touch the chemical without thick gloves. Ensure ventilation so you do not inhale vapors.

steps.instruction: Remove saturated clothing.
steps.helpDetail: Cut away the clothing immediately. Modesty is not a priority; time is tissue.

steps.instruction: Irrigate with copious water.
steps.helpDetail: Flush the affected area with continuously running, low-pressure water for at least 20 minutes.

steps.instruction: Direct water away.
steps.helpDetail: Ensure the runoff water does not pool under the patient or wash over uncontaminated parts of their body.

techniques.name: Tear Gas/OC Recovery
techniques.subtitle: RCA (Riot Control Agent) Decon

steps.instruction: Move to fresh air.
steps.helpDetail: Move upwind and to higher ground. Tear gas (CS) sinks and concentrates in low-lying areas.

steps.instruction: Do NOT rub eyes.
steps.helpDetail: Rubbing will grind the crystalline particulate into the cornea, causing severe damage and extending the pain.

steps.instruction: Blink rapidly and flush.
steps.helpDetail: Face the wind and let tears naturally wash the agent. If using water, use massive amounts. Small amounts of water can reactivate OC (pepper spray).

steps.instruction: Blow nose, do not swallow.
steps.helpDetail: Spit out saliva and blow your nose repeatedly to clear the crystals from your mucous membranes.

techniques.name: Nasopharyngeal Airway Insertion
techniques.subtitle: NPA insertion for unconscious patients

steps.instruction: Select appropriate size
steps.helpDetail: Measure from nostril to earlobe. Use the largest diameter that fits.

steps.instruction: Lubricate the NPA
steps.helpDetail: Use water, saliva, or blood if nothing else is available.

steps.instruction: Insert bevel toward septum
steps.helpDetail: Push along the floor of the nasal passage, not upward.

steps.instruction: Confirm airway patency
steps.helpDetail: Listen for air exchange. If resistance, try other nostril.

techniques.name: Needle Chest Decompression
techniques.subtitle: Emergency tension pneumothorax relief

steps.instruction: Identify tension pneumo signs
steps.helpDetail: Tracheal deviation, absent breath sounds on one side, JVD, hypotension.

steps.instruction: Locate insertion site
steps.helpDetail: 2nd intercostal space, midclavicular line. Or 5th ICS, anterior axillary line.

steps.instruction: Insert 14ga needle
steps.helpDetail: Puncture over the top of the rib (nerves/vessels run under). You'll hear a rush of air.

steps.instruction: Secure catheter
steps.helpDetail: Tape the catheter hub in place. Prepare for chest seal placement.

techniques.name: Combat Gauze Wound Packing
techniques.subtitle: Hemostatic gauze application for deep wounds

steps.instruction: Expose the wound completely
steps.helpDetail: Cut away clothing to visualize the hemorrhage source.

steps.instruction: Pack tightly into wound
steps.helpDetail: Push gauze deep into the wound cavity, packing firmly against the bleeding vessel.

steps.instruction: Apply direct pressure for 3 min
steps.helpDetail: Hold sustained pressure. Do not release to check.

steps.instruction: Apply pressure dressing over top
steps.helpDetail: Wrap with elastic bandage or improvised pressure dressing.

techniques.name: Improvised Pelvic Binder (Sheet)
techniques.subtitle: Using a bedsheet to stabilize pelvic fractures

steps.instruction: Fold sheet lengthwise
steps.helpDetail: Create a band about 20cm wide.

steps.instruction: Slide under patient at hip level
steps.helpDetail: Roll patient minimally. Place at level of greater trochanters.

steps.instruction: Cross ends over pelvis and pull tight
steps.helpDetail: Apply firm inward compression—this approximates the pelvic ring.

steps.instruction: Tie or clamp in place
steps.helpDetail: Secure with a knot, clamp, or twist with a stick for mechanical advantage.

techniques.name: Morgan Lens Eye Irrigation
techniques.subtitle: Continuous chemical burn eye flush

steps.instruction: Flush immediately with clean water
steps.helpDetail: Begin flushing before any assessment. Seconds matter for chemical burns.

steps.instruction: Pull lower lid down
steps.helpDetail: Create a pocket to pour water into directly over the cornea.

steps.instruction: Irrigate for minimum 20 minutes
steps.helpDetail: Chemical burns (especially alkali) require prolonged flushing.

steps.instruction: Check pH if possible
steps.helpDetail: Normal eye pH is 7.0. Continue flushing if still acidic or basic.

techniques.name: Improvised Traction Splint
techniques.subtitle: Femur fracture traction using improvised materials

steps.instruction: Pad the ankle and groin
steps.helpDetail: Use clothing to protect skin from friction and pressure.

steps.instruction: Secure two rigid poles alongside the leg
steps.helpDetail: Poles should extend from armpit past the foot.

steps.instruction: Apply traction via ankle hitch
steps.helpDetail: Use a cravat or belt around the ankle, tied to the pole end.

steps.instruction: Twist a windlass to apply steady pull
steps.helpDetail: Use a stick through the ankle hitch rope to create mechanical traction.

techniques.name: Field Core Temperature Check
techniques.subtitle: Accurate hypothermia staging via core temp

steps.instruction: Understand why oral temp fails
steps.helpDetail: Oral and axillary readings can be 2-4°F lower than core, leading to dangerous underestimation.

steps.instruction: Use rectal or esophageal probe if available
steps.helpDetail: This is the gold standard for hypothermia assessment.

steps.instruction: Stage hypothermia by core temp
steps.helpDetail: Mild: 90-95°F. Moderate: 82-90°F. Severe: <82°F. Each has different protocols.

steps.instruction: Document and monitor trends
steps.helpDetail: Rewarm at no more than 1-2°F per hour to avoid afterdrop.

techniques.name: Junctional Hemorrhage Control
techniques.subtitle: Axilla, groin, and neck bleed management

steps.instruction: Identify junctional zone
steps.helpDetail: Axilla (armpit), inguinal (groin), or cervical (neck). Tourniquets don't work here.

steps.instruction: Pack wound with hemostatic gauze
steps.helpDetail: Push gauze deep into the wound using your fingers.

steps.instruction: Hold direct pressure for 5 min
steps.helpDetail: CONTINUOUS pressure. Do not release.

steps.instruction: Apply improvised junctional pressure
steps.helpDetail: Use a knee, a ball, or a wrapped rock tied over the wound with bandage.

techniques.name: Oropharyngeal Airway (OPA)
techniques.subtitle: Maintaining airway in unconscious patients

steps.instruction: Confirm patient is UNCONSCIOUS
steps.helpDetail: If patient has a gag reflex, do NOT insert an OPA.

steps.instruction: Size OPA correctly
steps.helpDetail: Measure from corner of mouth to angle of jaw.

steps.instruction: Insert with tip pointing up
steps.helpDetail: Rotate 180° as it reaches the soft palate.

steps.instruction: Position properly
steps.helpDetail: The flange should rest against the lips. Confirm air exchange.

techniques.name: Improvised Eye Shield
techniques.subtitle: Protecting penetrating eye injuries

steps.instruction: Do NOT remove embedded objects
steps.helpDetail: Any object penetrating the eye must stay in place.

steps.instruction: Build a rigid shield
steps.helpDetail: Use a paper cup, cut bottle bottom, or cone of cardboard.

steps.instruction: Tape shield over the eye
steps.helpDetail: Do not apply any pressure to the globe.

steps.instruction: Patch both eyes
steps.helpDetail: Covering only one eye causes consensual movement in the injured eye.

techniques.name: Cardiac Tamponade Recognition
techniques.subtitle: Beck's Triad identification in trauma

steps.instruction: Suspect tamponade after chest trauma
steps.helpDetail: Penetrating injuries near the heart can cause blood in the pericardium.

steps.instruction: Identify Beck's Triad
steps.helpDetail: Muffled heart sounds, JVD, hypotension.

steps.instruction: Keep patient sitting upright
steps.helpDetail: Lean forward if possible to reduce cardiac compression.

steps.instruction: Evacuate to surgical care immediately
steps.helpDetail: This is not survivable in the field without surgical intervention.

techniques.name: Blast Injury Assessment
techniques.subtitle: Primary, secondary, tertiary blast injury triage

steps.instruction: Primary blast injury
steps.helpDetail: Overpressure wave damages air-filled organs: lungs, ears, bowel. Check for hemoptysis.

steps.instruction: Secondary blast injury
steps.helpDetail: Shrapnel and flying debris. Treat as penetrating trauma.

steps.instruction: Tertiary blast injury
steps.helpDetail: Body thrown by blast wind. Treat as blunt trauma—spine, pelvis, skull.

steps.instruction: Quaternary blast injury
steps.helpDetail: Burns, crush injuries, inhalation. Treat each component individually.

techniques.name: Crush Syndrome Management
techniques.subtitle: Preventing renal failure after prolonged entrapment

steps.instruction: Assess entrapment duration
steps.helpDetail: Crush syndrome risk increases dramatically after 1+ hours of compression.

steps.instruction: Start IV fluids BEFORE release if possible
steps.helpDetail: Aggressive hydration prevents potassium-induced cardiac arrest upon release.

steps.instruction: Apply tourniquet above crush site before release
steps.helpDetail: This prevents the surge of myoglobin and potassium from reaching the heart.

steps.instruction: Monitor cardiac rhythm
steps.helpDetail: Hyperkalemia causes peaked T-waves and can cause fatal arrhythmia.

techniques.name: Circumferential Burn Assessment
techniques.subtitle: Identifying compartment syndrome in burns

steps.instruction: Check for circumferential burns
steps.helpDetail: Burns that encircle a limb or the chest can act as a tourniquet as swelling increases.

steps.instruction: Assess distal circulation
steps.helpDetail: Check pulses, capillary refill, sensation, and movement below the burn.

steps.instruction: Elevate burned extremities
steps.helpDetail: Reduce swelling with elevation while monitoring circulation.

steps.instruction: Prepare for escharotomy
steps.helpDetail: In field conditions, a scalpel cut through the eschar along the lateral aspect may be life-saving.

techniques.name: Knocked-Out Tooth Preservation
techniques.subtitle: Reimplantation window and storage

steps.instruction: Handle tooth by crown only
steps.helpDetail: Never touch the root. Do not scrub or clean it.

steps.instruction: Reimplant within 30 minutes if possible
steps.helpDetail: Gently push back into the socket and have the patient bite down on gauze.

steps.instruction: If can't reimplant, store properly
steps.helpDetail: Place in milk, saliva (have patient hold in cheek), or saline. NOT water.

steps.instruction: Seek dental care within 2 hours
steps.helpDetail: Viability drops dramatically after this window.

techniques.name: Impaled Object Stabilization
techniques.subtitle: Securing penetrating objects in place

steps.instruction: NEVER remove impaled objects
steps.helpDetail: Removal can cause uncontrolled hemorrhage.

steps.instruction: Stabilize with bulky dressings
steps.helpDetail: Pack rolled gauze or clothing around the object on all sides.

steps.instruction: Tape or bandage to prevent movement
steps.helpDetail: The object must not shift during transport.

steps.instruction: Shorten if necessary for transport
steps.helpDetail: Cut (don't pull) if the object prevents evacuation. Leave 6+ inches protruding.

techniques.name: Open Fracture Field Management
techniques.subtitle: Managing bone protruding through skin

steps.instruction: Control bleeding first
steps.helpDetail: Apply pressure around (not on) the exposed bone.

steps.instruction: Irrigate wound with clean water
steps.helpDetail: Flush debris from around the bone. Do not push bone back in.

steps.instruction: Cover with moist sterile dressing
steps.helpDetail: Keep exposed bone moist to prevent desiccation.

steps.instruction: Splint in position found
steps.helpDetail: Do not attempt to realign. Splint exactly as found and evacuate.

techniques.name: Compartment Syndrome Recognition
techniques.subtitle: Identifying limb-threatening pressure buildup

steps.instruction: Suspect after fractures, crush, or tight casts
steps.helpDetail: Pressure within a muscle compartment exceeds perfusion pressure.

steps.instruction: The 6 P's
steps.helpDetail: Pain (out of proportion), Pressure (tense compartment), Paresthesia, Paralysis, Pallor, Pulselessness.

steps.instruction: Pain with passive stretch is the earliest sign
steps.helpDetail: Extending the fingers/toes of the affected limb causes severe pain.

steps.instruction: Loosen all circumferential dressings immediately
steps.helpDetail: Remove casts, tight bandages, and splints. Evacuate for fasciotomy.

techniques.name: Field Blood Type Identification
techniques.subtitle: Emergency blood typing for buddy transfusion

steps.instruction: Know your blood type in advance
steps.helpDetail: All survival team members should carry blood type on a card or dog tag.

steps.instruction: O-negative is universal donor
steps.helpDetail: If blood types are unknown, O-neg is safest but still carries risk.

steps.instruction: Improvised transfusion is last resort
steps.helpDetail: IV tubing directly between veins (walking blood bank) has been used in combat.

steps.instruction: Monitor for transfusion reaction
steps.helpDetail: Fever, chills, back pain, dark urine indicate incompatibility. Stop immediately.

techniques.name: Heat Cramps Field Treatment
techniques.subtitle: Managing exercise-associated muscle cramping

steps.instruction: Stop activity and rest in shade
steps.helpDetail: Continued exertion worsens cramping and risks heat exhaustion.

steps.instruction: Sip electrolyte solution
steps.helpDetail: Water alone may worsen cramps. Add salt to water (1/4 teaspoon per liter).

steps.instruction: Stretch and massage affected muscles
steps.helpDetail: Gentle sustained stretching relieves spasm faster than rest alone.

steps.instruction: Do not resume activity until cramps fully resolve
steps.helpDetail: Cramps that return indicate sodium depletion. Continue oral rehydration.

techniques.name: Three-Sided Occlusive Dressing
techniques.subtitle: Creating a flutter valve for open pneumothorax

steps.instruction: Find or improvise occlusive material
steps.helpDetail: Plastic wrap, a credit card, foil, or the wrapper from a chest seal.

steps.instruction: Place over the wound on exhalation
steps.helpDetail: The patient exhales fully, then you seal three sides with tape.

steps.instruction: Leave the bottom edge open
steps.helpDetail: This creates a flutter valve: air escapes on exhalation but cannot re-enter on inhalation.

steps.instruction: Monitor for tension pneumo
steps.helpDetail: If breathing worsens, briefly lift the dressing to release trapped air, then reseal.

techniques.name: Finger Thoracostomy
techniques.subtitle: Emergency chest decompression without needle

steps.instruction: Identify life-threatening tension pneumo
steps.helpDetail: JVD, tracheal deviation, absent breath sounds, shock.

steps.instruction: Locate 5th ICS, anterior axillary line
steps.helpDetail: This is the lateral chest wall at nipple level.

steps.instruction: Make 4cm incision through skin and muscle
steps.helpDetail: Cut down to the rib, then bluntly dissect over the top of the rib into the pleura.

steps.instruction: Insert gloved finger and sweep
steps.helpDetail: Confirm entry into pleural space by feeling the lung. You'll hear a rush of air.

techniques.name: Improvised Negative Pressure Wound Therapy
techniques.subtitle: Creating suction wound closure in the field

steps.instruction: Clean wound thoroughly
steps.helpDetail: Irrigate and debride. Remove all visible debris.

steps.instruction: Place gauze in wound bed
steps.helpDetail: Use clean, moist gauze to fill the wound cavity.

steps.instruction: Seal with plastic wrap and tape
steps.helpDetail: Create an airtight seal over the gauze using plastic sheeting.

steps.instruction: Attach manual suction
steps.helpDetail: A syringe with tubing inserted through the seal creates negative pressure.

techniques.name: Pediatric Assessment Triangle (PAT)
techniques.subtitle: Rapid pediatric evaluation without touching child

steps.instruction: Appearance (TICLS)
steps.helpDetail: Tone, Interactiveness, Consolability, Look/Gaze, Speech/Cry. Observe from across the room.

steps.instruction: Work of Breathing
steps.helpDetail: Nasal flaring, retractions, head bobbing, grunting, tripod position.

steps.instruction: Circulation to Skin
steps.helpDetail: Pallor, mottling, cyanosis indicate poor perfusion.

steps.instruction: Combine findings
steps.helpDetail: All three normal = stable. Any abnormal = intervene immediately based on affected domain.

techniques.name: START Triage (Mass Casualty)
techniques.subtitle: Simple Triage and Rapid Treatment for multiple victims

steps.instruction: Walk to designated area
steps.helpDetail: Anyone who can walk is GREEN (Minor). Direct them to a collection point.

steps.instruction: Assess breathing
steps.helpDetail: Not breathing after repositioning airway = BLACK (Deceased). Breathing >30/min = RED (Immediate).

steps.instruction: Assess perfusion
steps.helpDetail: Radial pulse absent OR capillary refill >2 sec = RED. Apply tourniquet if hemorrhaging.

steps.instruction: Assess mental status
steps.helpDetail: Cannot follow simple commands = RED. Can follow commands = YELLOW (Delayed).

techniques.name: Decompression Sickness (The Bends)
techniques.subtitle: Recognize and manage nitrogen bubble formation after diving

steps.instruction: Recognize symptoms: joint pain, skin rash, dizziness, weakness, numbness.
steps.helpDetail: Symptoms typically appear within 1-6 hours of surfacing. Can be delayed up to 24 hours.

steps.instruction: Lay patient flat. Administer 100% oxygen if available.
steps.helpDetail: High-flow O2 via non-rebreather mask. This is the single most important field treatment.

steps.instruction: Give oral fluids: 1 liter per hour if conscious.
steps.helpDetail: Dehydration worsens bubble formation. Avoid alcohol.

steps.instruction: Evacuate to nearest recompression chamber. Do NOT fly or ascend to altitude.
steps.helpDetail: Flying or ascending worsens the condition. Helicopter evacuation must stay below 1000ft AGL. Call DAN (Divers Alert Network) for nearest chamber location.

techniques.name: Ear Barotrauma
techniques.subtitle: Pressure injury to the ear from diving or altitude change

steps.instruction: Recognize: sharp ear pain, muffled hearing, dizziness, possible bleeding from ear.
steps.helpDetail: Occurs during descent when unable to equalize pressure.

steps.instruction: DO NOT insert anything into the ear canal.
steps.helpDetail: No drops, no cotton swabs, nothing.

steps.instruction: Apply gentle warmth to the affected ear.
steps.helpDetail: A warm cloth provides comfort. Decongestants can help reduce swelling.

steps.instruction: If eardrum rupture suspected: keep ear dry, no swimming, no blowing nose hard.
steps.helpDetail: Tympanic membrane ruptures usually heal in 2-6 weeks. Seek medical evaluation. Do not dive again until cleared.

techniques.name: Near-Drowning Management
techniques.subtitle: Post-submersion rescue breathing and monitoring

steps.instruction: Remove from water. Begin rescue breathing immediately — still in water if needed.
steps.helpDetail: Don't waste time trying to drain water from lungs. Ventilations are the priority.

steps.instruction: If no pulse: begin CPR on a flat surface.
steps.helpDetail: Standard 30:2 compression to ventilation ratio. Cold water victims may benefit from prolonged CPR — don't give up early.

steps.instruction: All near-drowning victims need hospital monitoring even if they seem fine.
steps.helpDetail: Secondary drowning (delayed pulmonary edema) can occur 2-24 hours later.

steps.instruction: Keep warm — immersion hypothermia is common co-morbidity.
steps.helpDetail: Remove wet clothing, insulate from ground, warm blankets. Monitor for deterioration.

techniques.name: Jellyfish Sting Treatment
techniques.subtitle: Species-specific sting management for box jelly, Portuguese man-o-war, and others

steps.instruction: Remove any visible tentacles using a stick, card, or gloved hand — DO NOT use bare hands.
steps.helpDetail: Tentacle fragments continue to fire nematocysts even when detached.

steps.instruction: For most jellyfish: rinse with vinegar (acetic acid) for 30+ seconds.
steps.helpDetail: Vinegar deactivates unfired nematocysts. EXCEPTION: Do NOT use vinegar on Portuguese man-o-war — use hot water instead.

steps.instruction: Immerse in hot water (113°F/45°C) for 20-45 minutes.
steps.helpDetail: Heat denatures the venom proteins. As hot as tolerable without burning. This is the gold standard for pain relief.

steps.instruction: DO NOT use fresh water, ice, urine, or alcohol — all cause additional nematocyst firing.
steps.helpDetail: Monitor for anaphylaxis. Box jellyfish stings can be fatal — evacuate immediately if systemic symptoms appear.

techniques.name: Stingray Wound Treatment
techniques.subtitle: Barbed wound with envenomation — heat destroys the venom

steps.instruction: Control bleeding with direct pressure.
steps.helpDetail: Stingray barbs cause lacerations, not punctures. Irrigate with clean water.

steps.instruction: Immerse wound in hot water (113°F/45°C) for 30-90 minutes.
steps.helpDetail: The venom is heat-labile — hot water neutralizes it and provides dramatic pain relief.

steps.instruction: DO NOT remove the barb if deeply embedded — stabilize and evacuate.
steps.helpDetail: The barb has retrograde serrations. Pulling it out causes more damage. If superficial, remove carefully.

steps.instruction: Wound infection risk is high — start broad-spectrum antibiotics if available.
steps.helpDetail: Marine wounds introduce Vibrio and other marine bacteria. Clean aggressively.

techniques.name: Acute Mountain Sickness (AMS)
techniques.subtitle: Recognize altitude sickness before it becomes life-threatening

steps.instruction: Symptoms at 8,000+ feet: headache that worsens, nausea, fatigue, dizziness.
steps.helpDetail: Lake Louise AMS Score: headache required, plus one of: GI symptoms, fatigue, or dizziness.

steps.instruction: STOP ascending. Rest at current altitude for 24-48 hours.
steps.helpDetail: Most AMS resolves with acclimatization. Do not sleep higher than you feel good at.

steps.instruction: Treat headache with ibuprofen 400mg or acetaminophen.
steps.helpDetail: Aspirin also works. Stay hydrated — altitude dehydration worsens symptoms.

steps.instruction: If symptoms worsen despite rest: DESCEND immediately. 1,000+ feet minimum.
steps.helpDetail: AMS can progress to HACE or HAPE — both lethal. Descent is the definitive cure.

techniques.name: High Altitude Cerebral Edema (HACE)
techniques.subtitle: Emergency descent — brain swelling at altitude is fatal without treatment

steps.instruction: Recognize: AMS symptoms PLUS ataxia (can't walk straight) and/or altered mental status.
steps.helpDetail: Test with tandem gait: walk heel-to-toe. If they can't do it — it's HACE.

steps.instruction: DESCEND IMMEDIATELY. This is an emergency — carry the patient if needed.
steps.helpDetail: Even 500-1000 feet improvement helps. Do not wait for morning.

steps.instruction: Dexamethasone 8mg immediately, then 4mg every 6 hours if available.
steps.helpDetail: Dex reduces brain swelling. This is a bridge treatment — descent is still required.

steps.instruction: Supplemental oxygen 2-4 L/min if available. Gamow bag if descent is impossible.
steps.helpDetail: A Gamow bag simulates descent by increasing pressure. Inflate and maintain.

techniques.name: High Altitude Pulmonary Edema (HAPE)
techniques.subtitle: Fluid in the lungs at altitude — descend or die

steps.instruction: Recognize: breathlessness at rest, cough (often with pink frothy sputum), extreme fatigue, cyanosis.
steps.helpDetail: HAPE can occur without preceding AMS. Most common above 12,000 feet.

steps.instruction: DESCEND AT LEAST 2,000 feet. Keep patient warm and minimize exertion.
steps.helpDetail: Patient should not carry anything. Descent is treatment.

steps.instruction: Nifedipine 30mg slow-release if available.
steps.helpDetail: Reduces pulmonary artery pressure. Not a substitute for descent.

steps.instruction: Supplemental oxygen 4-6 L/min. Gamow bag as last resort.
steps.helpDetail: Patient should rest in sitting/semi-upright position. Lying flat worsens pulmonary edema.

techniques.name: Pit Viper Snakebite (Rattlesnake, Copperhead, Cottonmouth)
techniques.subtitle: North American venomous snake protocol

steps.instruction: Move away from the snake. Note appearance but do NOT try to catch it.
steps.helpDetail: Photo from safe distance helps ID. Time the bite — treatment varies by time elapsed.

steps.instruction: Remove jewelry and constricting clothing near the bite.
steps.helpDetail: Swelling can be dramatic. Rings, watches, and tight sleeves must come off immediately.

steps.instruction: IMMOBILIZE the bitten limb at or below heart level.
steps.helpDetail: Do NOT apply tourniquet, ice, suction, or make cuts. All traditional remedies make it worse.

steps.instruction: Evacuate to hospital. Antivenom is the only definitive treatment.
steps.helpDetail: Walk patient out if possible. Carry if bitten on leg. Mark the swelling edge with pen and time to track progression.

techniques.name: Coral Snake Bite
techniques.subtitle: Neurotoxic venom — different protocol from pit vipers

steps.instruction: Identify: red-yellow-black bands. 'Red touches yellow, kills a fellow.'
steps.helpDetail: Coral snakes have small mouths and must chew to envenomate. Pain may be minimal initially.

steps.instruction: Apply a pressure immobilization bandage: firm wrap starting at the bite and wrapping toward the heart.
steps.helpDetail: Unlike pit vipers, pressure immobilization IS recommended for neurotoxic snakes.

steps.instruction: IMMOBILIZE entirely — splint the limb.
steps.helpDetail: Movement speeds venom absorption through lymphatic system.

steps.instruction: Transport immediately. Symptoms may be delayed 12+ hours but can progress to respiratory failure.
steps.helpDetail: Antivenom must be given before symptoms appear for best outcome. Hospital monitoring essential.

techniques.name: Pediatric Choking (Infant < 1 year)
techniques.subtitle: Back blows and chest thrusts for choking infants

steps.instruction: Position infant face-down along your forearm, head lower than body.
steps.helpDetail: Support the head and jaw with your hand. Rest your forearm on your thigh.

steps.instruction: Deliver 5 firm back blows between the shoulder blades with heel of hand.
steps.helpDetail: Firm, not gentle. The goal is to create enough force to dislodge the object.

steps.instruction: Flip infant face-up on your other forearm. Give 5 chest thrusts.
steps.helpDetail: Two fingers on the breastbone, just below the nipple line. Compress 1.5 inches.

steps.instruction: Alternate back blows and chest thrusts until object is expelled or infant becomes unconscious.
steps.helpDetail: If unconscious: begin infant CPR (30 compressions : 2 breaths). Check for visible objects before each breath — do NOT do blind finger sweeps.

techniques.name: Pediatric Fever Management (Field)
techniques.subtitle: Manage high fever in children without pharmacy access

steps.instruction: Assess temperature: hand on forehead is crude but feel for excessive heat.
steps.helpDetail: Normal child temp: 97.5-99.5°F. Fever: >100.4°F. High fever: >104°F.

steps.instruction: Remove excess clothing. Keep in cool shade.
steps.helpDetail: Do NOT bundle a feverish child. Single light layer.

steps.instruction: Cool with lukewarm (NOT cold) water on forehead, neck, and armpits.
steps.helpDetail: Cold water causes shivering which RAISES core temperature. Lukewarm evaporation is effective.

steps.instruction: Maintain hydration: small frequent sips of clean water.
steps.helpDetail: Dehydration is the primary danger of pediatric fever in the field. If child refuses to drink, wet their lips and cheeks.

techniques.name: Pediatric Dehydration Assessment
techniques.subtitle: Recognize dehydration severity in children — field signs

steps.instruction: Check the fontanelle (soft spot) in infants: sunken = dehydrated.
steps.helpDetail: This only works in children under 18 months. A noticeably depressed fontanelle indicates moderate dehydration.

steps.instruction: Skin turgor test: pinch skin on back of hand or abdomen.
steps.helpDetail: Stays tented for >2 seconds = moderate dehydration. >4 seconds = severe.

steps.instruction: Check mucous membranes: dry lips and mouth, no tears when crying.
steps.helpDetail: Absence of tears in a crying child is a significant dehydration sign.

steps.instruction: Monitor urine: dark yellow or no urine for 6+ hours = significant dehydration.
steps.helpDetail: Rehydrate with oral rehydration solution: 1L water + 6 tsp sugar + ½ tsp salt. Small frequent sips — don't let them gulp.

techniques.name: Dental Abscess Field Management
techniques.subtitle: Manage tooth infection without dentist access

steps.instruction: Recognize: throbbing toothache, swelling of gum or face, fever, bad taste in mouth.
steps.helpDetail: An abscess is a bacterial infection at the tooth root. It will NOT resolve without treatment.

steps.instruction: Rinse with warm salt water (½ tsp salt in 8oz water) every 2 hours.
steps.helpDetail: Draws out some pus and provides minor relief.

steps.instruction: Pain management: ibuprofen 400-600mg every 6 hours.
steps.helpDetail: Ibuprofen is superior to acetaminophen for dental pain. Alternate with acetaminophen if needed.

steps.instruction: If swelling spreads to eye, neck, or floor of mouth: EMERGENCY. This can become life-threatening.
steps.helpDetail: Ludwig's angina (submandibular space infection) can obstruct the airway. Requires immediate antibiotics and possibly surgical drainage.

techniques.name: Improvised Temporary Filling
techniques.subtitle: Seal a cavity or lost filling in the field

steps.instruction: Clean the cavity: rinse mouth with warm salt water, remove any debris.
steps.helpDetail: Use a toothpick or clean twig to gently remove food particles from the hole.

steps.instruction: Dry the tooth as much as possible.
steps.helpDetail: Use a cloth or gauze to absorb moisture around the tooth.

steps.instruction: Fill with temporary filling material, sugar-free gum, or candle wax.
steps.helpDetail: Commercial dental cement kits are ideal. In the field: softened beeswax or sugar-free gum pressed firmly into the cavity.

steps.instruction: Avoid chewing on that side. Replace the improvised filling daily.
steps.helpDetail: This is a temporary seal only — it prevents food impaction and cold sensitivity. Seek dental care when possible.

techniques.name: Jaw Dislocation Reduction
techniques.subtitle: Relocate a dislocated jaw — field technique

steps.instruction: Recognize: jaw locked open, patient cannot close mouth, drooling, extreme pain.
steps.helpDetail: Usually occurs after yawning widely, trauma, or seizure.

steps.instruction: Wrap your thumbs in thick cloth for protection.
steps.helpDetail: The jaw will snap closed forcefully. Protect your thumbs.

steps.instruction: Place wrapped thumbs on the lower molars. Apply steady DOWNWARD then BACKWARD pressure.
steps.helpDetail: Push down first to clear the articular eminence, then guide backward into the socket.

steps.instruction: The jaw should click back into place. Apply a bandage wrap to limit opening for 24 hours.
steps.helpDetail: No wide yawning, no hard foods, no excessive talking for several days. Recurrence is common.

techniques.name: Emergency Field Childbirth
techniques.subtitle: Deliver a baby when hospital is not reachable

steps.instruction: Prepare clean area: cleanest surface available, clean cloths, string or shoelace for cord.
steps.helpDetail: Boil string and any cutting implement if possible. Wash hands thoroughly.

steps.instruction: Support the baby's head as it emerges — DON'T PULL.
steps.helpDetail: The head delivers first in normal presentation. Support it, don't force anything. Check for cord around neck — slide over head if present.

steps.instruction: After head: body usually rotates and delivers with next contraction.
steps.helpDetail: Support but don't pull. Clear mouth and nose. Baby should cry within 30 seconds.

steps.instruction: Tie cord in two places (6 and 8 inches from baby), cut between ties. Keep baby warm skin-to-skin.
steps.helpDetail: The placenta delivers 5-30 minutes later. DO NOT pull on the cord. Keep the placenta — medical staff need to inspect it.

techniques.name: Postpartum Hemorrhage Management
techniques.subtitle: Control life-threatening bleeding after delivery

steps.instruction: Recognize: soaking more than one pad per hour, large clots, dizziness, rapid pulse.
steps.helpDetail: PPH is the #1 killer of mothers worldwide. Act fast.

steps.instruction: Fundal massage: place hand on lower abdomen and massage the uterus firmly.
steps.helpDetail: The uterus should feel like a hard grapefruit. If it's soft and boggy, massage aggressively in a circular motion.

steps.instruction: Encourage breastfeeding immediately — nipple stimulation causes uterine contraction.
steps.helpDetail: Oxytocin release from breastfeeding helps the uterus clamp down on bleeding vessels.

steps.instruction: If bleeding won't stop: bimanual compression — one fist inside the vagina against the uterus, other hand on abdomen.
steps.helpDetail: This is a last-resort field technique. Evacuate immediately. Misoprostol under the tongue if available.

techniques.name: Field Soap Making
techniques.subtitle: Produce soap from fat and wood ash lye — disease prevention

steps.instruction: Make lye water: fill a container with hardwood ash, pour water through it repeatedly.
steps.helpDetail: Oak, hickory, or maple ash works best. The drippings should feel slippery between fingers.

steps.instruction: Render animal fat: heat fat scraps in water, skim off the clean tallow layer when cooled.
steps.helpDetail: Any animal fat works. Strain through cloth to remove meat particles.

steps.instruction: Heat tallow and slowly add lye water, stirring constantly.
steps.helpDetail: Maintain low heat. The mixture will thicken over 1-2 hours. This is saponification.

steps.instruction: Pour into molds (bark containers, carved wood). Cure for 2-4 weeks.
steps.helpDetail: The soap hardens as water evaporates. It will be harsh but functional. Essential for disease prevention in extended survival.

techniques.name: Waterborne Disease Prevention Protocol
techniques.subtitle: Prevent dysentery, cholera, and giardia in the field

steps.instruction: ALWAYS treat water before drinking — no exceptions regardless of how clean it looks.
steps.helpDetail: Giardia cysts are invisible. Crypto is chlorine-resistant. Mountain streams are NOT safe.

steps.instruction: Establish a clean water station separate from washing and latrine areas.
steps.helpDetail: Minimum 200 feet downhill from latrines. Uphill from water source.

steps.instruction: Wash hands after latrine use and before food preparation — every single time.
steps.helpDetail: Fecal-oral transmission causes most waterborne illness. Ash or sand scrub if soap unavailable.

steps.instruction: Monitor for symptoms: watery diarrhea, vomiting, cramping. Begin ORS immediately.
steps.helpDetail: Oral rehydration: 1L water + 6 tsp sugar + ½ tsp salt. Dehydration kills — the infection usually doesn't.

techniques.name: Wound Irrigation (Pressure Irrigation)
techniques.subtitle: The single most important wound care technique

steps.instruction: Prepare irrigation solution: cleanest water available. Boiled and cooled is ideal.
steps.helpDetail: If nothing else: even tap water is better than NOT irrigating. Dilute iodine solution is optimal.

steps.instruction: Create pressure: poke a small hole in a plastic bag or use a syringe.
steps.helpDetail: You need PRESSURE to flush debris. Gravity pour is insufficient.

steps.instruction: Irrigate aggressively: 250ml minimum for small wounds, 1 liter for large or contaminated wounds.
steps.helpDetail: Direct the stream into the wound. Expect it to hurt. Debris removal prevents infection.

steps.instruction: Pat dry and dress. Re-irrigate every 12-24 hours if in the field.
steps.helpDetail: Wound infection rates drop from 30% to 3% with proper irrigation. This is more important than antibiotics.

techniques.name: Willow Bark Tea (Natural Aspirin)
techniques.subtitle: Salicylic acid from willow bark for pain and fever

steps.instruction: Harvest inner bark from willow, poplar, or aspen trees.
steps.helpDetail: Peel outer bark, then strip the soft inner bark (cambium). Spring is best but works year-round.

steps.instruction: Chop the inner bark into small pieces.
steps.helpDetail: More surface area = stronger brew.

steps.instruction: Steep 2 tablespoons of bark in 1 cup boiling water for 15 minutes.
steps.helpDetail: Do NOT boil the bark — steep like tea. Boiling destroys some compounds.

steps.instruction: Drink 1 cup every 4-6 hours for pain and fever. Tastes bitter.
steps.helpDetail: Contains salicin, the precursor to aspirin. Same contraindications: avoid if allergic to aspirin, bleeding risk, or in children under 12.

techniques.name: Activated Charcoal (Improvised)
techniques.subtitle: Adsorb ingested poisons with improvised activated charcoal

steps.instruction: Make charcoal: burn hardwood until it's black but not completely ash.
steps.helpDetail: You want pure carbon — black, lightweight, brittle.

steps.instruction: Crush charcoal into fine powder.
steps.helpDetail: The finer the better — surface area is what adsorbs toxins.

steps.instruction: Mix 25-50 grams of charcoal powder into 250ml of water.
steps.helpDetail: It will look like black ink. Stir thoroughly.

steps.instruction: Drink within 1 hour of poison ingestion for maximum effect.
steps.helpDetail: Works on most oral poisons EXCEPT: caustics (lye/acid), metals, alcohols, and petroleum. NOT a substitute for medical evaluation. Do NOT induce vomiting.

techniques.name: Honey Wound Dressing
techniques.subtitle: Medical-grade wound treatment — antimicrobial and promotes healing

steps.instruction: Use raw, unprocessed honey if possible. Any honey works in emergency.
steps.helpDetail: Manuka honey is ideal. Regular honey is effective. Do NOT use on infants under 1 year (botulism risk).

steps.instruction: Clean the wound first with irrigation.
steps.helpDetail: Honey on a dirty wound is less effective.

steps.instruction: Apply a thick layer of honey directly to the wound surface.
steps.helpDetail: Cover the wound entirely. Pack deep wounds.

steps.instruction: Cover with a clean dressing. Change every 12-24 hours.
steps.helpDetail: Honey creates a low pH, high osmolarity environment that kills bacteria. Used in hospitals as Medihoney for chronic wounds and burns. Effective against MRSA.

techniques.name: Pine Needle Tea (Vitamin C)
techniques.subtitle: Prevent scurvy in extended survival — massive vitamin C source

steps.instruction: Identify pine, spruce, or fir needles. AVOID yew (flat needles, red berries — toxic).
steps.helpDetail: All true pines are safe. The needles should be green and fragrant when crushed.

steps.instruction: Harvest a handful of fresh green needles.
steps.helpDetail: Younger, lighter-colored needles have more vitamin C. Chop finely.

steps.instruction: Steep in hot (not boiling) water for 10 minutes.
steps.helpDetail: Boiling destroys vitamin C. Use water just off the boil.

steps.instruction: Drink 1-2 cups daily. Tastes like Christmas.
steps.helpDetail: One cup contains 5x the vitamin C of an orange. Prevents scurvy in extended survival. Also has vitamin A and antioxidants.

techniques.name: Tension Pneumothorax Recognition
techniques.subtitle: Life-threatening air pressure buildup in chest — recognize and act fast

steps.instruction: Recognize: severe breathing difficulty, one side of chest not moving, distended neck veins.
steps.helpDetail: Trachea deviates AWAY from the affected side. This is a late sign — don't wait for it.

steps.instruction: Absent breath sounds on the affected side.
steps.helpDetail: Use your ear against the chest wall if no stethoscope. Compare both sides.

steps.instruction: If you have a needle: decompress at the 2nd intercostal space, midclavicular line.
steps.helpDetail: 14-gauge needle, perpendicular to chest wall, just above the 3rd rib. Rush of air confirms diagnosis.

steps.instruction: If no equipment: maintain oxygen, elevate head, and EVACUATE immediately.
steps.helpDetail: Without decompression, this will progress to cardiac arrest as the trapped air crushes the heart and great vessels.

techniques.name: Emergency Tourniquet (High and Tight)
techniques.subtitle: Catastrophic limb hemorrhage — apply before assessing wound

steps.instruction: Place tourniquet as HIGH and TIGHT on the limb as possible.
steps.helpDetail: Do NOT waste time finding the wound. High and tight on the upper limb is always correct initially.

steps.instruction: Tighten until ALL bleeding stops and distal pulse is absent.
steps.helpDetail: If blood is still flowing, it's not tight enough. Expect the patient to scream — this is correct.

steps.instruction: Note the TIME. Write 'TQ' and time on the patient's forehead.
steps.helpDetail: Tourniquet time matters for surgical decisions. Do NOT remove once applied.

steps.instruction: A tourniquet can safely stay on for 6+ hours in most cases.
steps.helpDetail: The old 'loosen every 15 minutes' advice is WRONG. Once on, leave it on until surgical care is available.

techniques.name: Junctional Hemorrhage Control
techniques.subtitle: Bleeding at neck, armpit, or groin — can't tourniquet these

steps.instruction: Pack the wound TIGHTLY with gauze or clean cloth.
steps.helpDetail: Stuff the wound cavity full. Use a finger to push material as deep as possible.
