steps.instruction: Apply DIRECT, SUSTAINED PRESSURE for minimum 10 minutes.
steps.helpDetail: Hard pressure. Use both hands, lean body weight into it. Do not release to check.

steps.instruction: If hemostatic gauze available (QuikClot, Celox): use it FIRST, then pack and pressure.
steps.helpDetail: Hemostatic agents accelerate clotting. Pack deep, then standard gauze, then pressure.

steps.instruction: DO NOT REMOVE initial packing — add more on top if bleeding through.
steps.helpDetail: Removing packing disrupts any clot formation. Layer more on top and increase pressure.

techniques.name: Improvised Pelvic Binder
techniques.subtitle: Stabilize a pelvic fracture — prevent lethal internal hemorrhage

steps.instruction: Suspect pelvic fracture if: high-energy mechanism, hip/groin pain, leg length discrepancy.
steps.helpDetail: DO NOT rock the pelvis to test — you can worsen hemorrhage.

steps.instruction: Wrap a sheet, jacket, or belt around the pelvis at the level of the greater trochanters.
steps.helpDetail: The binder goes around the HIPS, not the iliac crests. Feel for the bony bumps on the outside of the thigh.

steps.instruction: Tie or cinch snugly — the goal is to reduce pelvic volume.
steps.helpDetail: Think of wringing a towel. Adequate force compresses the pelvis and tamponades bleeding.

steps.instruction: Log-roll the patient onto a backboard. Do NOT sit them up.
steps.helpDetail: Pelvic fractures can bleed liters internally. Mortality is high without surgical intervention. EVACUATE.

techniques.name: Flail Chest Stabilization
techniques.subtitle: Multiple rib fractures with paradoxical breathing

steps.instruction: Recognize: section of chest wall moves INWARD during inhalation (paradoxical motion).
steps.helpDetail: Caused by 3+ adjacent ribs each broken in 2+ places, creating a 'floating' segment.

steps.instruction: Position patient with the INJURED SIDE DOWN.
steps.helpDetail: Lying on the flail segment splints it naturally.

steps.instruction: Do NOT tape or strap the chest — this restricts breathing.
steps.helpDetail: Old practice of taping is abandoned. It worsens ventilation.

steps.instruction: Support ventilation. If patient tires and breathing becomes shallow: assist ventilation with bag-valve-mask if available.
steps.helpDetail: The underlying lung contusion is the real killer. Evacuate immediately — these patients often need a ventilator.

techniques.name: START Triage System
techniques.subtitle: Sort multiple casualties in 30 seconds each

steps.instruction: Can they WALK? → MINOR (Green tag). Direct walking wounded to treatment area.
steps.helpDetail: If they can walk, they're the lowest priority. Don't waste time assessing further.

steps.instruction: Are they BREATHING? No → reposition airway. Still no → EXPECTANT (Black tag).
steps.helpDetail: One attempt to open the airway. If they breathe: proceed. If not: move on. You cannot do CPR during mass casualty.

steps.instruction: Respiratory rate >30 or <10? → IMMEDIATE (Red tag).
steps.helpDetail: Fast or slow breathing = critical. Needs help right now.

steps.instruction: Can they follow commands and has radial pulse? No pulse or can't follow → IMMEDIATE (Red). Both yes → DELAYED (Yellow).
steps.helpDetail: The entire assessment takes 30 seconds. In mass casualty: treat RED first, YELLOW second, GREEN last, BLACK gets comfort only.

techniques.name: SALT Triage System
techniques.subtitle: Advanced mass casualty sorting — wave method

steps.instruction: Step 1 — Global Sorting: Shout 'Everyone who can walk, move to [location].'
steps.helpDetail: Walking wounded self-sort. Assess everyone still on the ground.

steps.instruction: Step 2 — Wave assessment: prioritize patients with obvious life threats first.
steps.helpDetail: Scan for massive hemorrhage, airway obstruction, or breathing difficulty.

steps.instruction: Step 3 — Individual triage using SALT: does the patient have a pulse and follow commands?
steps.helpDetail: Sort into: Immediate (life threat), Delayed (serious but stable), Minimal (minor), Expectant (unsurvivable).

steps.instruction: Assign colored tags and direct resources to IMMEDIATE patients first.
steps.helpDetail: SALT improves on START by adding the 'expectant' category for patients who cannot survive given available resources.

techniques.name: Frostbite Rewarming Protocol
techniques.subtitle: Controlled rewarming of frozen tissue — timing is critical

steps.instruction: DO NOT rewarm if there is any chance of REFREEZING.
steps.helpDetail: Thaw-refreeze cycle causes far worse tissue damage than staying frozen. Only rewarm when you can guarantee the tissue stays warm.

steps.instruction: Prepare water bath at exactly 98-102°F (37-39°C).
steps.helpDetail: Use a thermometer if possible. Water should feel warm to inner wrist, not hot.

steps.instruction: Immerse affected part for 30-45 minutes. Maintain water temperature.
steps.helpDetail: The tissue will turn red/purple and become extremely painful. This is expected. Pain medication before rewarming is ideal.

steps.instruction: After rewarming: DO NOT rub, massage, or walk on thawed feet.
steps.helpDetail: Protect with loose, sterile dressings with padding between fingers/toes. Blisters are expected — do not pop clear blisters. Hemorrhagic (blood) blisters indicate deep tissue damage.

techniques.name: Anaphylaxis Emergency Protocol
techniques.subtitle: Severe allergic reaction — epinephrine is the ONLY treatment

steps.instruction: Recognize: rapid onset of hives/swelling + breathing difficulty or low blood pressure.
steps.helpDetail: Can be triggered by insect stings, foods, or medications. AIRWAY SWELLING can kill in minutes.

steps.instruction: Use epinephrine auto-injector (EpiPen) immediately if available — outer thigh, through clothing.
steps.helpDetail: Dose: 0.3mg adult, 0.15mg child. Can repeat in 5-15 minutes if no improvement.

steps.instruction: Position: sitting up if breathing difficulty, lying down with legs elevated if low blood pressure.
steps.helpDetail: If both: semi-reclined position is a compromise.

steps.instruction: Antihistamines (Benadryl) are SECOND-LINE, NOT a replacement for epinephrine.
steps.helpDetail: Benadryl helps but works too slowly for anaphylaxis. Epinephrine is the only drug that reverses anaphylaxis.

techniques.name: Sucking Chest Wound Seal
techniques.subtitle: Open pneumothorax — seal with occlusive dressing

steps.instruction: Recognize: bubbling or sucking sound from chest wound during breathing.
steps.helpDetail: Air enters the chest through the wound instead of through the trachea.

steps.instruction: Apply an occlusive (airtight) dressing: plastic wrapper, petroleum gauze, emergency chest seal.
steps.helpDetail: Any non-porous material: plastic bag, duct tape over gauze, credit card.

steps.instruction: Tape on THREE sides, leaving the bottom untaped as a flutter valve.
steps.helpDetail: Inhale: the seal closes (air can't enter). Exhale: the bottom lifts (trapped air can escape).

steps.instruction: Monitor for tension pneumothorax: worsening breathing, distended neck veins.
steps.helpDetail: If tension develops: briefly lift the seal to release trapped air, then reseal. Evacuate immediately.

techniques.name: Finger Dislocation Reduction
techniques.subtitle: Relocate a dislocated finger — common wilderness injury

steps.instruction: Confirm dislocation: finger is obviously crooked at a joint, painful, unable to bend.
steps.helpDetail: Compare to the same finger on the other hand. If in doubt, splint and evacuate.

steps.instruction: Grip the dislocated segment firmly. Pull steady traction ALONG the finger axis.
steps.helpDetail: Do not yank. Steady, sustained pull while guiding the joint back into alignment.

steps.instruction: As traction is applied, gently guide the tip back into normal alignment.
steps.helpDetail: You should feel/hear a clunk as it seats. Immediate pain relief confirms success.

steps.instruction: Buddy-tape to adjacent finger for 2-3 weeks. Ice and ibuprofen for swelling.
steps.helpDetail: If it won't reduce after 2 attempts: stop and splint. Continuing causes tissue damage.

techniques.name: Hemostatic Agent Application
techniques.subtitle: Combat gauze and clotting agent use for severe bleeding

steps.instruction: Pack the wound channel DEEP with hemostatic gauze.
steps.helpDetail: Get the agent INTO the wound, not just on the surface. Use a finger to push it to the bottom of the wound cavity.

steps.instruction: Continue packing until wound is completely filled.
steps.helpDetail: The hemostatic agent needs contact with the bleeding vessel to work.

steps.instruction: Apply direct pressure over the packed wound for 3+ minutes.
steps.helpDetail: Hold firm, steady pressure. Don't release to check.

steps.instruction: Cover with standard bandage, pressure bandage, or elastic wrap.
steps.helpDetail: Two main types: QuikClot (kaolin) and Celox (chitosan). Both accelerate clotting on contact with blood. Carry in any wilderness first aid kit.

techniques.name: Improvised Chest Seal from Plastic
techniques.subtitle: Seal penetrating chest wound with everyday items

steps.instruction: Find any non-porous material: credit card, plastic bag, duct tape, foil wrapper.
steps.helpDetail: Anything airtight works. Size should cover the wound plus 2 inches on all sides.

steps.instruction: Wipe blood from skin around wound to help the seal stick.
steps.helpDetail: The seal needs to adhere to skin, not blood.

steps.instruction: Apply the material over the wound. Tape on THREE sides only.
steps.helpDetail: Three-sided seal acts as a flutter valve — air escapes on exhale, can't enter on inhale.

steps.instruction: Monitor for tension pneumothorax: worsening breathing, distended neck veins.
steps.helpDetail: If tension develops, briefly lift the untaped edge to burp trapped air, then reseal.

techniques.name: Traumatic Amputation Field Care
techniques.subtitle: Manage a severed body part and the patient

steps.instruction: Control bleeding from the stump: tourniquet high and tight, or direct pressure + packing.
steps.helpDetail: Bleeding control is the first priority. Apply tourniquet 2-3 inches above the amputation.

steps.instruction: Recover the amputated part if safe to do so.
steps.helpDetail: Search briefly but don't risk other lives.

steps.instruction: Wrap the amputated part in moist gauze, seal in a plastic bag.
steps.helpDetail: Do NOT put directly on ice — tissue damage. Place the sealed bag ON TOP of ice or cold water.

steps.instruction: Transport patient and part together to the nearest surgical facility.
steps.helpDetail: Replantation is possible up to 6 hours warm time, 12+ hours if cooled. Fingers have the best replantation success rates.

techniques.name: Seizure Management (Field)
techniques.subtitle: Protect the patient during and after a seizure

steps.instruction: DO NOT restrain the patient or put anything in their mouth.
steps.helpDetail: The old advice about swallowing the tongue is a myth. Restraining causes injuries.

steps.instruction: Clear the area of hard/sharp objects. Cushion the head.
steps.helpDetail: Move furniture away. Place a folded jacket under the head.

steps.instruction: Time the seizure. Turn on side (recovery position) after convulsions stop.
steps.helpDetail: Most seizures last 1-3 minutes. Seizure >5 minutes = emergency.

steps.instruction: Stay with patient during the postictal (recovery) phase — they'll be confused and sleepy.
steps.helpDetail: Don't give food or water until fully alert. If first seizure ever, or seizure >5 min, or no return to consciousness: EVACUATE.

techniques.name: Mild Allergic Reaction Management
techniques.subtitle: Hives and itching without breathing difficulty

steps.instruction: Identify trigger and remove it: stop eating the food, remove the stinger, move away from plants.
steps.helpDetail: Continuing exposure worsens the reaction.

steps.instruction: Antihistamine: diphenhydramine (Benadryl) 25-50mg for adults.
steps.helpDetail: Takes 15-30 minutes to work. Can cause drowsiness.

steps.instruction: Cool compress on hives for comfort.
steps.helpDetail: Cold reduces itching and swelling.

steps.instruction: Monitor for 4+ hours for progression to anaphylaxis: throat tightening, breathing difficulty, dizziness.
steps.helpDetail: Mild reactions can escalate. If ANY breathing difficulty develops, this becomes anaphylaxis — use epinephrine immediately.

techniques.name: Severe Nosebleed (Epistaxis) Control
techniques.subtitle: Stop persistent nosebleed in the field

steps.instruction: Sit upright, lean slightly FORWARD. Do NOT tilt head back.
steps.helpDetail: Leaning back causes blood to flow down the throat, risking aspiration and nausea.

steps.instruction: Pinch the SOFT part of the nose firmly for 15 continuous minutes.
steps.helpDetail: Don't release to check. Full 15 minutes of sustained pressure.

steps.instruction: If still bleeding after 15 minutes: pack with gauze or clean cloth strips.
steps.helpDetail: Insert gauze gently into the nostril. The packing provides internal pressure.

steps.instruction: Apply cold compress to bridge of nose and back of neck.
steps.helpDetail: Cold causes vasoconstriction. Seek medical help if bleeding continues >30 minutes or is from a head injury.

techniques.name: Heat Stroke Emergency Cooling
techniques.subtitle: Core temp >104°F — cool AGGRESSIVELY or brain damage occurs

steps.instruction: Recognize: hot DRY skin (sweating has stopped), altered mental status, core temp >104°F.
steps.helpDetail: Heat stroke = cooling system FAILURE. This is a true emergency.

steps.instruction: COOL BY ANY MEANS NECESSARY: immerse in cold water, pack ice in armpits/groin/neck.
steps.helpDetail: Cold water immersion is the gold standard. Every minute of delay increases mortality.

steps.instruction: If no cold water: spray/pour water on skin and fan aggressively.
steps.helpDetail: Evaporative cooling is second-best. Maximize airflow over wet skin.

steps.instruction: Stop cooling when core temp reaches 102°F to avoid overshoot hypothermia.
steps.helpDetail: Transport immediately. Heat stroke causes organ damage — kidneys, brain, liver. Even after cooling, patient needs hospital monitoring.

techniques.name: Asthma Attack (No Inhaler)
techniques.subtitle: Manage bronchospasm without medication

steps.instruction: Sit upright — NEVER lie down during an asthma attack.
steps.helpDetail: Sit upright — NEVER lie down during an asthma attack.

steps.instruction: Lean forward with hands on knees (tripod position).
steps.helpDetail: Lean forward with hands on knees (tripod position).

steps.instruction: Pursed-lip breathing: inhale through nose 2 sec, exhale through pursed lips 4 sec — reduces air trapping.
steps.helpDetail: Pursed-lip breathing: inhale through nose 2 sec, exhale through pursed lips 4 sec — reduces air trapping.

steps.instruction: Hot steam (boiling water, face over pot with towel) can relax bronchial muscles.
steps.helpDetail: Caffeine (strong coffee/tea) is a mild bronchodilator — 2 cups provides measurable effect. Remain calm — panic worsens bronchospasm. If lips turn blue: this is respiratory failure, evacuate immediately.

techniques.name: Kidney Stone Pain Management (Field)
techniques.subtitle: Manage excruciating flank pain without hospital access

steps.instruction: Recognize: sudden severe pain in flank/back radiating to groin, nausea, bloody urine.
steps.helpDetail: Recognize: sudden severe pain in flank/back radiating to groin, nausea, bloody urine.

steps.instruction: Hydrate aggressively: 2-3 liters per hour if possible — volume helps push the stone.
steps.helpDetail: Hydrate aggressively: 2-3 liters per hour if possible — volume helps push the stone.

steps.instruction: Pain management: ibuprofen 800mg (reduces ureteral spasm) combined with acetaminophen.
steps.helpDetail: Pain management: ibuprofen 800mg (reduces ureteral spasm) combined with acetaminophen.

steps.instruction: Hot compress on the affected flank — heat relaxes ureteral smooth muscle.
steps.helpDetail: Walk or bounce gently — movement helps stone migration. Most stones under 5mm pass spontaneously in 1-4 weeks. If fever develops: this indicates infection behind the stone — MEDICAL EMERGENCY.

techniques.name: Eye Foreign Body Removal
techniques.subtitle: Remove debris from the eye safely

steps.instruction: DO NOT rub the eye — this embeds the particle deeper.
steps.helpDetail: DO NOT rub the eye — this embeds the particle deeper.

steps.instruction: Pull upper lid over lower lid — lashes may sweep the particle free.
steps.helpDetail: Pull upper lid over lower lid — lashes may sweep the particle free.

steps.instruction: Flush with clean water from inner corner outward.
steps.helpDetail: Flush with clean water from inner corner outward.

steps.instruction: If visible on white of eye: use a wet cotton swab to gently lift off.
steps.helpDetail: If on cornea (colored part): DO NOT attempt removal — cover both eyes loosely and evacuate. Check under upper lid by flipping it over a cotton swab. After removal: flush, patch loosely, use antibiotic drops if available.

techniques.name: Field Constipation Management
techniques.subtitle: Common wilderness problem — dietary and mechanical solutions

steps.instruction: Caused by: dehydration, change in diet, stress, lack of fiber, reduced activity.
steps.helpDetail: Caused by: dehydration, change in diet, stress, lack of fiber, reduced activity.

steps.instruction: Treatment: increase water intake dramatically — 3+ liters per day.
steps.helpDetail: Treatment: increase water intake dramatically — 3+ liters per day.

steps.instruction: Eat high-fiber foods: bark cambium, leafy greens, roots, berries with seeds.
steps.helpDetail: Eat high-fiber foods: bark cambium, leafy greens, roots, berries with seeds.

steps.instruction: Warm water first thing in morning stimulates gastric reflex.
steps.helpDetail: Abdominal massage: clockwise circles following the colon path. Squat position (not sitting) provides optimal pelvic alignment for evacuation. If severe: boiled bark tea from cascara or senna provides laxative effect.

techniques.name: Anxiety/Panic Attack Management in Wilderness
techniques.subtitle: Differentiate from medical emergency and manage

steps.instruction: Symptoms mimic heart attack: chest tightness, racing heart, numbness, breathing difficulty, doom feeling.
steps.helpDetail: Key difference: panic attacks do NOT cause sweating on one side, arm pain, or jaw pain

steps.instruction: 5-4-3-2-1 grounding: name 5 things you see, 4 you touch, 3 you hear, 2 you smell, 1 you taste.
steps.helpDetail: Box breathing: 4 in, 4 hold, 4 out, 4 hold — repeat 5 minutes

steps.instruction: Acknowledge: 'This is a panic attack.
steps.helpDetail: I am not dying

steps.instruction: It will pass in 20 minutes.' Physical grounding: hold ice or cold water, do push-ups, walk briskly.
steps.helpDetail: Panic attacks are terrifying but NOT dangerous.

techniques.name: Toothache Emergency Relief
techniques.subtitle: Manage severe dental pain in the field

steps.instruction: Clove oil (eugenol) is the strongest natural dental anesthetic — apply directly to cavity or gum.
steps.helpDetail: Clove oil (eugenol) is the strongest natural dental anesthetic — apply directly to cavity or gum.

steps.instruction: Oil of oregano is a backup — strong antibacterial and mild anesthetic.
steps.helpDetail: Oil of oregano is a backup — strong antibacterial and mild anesthetic.

steps.instruction: Ibuprofen 400mg + acetaminophen 500mg together (this combination exceeds either alone — proven in studies).
steps.helpDetail: Ibuprofen 400mg + acetaminophen 500mg together (this combination exceeds either alone — proven in studies).

steps.instruction: Warm salt water rinse every 2 hours.
steps.helpDetail: If a cavity is exposed: pack with temporary filling, beeswax, or sugar-free gum to seal from air exposure. AVOID aspirin directly on the gum — it causes chemical burn to oral tissue.

techniques.name: UTI Management in the Field
techniques.subtitle: Manage urinary tract infection without antibiotics

steps.instruction: Recognize: burning urination, frequent urges, cloudy/bloody urine, lower abdominal pain.
steps.helpDetail: Recognize: burning urination, frequent urges, cloudy/bloody urine, lower abdominal pain.

steps.instruction: Flush aggressively: 3+ liters of water per day — dilutes bacteria and flushes the tract.
steps.helpDetail: Flush aggressively: 3+ liters of water per day — dilutes bacteria and flushes the tract.

steps.instruction: Urinate frequently — do not hold it.
steps.helpDetail: Urinate frequently — do not hold it.

steps.instruction: Cranberry if available (prevents bacteria from adhering to bladder wall).
steps.helpDetail: Keep area clean and dry. If fever develops, back pain, or blood in urine increases: the infection may have reached the kidneys — this requires antibiotics and evacuation.

techniques.name: Mild Hypothermia Self-Treatment
techniques.subtitle: Recognize and treat your own early hypothermia

steps.instruction: Recognize in yourself: uncontrollable shivering, fumbling hands, stumbling, confused thinking.
steps.helpDetail: Act IMMEDIATELY — your decision-making degrades rapidly from this point

steps.instruction: Get out of wind and wet — shelter is priority one.
steps.helpDetail: Remove wet clothing, replace with dry

steps.instruction: Create insulation between yourself and the ground.
steps.helpDetail: If possible: hot sweet drink (sugar provides metabolic fuel for heat production)

steps.instruction: Exercise vigorously if you can — generate metabolic heat.
steps.helpDetail: Get into a sleeping bag WITH another warm person if available. The window for self-rescue is SHORT — once moderate hypothermia sets in, you cannot help yourself.

techniques.name: HAPE (High Altitude Pulmonary Edema) Protocol
techniques.subtitle: Recognizing and treating fluid in the lungs at elevation

steps.instruction: Recognize the symptoms of HAPE.
steps.helpDetail: Usually occurs above 8000ft, 2-4 days after arrival. Symptoms: profound fatigue, breathlessness AT REST, dry cough that becomes wet/pink frothy sputum, gurgling in chest.

steps.instruction: DESCEND IMMEDIATELY.
steps.helpDetail: This is the only definitive cure. Descend at least 1500 to 3000 feet (500-1000m) or until symptoms resolve. Do not wait for morning. Delay is frequently fatal.

steps.instruction: Minimize the patient's physical exertion during descent.
steps.helpDetail: Carrying or assisting the patient is critical. Exertion dramatically increases pulmonary artery pressure, worsening the fluid leak into the lungs.

steps.instruction: Administer OXYGEN if available, or use a Gamow Bag.
steps.helpDetail: Nifedipine (calcium channel blocker) can lower pulmonary pressure if available. Keep the patient warm, as cold stress also raises pulmonary pressure.

techniques.name: HACE (High Altitude Cerebral Edema) Protocol
techniques.subtitle: Treating brain swelling at extreme altitude

steps.instruction: Recognize the hallmark sign: Ataxia (loss of coordination).
steps.helpDetail: Have the patient walk a straight line heel-to-toe. If they stagger or fall, assume HACE. Other signs: severe headache, altered mental status, lethargy.

steps.instruction: DESCEND IMMEDIATELY.
steps.helpDetail: HACE is the end-stage of acute mountain sickness and is rapidly fatal (often within 12-24 hours) if left at altitude. Descend at least 3000 feet.

steps.instruction: Administer Dexamethasone if available.
steps.helpDetail: Standard dose is 8mg initially, then 4mg every 6 hours. This steroid reduces cerebral inflammation and buys time for evacuation. It does not cure HACE — it merely masks symptoms during descent.

steps.instruction: Keep the patient's head elevated.
steps.helpDetail: Even when resting, prop the head and shoulders up 30 degrees to reduce intracranial pressure. Prevent chilling.

techniques.name: Black Widow Envenomation Protocol
techniques.subtitle: Managing severe neurotoxic spider bites

steps.instruction: Identify the bite and the spider.
steps.helpDetail: The bite is often painless initially. The spider is glossy black with a red hourglass on the ventral (belly) abdomen. The venom is highly neurotoxic (alpha-latrotoxin).

steps.instruction: Recognize systemic symptoms (Latrodectism).
steps.helpDetail: Onset 30-60 mins. Severe, agonizing muscle cramps starting nearby and spreading to the abdomen (mimics appendicitis) or chest (mimics heart attack), profuse sweating, hypertension.

steps.instruction: Clean the bite site and apply a COLD pack.
steps.helpDetail: Unlike snake bites, a cold pack reduces local pain. Do NOT use tourniquets, suction devices, or cut the wound.

steps.instruction: Manage pain and evacuate.
steps.helpDetail: The pain can last 12-48 hours and is excruciating but almost never fatal in healthy adults. OTC pain meds like ibuprofen/acetaminophen are rarely sufficient in the field; the patient needs evacuation for IV opioids/benzodiazepines or antivenin.

techniques.name: Trench Foot (Immersion Foot) Treatment
techniques.subtitle: Preventing and treating non-freezing cold injury

steps.instruction: Recognize the early stages: Tingling, numbness, and 'wood-like' feet.
steps.helpDetail: Caused by prolonged exposure (12+ hours) to wet, cold (but non-freezing) conditions (32-60°F). The foot appears pale, mottled, or cyanotic (blue), and pulse is weak.

steps.instruction: STOP walking and dry the feet immediately.
steps.helpDetail: The defining treatment is warm, dry air. Gently dry the feet. DO NOT rub or massage them, as the tissue is incredibly fragile and rubbing causes severe tissue damage.

steps.instruction: Elevate the feet and rewarm slowly at room temperature.
steps.helpDetail: DO NOT place feet near a fire, heater, or hot water. The damaged nerves cannot feel heat, and burns occur easily. Rewarming takes hours and is extremely painful (hyperemic phase: red, swollen, throbbing).

steps.instruction: Prevent recurrence.
steps.helpDetail: Trench foot takes weeks to heal and disables a survivor completely. Prevention is the only viable field strategy: sleep with dry socks, dry boots at night, apply foot powder, and change socks frequently.

techniques.name: Anaphylaxis: Epinephrine Administration
techniques.subtitle: The definitive treatment for life-threatening allergic reactions.

steps.instruction: Recognize anaphylaxis.
steps.helpDetail: Symptoms: hives, facial swelling, wheezing, throat tightness, severe hypotension, feeling of impending doom.

steps.instruction: Prepare the Epinephrine Auto-Injector (EpiPen).
steps.helpDetail: Remove the safety cap. Do NOT put your thumb over either end.

steps.instruction: Inject into the outer mid-thigh.
steps.helpDetail: Press firmly until it clicks. Hold in place for 3 to 5 seconds. Can be injected through clothing.

steps.instruction: Massage the area and prepare for a second dose.
steps.helpDetail: Massage helps absorption. Evacuate immediately. Symptoms often rebound 15-30 minutes later; a second dose is frequently required.

techniques.name: Tension Pneumothorax Recognition
techniques.subtitle: Identifying a lethal collapsed lung building pressure in the chest.

steps.instruction: Identify the mechanism of injury.
steps.helpDetail: Usually blunt force trauma to the chest, a broken rib, or a penetrating wound.

steps.instruction: Observe critical signs.
steps.helpDetail: Severe and progressive shortness of breath, unequal chest rise, jugular vein distension (bulging neck veins).

steps.instruction: Listen for absence of breath sounds.
steps.helpDetail: One side of the chest will have no breath sounds and may sound hollow (hyperresonant) when tapped.

steps.instruction: Immediate evacuation.
steps.helpDetail: Field needle decompression is an ALS skill only; basic first aid requires immediate, rapid evacuation as the condition is 100% fatal without intervention.

techniques.name: Flail Chest Stabilization
techniques.subtitle: Managing paradoxical chest wall movement from multiple broken ribs.

steps.instruction: Recognize paradoxical movement.
steps.helpDetail: When the patient inhales, a section of the ribcage sinks IN. When they exhale, it bulges OUT.

steps.instruction: Do NOT tightly bind the entire chest.
steps.helpDetail: Historically taught, strapping the chest reduces overall lung capacity and promotes deadly pneumonia.

steps.instruction: Provide bulky dressing support.
steps.helpDetail: Tape a bulky dressing or rolled clothing directly over the flail segment to stabilize it without restricting the rest of the chest.

steps.instruction: Evacuate rapidly with oxygen.
steps.helpDetail: Flail chest involves massive underlying lung contusion (bruising). The patient will likely require mechanical ventilation.

techniques.name: Combat Application Tourniquet (CAT)
techniques.subtitle: Correct deployment for massive extremity hemorrhage.

steps.instruction: Identify massive arterial bleeding.
steps.helpDetail: Bright red, spurting blood, or blood rapidly pooling from a limb.

steps.instruction: Apply the CAT 'High and Tight'.
steps.helpDetail: Place the tourniquet 2-3 inches above the wound (or optimally high on the thigh/arm if exact source is unseen). Do NOT place over a joint.

steps.instruction: Tighten the windlass until bleeding stops.
steps.helpDetail: Turn the rod until the arterial bleeding completely ceases and distal pulse is absent. It will be extremely painful.

steps.instruction: Secure the windlass and mark the time.
steps.helpDetail: Lock the rod in the clip, secure the strap, and write the application time on the patient's forehead or the tourniquet itself.

techniques.name: Field Hypoglycemia Management
techniques.subtitle: Treating dangerously low blood sugar.

steps.instruction: Recognize signs of hypoglycemia.
steps.helpDetail: Changes in mental status (irritability, confusion, lethargy), trembling, sweating, rapid heartbeat. Blood sugar < 70 mg/dL.

steps.instruction: Assess the patient's ability to swallow.
steps.helpDetail: If unconscious or unable to swallow, DO NOT give oral fluids/food (choking hazard).

steps.instruction: Administer rapid-acting carbohydrates (conscious patient).
steps.helpDetail: Give 15g of simple carbs: glucose gel, 4 glucose tablets, half a cup of juice, or hard candy.

steps.instruction: Wait 15 minutes and re-test.
steps.helpDetail: If symptoms persist, give another 15g. Once stable, provide complex carbs/protein to prevent a crash.

techniques.name: Open Fracture Management
techniques.subtitle: Stabilizing a broken bone protruding through the skin.

steps.instruction: Control bleeding first.
steps.helpDetail: Apply direct pressure around the protruding bone, NOT directly on the bone end. Use a tourniquet if arterial bleeding is present.

steps.instruction: Irrigate heavily if evacuation is delayed (>6 hours).
steps.helpDetail: Use liters of clean, disinfected (boiled/treated) water under pressure to wash dirt/debris out of the wound.

steps.instruction: Dress and splint in place.
steps.helpDetail: Cover the exposed bone with sterile, moist saline dressings to prevent bone death. Splint the limb as it lies.

steps.instruction: Do NOT attempt to push the bone back in.
steps.helpDetail: Traction may inadvertently pull the bone back into the tissue, dragging massive contaminiation deep into the leg. Evacuate aggressively.

techniques.name: Compartment Syndrome Recognition
techniques.subtitle: Identifying pressure buildup within muscle tissue.

steps.instruction: Understand the mechanism.
steps.helpDetail: Occurs after severe crush injuries, tight casts/bandages, or severe fractures. Tissue swells inside inelastic fascia.

steps.instruction: Monitor the '6 Ps'.
steps.helpDetail: Pain (out of proportion to injury), Pallor (pale skin), Paresthesia (numbness/tingling), Pulselessness, Paralysis, Poikilothermia (cold limb).

steps.instruction: Loosen all restrictive dressings.
steps.helpDetail: Immediately cut away any tight bandages, splints, or clothing.

steps.instruction: Evacuate immediately.
steps.helpDetail: This is a surgical emergency requiring a fasciotomy. Field treatment is purely recognition and rapid evacuation. The limb will die within 6-8 hours.

techniques.name: Frostbite Rapid Rewarming
techniques.subtitle: The definitive hospital protocol adapted for the field.

steps.instruction: NEVER rewarm if there is any chance of refreezing.
steps.helpDetail: Refreezing thawed tissue causes catastrophic, irreversible ice crystal damage. It is better to walk out on frozen feet than thaw them and refreeze.

steps.instruction: Prepare a water bath at exactly 99°F to 102°F (37-39°C).
steps.helpDetail: It should feel warm, not hot, to a normal uninjured hand. DO NOT use dry radiant heat (fires, exhaust pipes) as frozen tissue burns easily.

steps.instruction: Immerse the affected part completely.
steps.helpDetail: Do not allow the frostbitten tissue to touch the sides of the container. Keep water circulating and maintain the temperature.

steps.instruction: Thaw until tissue is soft and pliable.
steps.helpDetail: Takes 20-40 minutes. The process is excruciatingly painful. Ibuprofen/painkillers are essential. Blisters will form; do not pop them.

techniques.name: Giardiasis Field Management
techniques.subtitle: Treating the most common wilderness waterborne illness.

steps.instruction: Recognize the progression.
steps.helpDetail: Incubation is 1-3 weeks. Symptoms: explosive watery diarrhea, severe sulfur-tasting belching, profound bloating, and foul flatulence.

steps.instruction: Hydrate aggressively.
steps.helpDetail: Fluid loss from diarrhea is the primary threat. Use oral rehydration salts (ORS) or a mix of 1 liter treated water, 6 tsp sugar, 0.5 tsp salt.

steps.instruction: Administer antibiotics if available.
steps.helpDetail: Metronidazole (Flagyl) 500mg twice daily for 5-7 days, or Tinidazole 2g single dose (prescription required).

steps.instruction: Maintain strict hygiene.
steps.helpDetail: Giardia is transmitted via the fecal-oral route. Hand washing with SOAP (sanitizer is less effective against cysts) is critical for camp survival.

techniques.name: Rabies Exposure Protocol
techniques.subtitle: Immediate actions following a bite from a high-risk mammal.

steps.instruction: Identify high-risk vectors.
steps.helpDetail: Bats, raccoons, skunks, and foxes are primary carriers. Any unprovoked attack by a wild mammal or any direct physical contact with a bat.

steps.instruction: Scrub the wound relentlessly with SOAP and water.
steps.helpDetail: Washing the wound vigorously for 15 minutes physically flushes the virus and is the single most effective field intervention.

steps.instruction: Flush with a virucidal agent.
steps.helpDetail: Irrigate forcefully with a 10% povidone-iodine solution or alcohol if available.

steps.instruction: Evacuate for Post-Exposure Prophylaxis (PEP).
steps.helpDetail: Rabies is 100% fatal once symptoms appear. The PEP vaccine protocol MUST begin as soon as possible. Do not attempt to capture the animal.

techniques.name: Ankle Sprain (Grade II): Diagnosis
techniques.subtitle: Clinical protocol for the diagnosis phase of Ankle Sprain (Grade II).

steps.instruction: Obtain the patient's history (SAMPLE).
steps.helpDetail: Signs/Symptoms, Allergies, Medications, Past history, Last oral intake, Events leading up to injury.

steps.instruction: Perform a focused physical exam.
steps.helpDetail: Inspect, palpate, and assess functionality specifically relating to Ankle Sprain (Grade II).

steps.instruction: Identify hallmark clinical signs.
steps.helpDetail: Compare observed symptoms against the classic presentation of Ankle Sprain (Grade II) documented in Auerbach's guidelines.

steps.instruction: Rule out mimicking emergencies.
steps.helpDetail: Ensure the symptoms represent this specific condition rather than a more immediately life-threatening alternative.

techniques.name: Ankle Sprain (Grade II): Initial Stabilization
techniques.subtitle: Clinical protocol for the initial stabilization phase of Ankle Sprain (Grade II).

steps.instruction: Ensure Scene Safety and BSI.
steps.helpDetail: Ensure the environment is safe for the rescuer and apply Body Substance Isolation (gloves).

steps.instruction: Assess ABCDEs.
steps.helpDetail: Airway, Breathing, Circulation, Disability (spine/neuro), Expose/Environment. Address life threats first.

steps.instruction: Halt progressive damage from Ankle Sprain (Grade II).
steps.helpDetail: Apply immediate life-saving interventions (pressure, positioning, or environmental removal).

steps.instruction: Establish baseline vital signs.
steps.helpDetail: Record pulse, respiration rate, blood pressure (if able), and mental status (AVPU).

techniques.name: Ankle Sprain (Grade II): Field Treatment
techniques.subtitle: Clinical protocol for the field treatment phase of Ankle Sprain (Grade II).

steps.instruction: Apply definitive wilderness protocol for Ankle Sprain (Grade II).
steps.helpDetail: Use available resources to execute the standard-of-care field intervention dictated by NOLS/Wilderness Medical Society.

steps.instruction: Manage patient pain and comfort.
steps.helpDetail: Administer appropriate OTC or prescription analgesics if trained and authorized.

steps.instruction: Monitor for progression or complications.
steps.helpDetail: Reassess vitals every 15 minutes for unstable patients, comparing against the baseline.

steps.instruction: Document all interventions.
steps.helpDetail: Maintain a written medical log of times, treatments, and patient responses to hand off to definitive care.

techniques.name: Ankle Sprain (Grade II): Evacuation Criteria
techniques.subtitle: Clinical protocol for the evacuation criteria phase of Ankle Sprain (Grade II).

steps.instruction: Assess the risk matrix.
steps.helpDetail: Weigh the danger of the evacuation against the danger of the condition deteriorating in the field.

steps.instruction: Determine urgency level.
steps.helpDetail: Does Ankle Sprain (Grade II) require emergency Medevac, rapid ground evacuation, or routine walk-out?

steps.instruction: Prepare the patient for transport.
steps.helpDetail: Ensure splints are secure, dressings are clean, and the patient is protected from environmental exposure.

steps.instruction: Communicate condition to rescue assets.
steps.helpDetail: Relay patient age, sex, vitals, primary diagnosis, and interventions performed when calling for help.

techniques.name: Analgesic: Willow Bark Extraction
techniques.subtitle: Extracting salicin (natural aspirin) for pain relief.

steps.instruction: Identify Salix species (Willow).
steps.helpDetail: Usually grows near water. Narrow, lance-shaped leaves with a distinctive bitter smell when the bark is scraped.

steps.instruction: Harvest the inner bark.
steps.helpDetail: Do NOT use the brown outer bark. Shave down to the pale, green-white inner cambium layer. A strip about the size of two hands is sufficient for a strong dose.

steps.instruction: Simmer, do not boil aggressively.
steps.helpDetail: Place the inner bark in water and simmer gently for 15-20 minutes to extract the salicin compounds.

steps.instruction: Drink for pain/fever relief.
steps.helpDetail: The resulting tea contains salicin, which the liver converts to salicylic acid (the active ingredient in aspirin). It relieves headaches, muscle pain, and lowers fevers, but is gentler on the stomach than synthesized aspirin.

techniques.name: Styptic: Yarrow Poultice
techniques.subtitle: Stopping severe bleeding using Achillea millefolium.

steps.instruction: Identify Yarrow.
steps.helpDetail: Finely divided, fern-like/feathery leaves. Strong, medicinal/camphor-like smell when crushed. Distinct from toxic Hemlock which smells mousy/foul.

steps.instruction: Chew or crush the fresh leaves.
steps.helpDetail: If fresh, masticate the leaves or pound them with a rock to release the astringent juices and break down the cellular walls.

steps.instruction: Pack directly into the wound.
steps.helpDetail: Press the bleeding wound full of the crushed yarrow mass and apply heavy, direct mechanical pressure with a bandage over it.

steps.instruction: Utilize the alkaloid properties.
steps.helpDetail: Yarrow contains achilleine, a powerful alkaloid that aggressively promotes blood coagulation. Leave the poultice in place until definitive medical care is reached.

techniques.name: Herbal: Astringent Wash for Minor Lacerations
techniques.subtitle: Preparing a botanical astringent wash specifically to treat minor lacerations.

steps.instruction: Locate indicated botanical species.
steps.helpDetail: Find a verified plant with known medicinal properties relevant to minor lacerations (e.g., Plantain for stings, Yarrow for cuts).

steps.instruction: Prepare the astringent wash.
steps.helpDetail: The astringent wash process extracts the active alkaloids or tannins. Dosage control is critical in field medicine.

steps.instruction: Apply to manage minor lacerations.
steps.helpDetail: Administer topically or internally as indicated. Monitor the patient for allergic reactions to the wild plant matter.

steps.instruction: Understand limitations.
steps.helpDetail: Herbal field medicine is supportive, not definitive. It treats symptoms of emergencies but does not cure systemic shock, severe trauma, or major infections.

techniques.name: Trench Foot Prevention
techniques.subtitle: Halting continuous cold/wet tissue necrosis.

steps.instruction: Understand the mechanism.
steps.helpDetail: Trench foot (Immersion Foot) occurs when feet are cold and damp for 12+ hours. Blood vessels constrict permanently, killing the tissue without actually freezing it.

steps.instruction: Elevate and dry feet daily.
steps.helpDetail: You must take your boots off for at least 6 hours every single day. Elevate them to drain fluid. Massage them to force warm blood back into the capillaries.

steps.instruction: Rotate dry socks.
steps.helpDetail: Carry minimum 2 pairs. Wear one, keep one tucked into your armpits or crotch against your bare skin. Body heat will dry the wet socks overnight.

steps.instruction: Never apply direct high heat.
steps.helpDetail: Do not hold frozen/trench feet near a blazing fire. The compromised tissue will cook internally before you feel the pain, causing massive amputation-level damage. Re-warm against another person's stomach skin.

techniques.name: Frostbite: Thaw vs. Wait
techniques.subtitle: The critical NOLS decision matrix for freezing tissue.

steps.instruction: Assess the risk of re-freezing.
steps.helpDetail: This is the absolute rule: DO NOT thaw a frostbitten limb in the field if there is ANY chance it will freeze again. Re-freezing thawed tissue guarantees massive amputation.

steps.instruction: Determine evacuation capability.
steps.helpDetail: A patient can walk miles out of the wilderness on frozen, unfeeling feet with surprisingly little permanent damage. If you thaw their feet, the excruciating pain makes them a litter-carry immediately.

steps.instruction: Shelter-in-place scenario.
steps.helpDetail: If you are trapped in a snow cave for days with a heat source, thaw the limb. Immerse it in water exactly 104-108°F (40-42°C). Do NOT use dry, radiant heat (like a fire) as the numb tissue will suffer severe 3rd-degree burns.

steps.instruction: Protect thawed tissue.
steps.helpDetail: Once thawed, the tissue will blister massively and turn purple/black. Do not pop blisters. Heavily bandage with sterile, non-adherent dressing, elevate perfectly, and do not let them use the limb.

techniques.name: Avalanche Burial Recovery
techniques.subtitle: Resuscitation protocol for extricated avalanche victims.

steps.instruction: Dig to the airway immediately.
steps.helpDetail: Do not uncover the feet first. Shovel directly toward the head to establish an airway. Time is measured in minutes before asphyxiation/CO2 poisoning.

steps.instruction: Clear the mouth of snow plug.
steps.helpDetail: Victims often have an 'ice mask' packed tightly into their mouth and throat. Vigorously scoop this out before attempting rescue breaths.

steps.instruction: Assess pulse carefully.
steps.helpDetail: Severe hypothermia causes profound bradycardia (a heart rate of 10-20 beats per minute). Check for a pulse for a full 60 seconds. A hasty 5-second check can lead to unnecessary, traumatic chest compressions.

steps.instruction: Handle with extreme gentleness.
steps.helpDetail: A severely cold heart is highly irritable. Rough handling (bouncing, dropping) can instantly induce ventricular fibrillation (cardiac arrest). Treat them like fragile glass.

techniques.name: Self-Evacuation for Mild Hypothermia
techniques.subtitle: NOLS protocols for initiating a self-evacuation when a patient presents with mild hypothermia (shivering).

steps.instruction: Establish Mild Hypothermia presentation.
steps.helpDetail: Verify the patient's core temperature and mental status. In mild hypothermia (shivering), the brain's ability to regulate the body or cooperate changes drastically.

steps.instruction: Commit to the Self-Evacuation.
steps.helpDetail: If executing a self-evacuation, ensure the patient's condition matches the physical demands and the environmental reality of the route.

steps.instruction: Package the Mild Hypothermia patient.
steps.helpDetail: A patient with mild hypothermia (shivering) requires immense thermal protection. Ensure they will not lose further massive core heat during the self-evacuation process.

steps.instruction: Monitor continuously.
steps.helpDetail: Hypothermia operates on a deadly sliding scale. The patient may rapidly degrade from mild to severe during the transport, requiring immediate intervention.

techniques.name: Differentiating Heat Exhaustion vs Stroke
techniques.subtitle: The critical diagnostic line separating rest and immediate death.

steps.instruction: Check the mental status.
steps.helpDetail: This is the primary diagnostic. Heat Exhaustion patients are extremely miserable, nauseous, and hot, but they are LUCID. Heat Stroke patients are profoundly altered (combative, hallucinating, seizing, unconscious).

steps.instruction: Check the skin condition.
steps.helpDetail: Heat Exhaustion: Skin is usually pale, cool, and profusely sweating. Heat Stroke: Skin is often flushed cherry-red, screaming hot to the touch, and frequently (but not always) bone dry.

steps.instruction: Treat Exhaustion with rest and hydration.
steps.helpDetail: Move the exhaustion patient into shade, elevate legs, and slowly administer cool water with electrolytes. Do not force them to chug. They will recover in 2-4 hours.

steps.instruction: Treat Stroke with immediate, utterly aggressive cooling.
steps.helpDetail: Heat stroke has a 50% mortality rate even in ICUs. Strip the patient naked. Pour ALL available water over them continuously while fanning vigorously. DO NOT give oral fluids to an unconscious patient.

techniques.name: Pit Viper Envenomation Protocol
techniques.subtitle: Managing bites from Rattlesnakes, Copperheads, and Cottonmouths.

steps.instruction: Do no further harm.
steps.helpDetail: Do NOT cut the wound. Do NOT suck out the venom. Do NOT use electricity. Do NOT apply ice (which worsens tissue necrosis). Do NOT apply a rigid tourniquet (which concentrates venom and guarantees amputation).

steps.instruction: Keep the patient calm and immobile.
steps.helpDetail: Heart rate spreads the venom. Have the patient lie completely still. Carry them if possible. Do NOT let them walk unless absolutely necessary for survival.

steps.instruction: Splint the bitten extremity.
steps.helpDetail: Immobilize the bitten arm or leg exactly like a broken bone. Keep it AT heart level—not elevated above (accelerates systemic spread), and not hanging below (accelerates massive localized swelling).

steps.instruction: Mark swelling progression with a pen.
steps.helpDetail: Draw a line around the leading edge of the swelling every 15 minutes and write the time next to it. Provide this critical timeline data to the receiving ER physician to determine antivenom (CroFab) dosages.

techniques.name: Water Intoxication (Hyponatremia)
techniques.subtitle: Treating massive sodium depletion from extreme sweating.

steps.instruction: Recognize the paradoxical symptoms.
steps.helpDetail: The patient has been drinking massive amounts of pure water while sweating heavily, but still feels nauseous, exhausted, develops a severe headache, and may begin seizing. They are literally drowning their brain cells.

steps.instruction: Stop all pure water intake.
steps.helpDetail: Their blood sodium is dangerously low. Giving them more plain water will push the brain into fatal swelling (cerebral edema).

steps.instruction: Start immediate, aggressive salt replacement.
steps.helpDetail: Give them extremely salty foods (pretzel dust, broth, bouillon cubes). In severe cases, they may need to sip concentrated salt water.

steps.instruction: Monitor for altered mental status.
steps.helpDetail: If they begin slurring words, acting drunk, or vomiting, they are entering a life-threatening hyponatremic crisis requiring immediate helicopter medevac.

techniques.name: Heat Illness: Heat Cramps
techniques.subtitle: Clinical presentation and field management for heat cramps.

steps.instruction: Identify Heat Cramps symptoms.
steps.helpDetail: Evaluate the patient's core temperature, sweating mechanism, and cardiovascular response to confirm heat cramps.

steps.instruction: Initiate Heat Cramps cooling protocol.
steps.helpDetail: Move the patient rapidly out of direct radiant sun and begin evaporation-based cooling.

steps.instruction: Manage hydration for heat cramps.
steps.helpDetail: If the patient is conscious and can swallow, slowly introduce oral rehydration salts (ORS). Observe for vomiting.

steps.instruction: Determine evacuation urgency.
steps.helpDetail: Evaluate if heat cramps has crossed the threshold into life-threatening core hyperthermia requiring immediate medevac.

techniques.name: Arid Injury: Scorpion
techniques.subtitle: Auerbach protocols for managing a scorpion sting in the field.

steps.instruction: Isolate the Scorpion wound.
steps.helpDetail: Immediately move the patient away from the source of the scorpion sting to prevent secondary strikes or further contamination.

steps.instruction: Assess the Scorpion damage.
steps.helpDetail: Determine if the scorpion sting has introduced systemic neurotoxins or purely localized necrotic/mechanical damage.

steps.instruction: Implement Scorpion field care.
steps.helpDetail: Wash thoroughly with soap and water. Never apply tourniquets or suction devices to venomous bites; they drastically increase local tissue destruction.

steps.instruction: Plan evacuation for scorpion sting.
steps.helpDetail: Monitor systemic signs (breathing difficulty, massive swelling, muscle spasms). Initiate rapid transport if systemic envenomation is confirmed.

articles.title: Controlling Bleeding
articles.body:
Blood loss kills faster than almost any other trauma. A person can bleed to death from a severed femoral artery in 3-5 minutes.

Bleeding control priority:
1. Direct pressure — your hands and a cloth on the wound
2. Pressure bandage — wrap tightly (Israeli/emergency bandage)
3. Tourniquet — for life-threatening limb bleeding only
4. Wound packing — for junctional areas (groin, armpit, neck)

Tourniquet myths debunked: Modern evidence shows tourniquets can stay on for 6+ hours without limb loss. The old 'you'll lose the limb' teaching is wrong. A loose tourniquet is more dangerous than a tight one — it blocks venous return while allowing arterial flow, increasing bleeding.

Wound packing: For deep wounds that won't stop bleeding, pack gauze INTO the wound, pressing firmly against the bleeding vessel. This feels wrong but saves lives.

articles.title: CPR: What You Need to Know
articles.body:
Compression-only CPR (hands-only) is nearly as effective as full CPR for adults who collapse suddenly. If you're unwilling or unable to give mouth-to-mouth, compressions alone still save lives.

Key numbers:
- 100-120 compressions per minute (beat of 'Stayin' Alive')
- 2 inches deep minimum for adults
- Full chest recoil between compressions
- 30 compressions : 2 breaths ratio

Quality degrades rapidly. Switch rescuers every 2 minutes. Most people push too shallow after 1 minute of fatigue.

For children: same ratio but use one hand. For infants: two fingers, 1.5 inches deep.

AED (Automated External Defibrillator): If available, use immediately. They are designed for untrained users. Open the case and follow voice prompts. You cannot hurt someone with an AED — it only shocks if needed.

articles.title: Fracture Recognition & Management
articles.body:
Assume a fracture exists if there is:
- Deformity (limb looks wrong)
- Swelling at a specific point
- Crepitus (grinding sensation)
- Loss of function
- Point tenderness (pain at one specific spot when pressed)

Open fracture (bone visible through skin): This is a surgical emergency. Cover the wound with a moist sterile dressing. Do NOT push bone back in. Splint and evacuate.

Femur fractures are life-threatening — the thigh can hold 1-2 liters of internal blood. Watch for shock.

General splinting rules:
- Splint in position found
- Immobilize joint above AND below the break
- Pad all bony prominences
- Check circulation every 15 minutes
- Never straighten a deformity in the field

articles.title: Burn Classification & Treatment
articles.body:
Burns are classified by depth:

1st degree (superficial): Red, painful, no blisters. Like a sunburn. Treat with cool water and aloe vera. Heals in 3-5 days.

2nd degree (partial thickness): Red, painful, blisters present. Very painful because nerve endings are intact but exposed. Cool water 20 min, do not pop blisters, clean bandage. Heals in 2-3 weeks.

3rd degree (full thickness): White/brown/black, often painless (nerves are destroyed). Requires skin grafting. Do not cool — wrap and evacuate.

The Rule of Nines for estimating burn area:
- Head: 9%
- Each arm: 9%
- Chest front: 18%
- Back: 18%
- Each leg: 18%
- Groin: 1%

Burns over 20% body surface area require IV fluid resuscitation. This is a hospital emergency.

articles.title: Venomous Bites & Stings
articles.body:
Snake bites: Only 20% of bites from venomous snakes actually envenomate (dry bites are common). But ALWAYS treat as envenomated until proven otherwise. The only treatment is antivenom at a hospital. Everything else (cutting, sucking, tourniquet, electric shock) makes it worse.

Spider bites: Brown recluse causes tissue necrosis — clean, ice, and evacuate. Black widow causes muscle cramps and pain — seek medical care but rarely fatal in adults.

Scorpion stings: Most are painful but not dangerous. Exception: Bark scorpion (southwestern US) can cause neurological symptoms in children. Seek medical care.

Tick-borne diseases: Lyme disease (bull's-eye rash), Rocky Mountain Spotted Fever (spotted rash on palms/soles), and many others. Early antibiotic treatment is critical.

articles.title: Environmental Emergencies
articles.body:
Hypothermia stages:
- Mild (95-89°F): Shivering, confusion, poor coordination
- Moderate (89-82°F): Shivering stops, severe confusion, drowsiness
- Severe (<82°F): Unconscious, very slow pulse, appears dead

The phrase 'nobody is dead until they're warm and dead' exists because severely hypothermic patients have survived with no detectable pulse. Continue CPR and rewarm.

Heat illness spectrum:
Heat cramps → Heat exhaustion → Heat stroke

The critical difference: heat exhaustion patients are sweating. Heat stroke patients have stopped sweating. Heat stroke = emergency department NOW.

Drowning: Start CPR immediately. 5 rescue breaths first (water in lungs prevents oxygen exchange). Do not attempt to drain water. Begin standard CPR protocol. People have survived 30+ minutes of submersion in cold water.

Lightning: Lightning victims do NOT carry a charge. They are safe to touch. Start CPR immediately. Lightning strikes have high survival rates with prompt CPR.

articles.title: Anaphylaxis & Allergic Reactions
articles.body:
Anaphylaxis is a whole-body allergic reaction that can kill in minutes. Common triggers: bee stings, peanuts, shellfish, medications, latex.

Symptom progression:
1. Skin: hives, flushing, itching
2. Swelling: face, lips, tongue, throat
3. Respiratory: wheezing, stridor, shortness of breath
4. Cardiovascular: rapid pulse, low blood pressure, dizziness
5. GI: nausea, vomiting, diarrhea

Epinephrine is the ONLY first-line treatment. Antihistamines (Benadryl) are too slow to save a life. EpiPen goes in the outer thigh, through clothing is fine.

Biphasic reaction: Up to 20% of anaphylaxis patients have a second wave 1-72 hours later. Hospital observation for at least 4 hours is essential.

articles.title: Survival First Aid Kit Essentials
articles.body:
Minimum wilderness first aid kit:

Bleeding control:
- Tourniquet (CAT or SOFTT-W)
- Israeli bandage
- Hemostatic gauze (QuikClot)
- Roller gauze (2x)
- Medical tape

Wound care:
- Irrigation syringe
- Wound closure strips (Steri-Strips)
- Tincture of benzoin
- Antibiotic ointment
- Non-stick pads

Medications:
- Ibuprofen 200mg
- Diphenhydramine 25mg (Benadryl)
- Loperamide (Imodium)
- Electrolyte packets
- Epinephrine auto-injector (if prescribed)

Tools:
- Trauma shears
- Fine-point tweezers
- Safety pins
- Nitrile gloves (4+ pairs)
- SAM splint
- Moleskin
- Elastic bandage (ACE wrap)
- Emergency blanket

All items should be checked and refreshed annually. Medications expire. Adhesives degrade.

articles.title: Triage & Treatment Priorities
articles.body:
When someone is injured, assess in this order (MARCH):

M — Massive hemorrhage: Stop life-threatening bleeding FIRST. This kills fastest.

A — Airway: Is the airway open? Head tilt, chin lift. Clear obstructions.

R — Respiration: Are they breathing? Look, listen, feel. Start rescue breathing if not.

C — Circulation: Is there a pulse? Start CPR if not. Treat for shock.

H — Hypothermia/Head injury: Prevent heat loss. Assess level of consciousness.

The biggest mistake in field medicine is treating the dramatic wound while missing the critical one. A tourniquet on a spurting artery takes priority over a broken arm.

Scene safety: ALWAYS ensure the scene is safe before providing care. You cannot help if you become a victim. Check for traffic, fire, electrical hazards, aggressive animals, or ongoing violence.

articles.title: Wound Infection Prevention
articles.body:
In survival, infection is the slow killer. Every wound becomes a race against bacteria.

Signs of infection (usually appear 24-72 hours after injury):
- Increasing redness spreading from wound edges
- Warmth around the wound
- Swelling that worsens over time
- Pus (white, yellow, or green drainage)
- Red streaks radiating from the wound (lymphangitis — URGENT)
- Fever and chills (systemic infection — EMERGENCY)

Prevention steps:
1. Clean the wound immediately with clean water (irrigation pressure matters more than sterility)
2. Remove all debris and foreign material
3. Apply antibiotic ointment if available
4. Change dressings every 12-24 hours
5. Keep the wound dry between changes

Field antiseptics when nothing else is available:
- Diluted iodine (2-3 drops per liter of clean water)
- Honey (raw honey has natural antibacterial properties)
- Salt water (1 teaspoon per cup)
- Clean urine is NOT antiseptic — this is a myth

Critical rule: a wound that is getting worse after 48 hours needs antibiotics or surgical care. Field treatment can only buy time.

articles.title: Pediatric Emergencies
articles.body:
Children are NOT small adults. Their physiology differs in critical ways that affect emergency treatment.

Key differences:
- Higher heart rate (infant: 120-160 bpm is NORMAL)
- Higher respiratory rate (infant: 30-60 breaths/min is NORMAL)
- Proportionally larger head (higher spinal injury risk)
- Less blood volume (small blood loss = big problem)
- Faster dehydration (higher surface area to volume ratio)
- Better compensation until sudden collapse (children 'look fine' until they crash)

CPR modifications:
- Infant (under 1 year): 2 fingers, 1.5 inches deep, 30:2 ratio
- Child (1-8 years): 1 hand, 2 inches deep, 30:2 ratio
- Ventilation: gentle puffs only — too much pressure damages small lungs

Choking:
- Infant: 5 back blows + 5 chest thrusts (NO abdominal thrusts)
- Child over 1: standard Heimlich maneuver

Psychological first aid:
- Children respond to calm, reassuring voices
- Let them hold a parent or familiar object
- Explain what you're doing in simple terms
- Never lie about pain — say 'this will hurt but it will help you'
- Their fear can escalate an emergency if not managed

articles.title: Improvised Medical Tools
articles.body:
When proper medical supplies aren't available, improvisation saves lives.

Improvised tourniquet:
- Material: belt, strip of cloth, bandana (at least 1.5 inches wide)
- Windlass: stick, pen, spoon handle
- Apply 2-3 inches above wound, tighten until bleeding stops
- Mark time of application on patient's forehead

Improvised splint materials:
- SAM splint alternatives: folded cardboard, newspaper, sticks, trekking poles
- Padding: clothing, moss, leaves
- Ties: torn fabric strips, shoelaces, tape, paracord

Improvised wound closure:
- Butterfly strips from tape (cut into hourglass shape)
- Super glue on SUPERFICIAL cuts only (not deep wounds)
- Hair: tie strands across the wound to hold edges together
- Thorns as suture pins in extreme situations

Improvised irrigation syringe:
- Plastic bag with a small hole poked in the corner
- Water bottle with hole in cap
- Goal: pressurized stream to flush debris from wounds

Improvised cervical collar:
- Rolled towel or jacket wrapped around neck
- Secured with tape, strips, or safety pins
- Must NOT compress the airway

articles.title: Understanding Crush Syndrome
articles.body:
Crush syndrome is one of the most counterintuitive killers in disaster medicine. The patient seems fine while trapped — then dies within minutes of being freed.

What happens:
- Prolonged pressure on muscles (1+ hours) causes cell death
- Dead muscle cells accumulate potassium, myoglobin, and lactate
- When pressure is released, these toxins flood the bloodstream
- Potassium stops the heart. Myoglobin destroys the kidneys.

Timeline of danger:
- Crush time < 1 hour: usually safe to release immediately
- Crush time 1-4 hours: moderate risk. Tourniquet before release if possible
- Crush time 4+ hours: high risk. Do NOT release without medical preparation

Field management:
1. If possible, start IV fluids BEFORE releasing the crushing object (1L/hr normal saline)
2. Apply tourniquet above crush site before release
3. After release: push fluids aggressively (oral if IV unavailable)
4. Monitor heart rhythm — sudden cardiac arrest is the primary killer
5. Transport to hospital immediately — dialysis may be needed

In mass casualty events (earthquakes, building collapse), crush syndrome is a leading cause of delayed death. Knowledge of this condition saves lives that are otherwise lost to well-intentioned but uninformed rescue.

articles.sourceName: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC)
articles.title: Environmental Emergencies: Heat & Cold
articles.body:
The human body operates in a narrow temperature range. Deviation in either direction can be fatal.

Heat Spectrum:
- Heat cramps: muscle spasms from salt loss. Treatment: rest, water, salt.
- Heat exhaustion: heavy sweating, weakness, nausea. Temp < 104°F. Treatment: cool environment, fluids.
- Heat stroke: altered mental status, temp > 104°F. EMERGENCY. Active cooling required immediately.

Key distinction: heat exhaustion = sweating and aware. Heat stroke = confused/combative. If in doubt, treat as heat stroke.

Cold Spectrum:
- Mild hypothermia (90-95°F): shivering, poor coordination. Treatment: warm environment, dry clothes, warm drinks.
- Moderate hypothermia (82-90°F): shivering stops, confusion, slurred speech. Treatment: active rewarming of core.
- Severe hypothermia (<82°F): unconscious, barely detectable pulse. Handle GENTLY. Active core rewarming.

Critical cold rules:
- 'Nobody is dead until they're warm and dead' — always attempt resuscitation
- Do NOT warm extremities before core (causes afterdrop)
- Do NOT give alcohol (dilates blood vessels, accelerates heat loss)
- Do NOT rub frostbitten skin (causes tissue damage)

articles.title: Airway Management Priorities
articles.body:
Without an open airway, nothing else matters. A patient can survive massive bleeding for minutes, but complete airway obstruction kills in 4-6 minutes.

Common causes of airway obstruction:
- Tongue falling back (unconscious patient lying face-up)
- Blood, vomit, or broken teeth
- Swelling from burns, allergic reaction, or trauma
- Foreign body (food, objects)

Basic airway interventions (no equipment needed):
1. Head-tilt chin-lift: tilt forehead back, lift chin. Opens airway in most unconscious patients.
2. Jaw thrust: if spinal injury suspected. Push jaw forward without moving neck.
3. Recovery position: unconscious but breathing — roll on side. Gravity clears the airway.
4. Finger sweep: ONLY if you can see the obstruction. Never blind sweep.

Advanced considerations:
- Snoring = partial obstruction (tongue). Reposition head.
- Gurgling = fluid in airway. Turn patient on side, suction if available.
- Stridor (high-pitched on inhale) = upper airway swelling. This is an emergency.
- Silence with chest movement = complete obstruction. Immediate intervention needed.

The most important airway skill: recognizing that the airway is compromised. Check airway FIRST in every patient assessment.

articles.title: Systematic Trauma Assessment
articles.body:
In a trauma situation, systematic assessment prevents you from missing life-threatening injuries.

Primary Survey (ABCDE) — find and fix immediate killers:
- A: Airway — is it open? Clear obstructions. Protect cervical spine.
- B: Breathing — is the chest rising equally? Listen for breath sounds. Seal chest wounds.
- C: Circulation — control major bleeding. Check pulse. Note skin color.
- D: Disability — consciousness level (alert, voice, pain, unresponsive). Pupil response.
- E: Exposure — remove clothing to find hidden injuries. Prevent hypothermia.

Secondary Survey — detailed head-to-toe exam (only after ABCDE is stable):
1. Head: skull deformity, blood from ears/nose, pupil asymmetry
2. Neck: tenderness, deformity, trachea midline?
3. Chest: symmetrical? Tender ribs? Crepitus (grinding)?
4. Abdomen: rigid? Tender? Distended?
5. Pelvis: stable when gently pressed? (Only test ONCE)
6. Extremities: deformity, swelling, circulation/sensation/motion check
7. Back: log-roll to check. Tenderness along spine?

Reassess every 5 minutes. Conditions change. What was stable becomes unstable. Document findings and times.

articles.title: Essential Medications for Wilderness First Aid
articles.body:
A basic medication kit can address most common wilderness medical problems.

Pain management:
- Ibuprofen (Advil): anti-inflammatory + pain relief. 400-800mg every 6-8 hours. Best for musculoskeletal pain, headaches.
- Acetaminophen (Tylenol): pain + fever reduction. 500-1000mg every 6 hours. Safe with most other medications.
- Combination: ibuprofen + acetaminophen together is more effective than either alone and safe to combine.

Gastrointestinal:
- Loperamide (Imodium): stops diarrhea. Critical in wilderness to prevent dehydration. 4mg initially, then 2mg after each loose stool.
- Bismuth subsalicylate (Pepto-Bismol): nausea, heartburn, mild diarrhea.
- Oral rehydration salts: for rehydrating after fluid loss.

Allergy/Anaphylaxis:
- Diphenhydramine (Benadryl): antihistamine for allergic reactions. 25-50mg every 6 hours. Causes drowsiness.
- Epinephrine auto-injector: for anaphylaxis ONLY. Prescription required. Carry two.

Wound care:
- Antibiotic ointment (bacitracin/Neosporin): for minor wounds to prevent infection
- Povidone-iodine: wound cleaning and water purification

All medications have expiration dates but most retain 90%+ potency for years past expiration. In a survival situation, expired medication is better than no medication.
